Q: How does emergent misalignment connect to representation convergence?
A: If features cluster by abstract role ("aligned-vs-misaligned mode") rather than by surface task, fine-tuning that pushes one task into the misaligned cluster pulls others with it. Convergent representations make narrow→broad generalization easier.
Q: What's the program-synthesis hypothesis's strongest empirical claim?
A: That mechanistic circuits found in trained models (induction heads, modular-arithmetic algorithms) are not lucky finds but the typical structure of solutions SGD prefers.

Problems

30 min · derivationProblem 1. In the linear regression setting y=Xβ∗+ε with X∈Rn×p i.i.d. Gaussian, derive (heuristically) the location of the double-descent peak and explain why min-norm regression on the right of p=n generalizes.
Solution sketch. Variance of OLS is σ2tr((X⊤X)−1). For p<n: this is σ2p/(n−p) by Wishart trace. Diverges at p=n. For p>n: min-norm solution is X+y; expected squared error to truth involves tr((XX⊤)−1), which is σ2n/(p−n), finite for p>n. As p→∞ with n fixed, variance shrinks. Bias also shrinks because random features span more of β∗'s direction. Net effect: second descent.
15 min · scenarioProblem 2. Sketch an experiment that would distinguish "emergence as Boolean conjunction of smooth sub-skills" from "emergence as a true phase transition in optimization."
Solution sketch. Probe for each putative sub-skill independently at multiple scales (e.g., for arithmetic word problems: tokenization, single-digit addition, multi-step composition). If each sub-skill shows smooth scaling and the joint capability turns on only when all are present, conjunction. If instead the joint capability appears without all sub-skills being independently present — i.e., a previously-absent representation suddenly forms — phase transition. Mechanistic probes (Hessian spectrum, circuit linear-probe accuracy) crossing zero at the transition point distinguishes optimization-side phase changes.

Going deeper (optional)

If you read one thing: Huh, Cheung, Wang, Tegmark — The Platonic Representation Hypothesis (2024) — strongest single statement of the convergence claim with quantitative evidence.

Optional depth: Deep Learning as Program Synthesis — Solomonoff-to-NN bridge; Deep Learning is Not So Mysterious or Different (Wilson 2025) — PAC-Bayes-flavored counterpoint; Emergent Misalignment (Betley et al. 2025) — narrow→broad generalization with safety stakes; Olsson et al., In-context Learning and Induction Heads (Anthropic 2022); Nakkiran et al., Deep Double Descent (2020); Schaeffer–Miranda–Koyejo, Are Emergent Abilities a Mirage? (2023).

Solo assignment

Pick one mystery (your choice: double descent, simplicity bias, Platonic, ICL, or emergence). Write a 2-page memo: (i) the cleanest mathematical statement of the phenomenon; (ii) the two best mechanistic explanations and what experimental signature distinguishes them; (iii) which explanation you find most likely and why. Conclude with one prediction this view makes about frontier models that hasn't yet been tested.

B.3 Singular Learning Theory Singular models, the RLCT λ, Watanabe's free-energy formula, phase transitions, and developmental interpretability.
Prerequisites: B.1 (Bayesian learning, KL divergence); Laplace's method / saddle-point asymptotics; algebraic geometry at the level of desingularization / blow-ups (Hironaka's theorem stated, not proven); Fisher information matrix; eigendecomposition under degeneracy.

Neural networks are singular statistical models — their Fisher information matrix is degenerate at any solution, so classical BIC and Laplace approximations fail. Watanabe's free-energy formula Fn​=nLn​+λlogn−(m−1)loglogn+O(1) replaces the BIC penalty with the real log canonical threshold λ, which can be far smaller than d/2 for singular regions. Posteriors concentrate around low-λ regions, not just low-loss regions; a measurable training-time signal — the local learning coefficient — traces stagewise developmental phase transitions, giving the SLT picture an empirical hook.

Pre-reading questions

  1. What does it mean for a statistical model to be singular, and why are neural networks singular?
  2. State Watanabe's free-energy asymptotic Fn​=nLn​+λlogn−(m−1)loglogn+O(1). What is each term measuring?
  3. Why is λ≤d/2, with equality iff the model is regular at the truth?
  4. How does the RLCT λ define an "effective dimension" different from parameter count?
  5. What is the link from λ to phase transitions and to developmental interpretability (Hoogland, Murfet, Timaeus)?

Core exposition

Why classical statistics fails for neural networks

Classical asymptotic theory (BIC, Laplace approximation) assumes the model is regular: the Fisher information matrix I(θ∗)=E[∇logp(x∣θ∗)∇logp(x∣θ∗)⊤] at the truth θ∗ is positive definite, and the parameter θ∗ is identifiable (unique). Under these conditions, BIC gives Fn​≈nLn​+(d/2)logn where d is the parameter dimension. Neural networks satisfy neither condition. The parameter-function map is many-to-one (permutation symmetries, scale symmetries in ReLU networks: (αw,b/α) gives the same function); the Fisher matrix has whole zero eigendirections, sometimes everywhere.

A statistical model is singular if the Fisher information matrix is degenerate (rank deficient) at the true parameter. Equivalently, the model has a positive-codimension level set {θ:p(⋅∣θ)=p(⋅∣θ∗)} of equivalent parameters. The whole apparatus of asymptotic normality, Wald tests, and the standard BIC penalty fails. Watanabe's Singular Learning Theory (Watanabe 2009) provides the correct replacement.

Watanabe's program. Sumio Watanabe's singular learning theory was developed primarily in the 1990s–2000s, drawing on Hironaka's 1964 resolution-of-singularities theorem (which won the Fields Medal in 1970). The full machinery appeared in his 2009 Cambridge monograph Algebraic Geometry and Statistical Learning Theory, with key precursors in his 2001 paper "Algebraic analysis for nonidentifiable learning machines" (Neural Computation) and the WAIC criterion in 2010. The free-energy asymptotic was largely ignored by the ML mainstream until Murfet, Hoogland, and the Timaeus collaboration (2022–) reframed it as a quantitative theory of deep-learning development. The 50-year gap between the algebraic-geometry tools (resolution of singularities, zeta functions) and their use in deep-learning theory is itself instructive about how isolated mathematical communities can be.

The real log canonical threshold (RLCT) and the free-energy formula

The setup: data Dn​=(x1​,…,xn​) drawn i.i.d. from truth q; parameterized model p(x∣θ); prior φ(θ) on parameter space W. Define the average log-loss at the truth as Ln​=(1/n)∑−logp(xi​∣θ∗) and the KL function K(θ)=Eq​[logq(x)−logp(x∣θ)]. K(θ)≥0 with equality on the truth set W∗={θ:K(θ)=0}.

The Bayesian partition function is

Zn​=∫W​exp(−nK(θ))φ(θ)dθ,

and the free energy is Fn​=−logZn​ (after subtracting nEq​[logq]). In the regular case, Laplace's method around the unique θ∗ gives Fn​≈(d/2)logn−(1/2)logdetI(θ∗)+const, yielding BIC.

In the singular case, the set W∗ may be a high-dimensional analytic variety with singularities. Watanabe's central insight is that for any analytic K, one can apply Hironaka's resolution of singularities: there exists a real analytic manifold U and a proper analytic map g:U→W such that

K(g(u))∣g′(u)∣​=u12k1​​u22k2​​⋯ud2kd​​,=∣u1h1​​⋯udhd​​∣​

in local coordinates. The integral defining Zn​ then becomes a sum of standard analytically tractable integrals.

The result:

Watanabe's free-energy formula. Under analyticity and integrability conditions,

Fn​=nLn​+λlogn−(m−1)loglogn+Op​(1),

where:

  • λ is the real log canonical threshold (RLCT): λ=minα​(hα​+1)/(2kα​) minimized over the resolution charts. Equivalently, λ is the smallest pole of the zeta function ζ(s)=∫K(θ)−sφ(θ)dθ.
  • m is the multiplicity of that pole.
  • Ln​ is the empirical average of −logp(xi​∣θ∗).

The λlogn term is the singular analogue of the BIC penalty. In the regular case λ=d/2 and m=1, recovering BIC. In the singular case, λ≤d/2 strictly, often much less: a low-rank neural network solution can have λ orders of magnitude below the parameter count.

Why λ ≤ d/2

Heuristically: the volume of the ε-neighborhood {θ:K(θ)<ε} scales as vol(ε)∼ελ(log(1/ε))m−1. For a regular model with d-dimensional parameter and full-rank Hessian, this volume scales as εd/2, so λ=d/2. Whenever the Hessian is degenerate, the level set {K<ε} extends along the flat directions, and the volume scales more slowly in ε, i.e., with a smaller exponent. Smaller exponent of ε means smaller λ. Concretely, if the Hessian has rank r<d, then locally λ≤r/2+(d−r)⋅(something involving higher-order vanishing); for a quartic minimum in one direction (i.e., K≈θ4), that direction contributes 1/4 rather than 1/2.

Why λ≤d/2 (volume picture in one line). The λlogn penalty is −logvol({K<1/n}): the log of how much parameter volume sits within 1/n of the truth. A regular minimum is a paraboloid K≈θ⊤Hθ, so {K<ε}≈ ellipsoid of axes ε/λi​​; volume ∝εd/2 gives λ=d/2. If even one direction is flatter than quadratic — say K≈θ2k — that direction's axis is ε1/(2k) wide, contributing 1/(2k)<1/2 to the exponent. Each degenerate direction reduces λ. The whole point of "neural nets are singular" is that they have many such directions.

RLCT λ vs LLC vs effective dimension. Three things often confused. (i) RLCT λ — a global property of the model+prior+truth triple defined via the zeta function; an algebraic invariant. (ii) Local learning coefficient (LLC) λ^(θ) — a finite-n estimator computed from SGLD-based volume scaling around a specific θ; does not require knowing the truth. (iii) Effective dimension in PAC-Bayes / Rademacher contexts — typically based on Hessian-rank or trace-of-H−1 and has a different asymptotic interpretation. Of the three, only λ appears in Watanabe's free-energy formula; LLC is the empirical proxy you can compute on a real model; "effective dimension" is a heuristic from a different lineage and is not the same number, although the qualitative story (singular models have effectively fewer parameters than d) is shared.

Phase transitions and Bayesian model selection

The free-energy formula refines Bayesian model selection: posteriors concentrate around regions of low RLCT, not just regions of low loss. For finite n, the posterior may switch between two singular regions as n grows — a Bayesian phase transition. Schematically: region A has slightly worse fit LA​>LB​ but much lower complexity λA​<λB​. The free-energy difference is

FA​−FB​≈n(LA​−LB​)+(λA​−λB​)logn.

For small n: complexity dominates, region A wins. For large n: fit dominates, region B wins. Setting FA​=FB​ gives the implicit crossover scale

n∗≈LA​−LB​λB​−λA​​logn∗,

logarithmic in scale: small complexity differences Δλ can shift the transition by orders of magnitude in n. This is the SLT picture of developmental phase transitions in neural networks.

Developmental interpretability (Timaeus / Hoogland / Murfet)

The Timaeus program (Hoogland, Murfet, van Wingerden, and collaborators) takes the SLT picture as a generative theory of training stages, not just final-state model selection. The local learning coefficient (LLC) — a finite-n, locally-estimated proxy for λ around a current parameter — is computed via stochastic gradient Langevin dynamics: run SGLD around the current θ, estimate the expected loss increase, and fit to λ^. Empirically, LLC traces over training reveal stagewise development: it rises sharply at moments of new structure formation (e.g., induction-head emergence in transformers) and plateaus during memorization or refinement phases.

This connects to safety: if developmental phase transitions can be detected by LLC inflections, then training-time interventions — pausing, perturbing, or steering — become temporally targeted. Hoogland et al.'s 2024–2025 papers on stagewise development in language models and on the local-learning-coefficient estimator are the canonical references; the OpenReview paper on the LLC scaling to transformer-sized models is the load-bearing scalability result.

Has developmental interp produced predictive results yet? Honestly: not many. The LLC retrodicts known transitions (induction-head formation, grokking, toy-model phase changes) and the program has scaled the estimator to transformer-sized models — these are real accomplishments. What it has not yet done is prospectively predict a previously-unobserved phase transition, identify the precise capability acquired at a transition before mechanistic probes confirm it, or produce a training-time intervention that demonstrably exploited the LLC signal to improve a safety property. The Timaeus 2024–2025 work is the strongest case for "this could become predictive," but treat it as a research program with promising hooks rather than a delivered theory of safety-relevant training dynamics. Compare to physics: post-1925 quantum mechanics was retrodictive for a decade before its first stage-zero predictions (Dirac antiparticle, 1928).

Phenomena in practice: SLT measurements that exist
RLCT computations — concrete model classes (Watanabe; Aoyagi 2024)

Regular models (linear regression with full-rank design, generic exponential families away from the boundary): RLCT λ = d/2, matching the classical BIC penalty. Watanabe's free-energy formula collapses to BIC in this case — there's no SLT-specific story.

Singular models (neural networks, mixture models, latent-variable models, reduced-rank regression): λ ≤ d/2, often strictly less. The strictness is where SLT earns its keep — model complexity, as measured by what the Bayesian posterior actually charges, is smaller than the naive parameter count.

  • Reduced-rank regression is the cleanest worked closed-form example. For predicting Y ∈ ℝᵏ from X ∈ ℝᵈ via low-rank coefficient matrix, the RLCT depends on the rank constraint; rank-1 in 2D yields λ = 1/2, halving effective dimension compared to the regular d/2 = 1.
  • Deep linear networks: Aoyagi (2024) derives closed-form λ depending on architecture and rank/symmetry structure. The number is generally much smaller than d/2, sometimes by an order of magnitude.
  • Hidden-layer ReLU networks: λ is task-and-architecture dependent and currently not closed-form for general cases — this is where empirical LLC estimation enters.
LLC measurement — actually estimating λ via SGLD (Lau, Furman, Murfet, Wei 2023)

Setup. The "Local Learning Coefficient" λ̂ is estimated by running stochastic gradient Langevin dynamics around a current parameter θ*, measuring expected loss increase under the posterior tempered by sample size, and fitting a free-energy-style log-scaling. SGLD config used in the original paper: batch m = n, step size ε = 0.0005, chain length T = 10,000 (smaller ε = 0.0001 and T = 5,000 for tiny 2D experiments).

Estimator validation on monomial potentials ρ(w₁, w₂) = w₁^k₁ w₂^k₂ with theoretically known λ:

  • w₁¹w₂³: theoretical λ = 0.17, SGLD estimate 0.11 ± 0.07
  • w₁¹w₂²: theoretical λ = 0.25, SGLD estimate 0.14 ± 0.12
  • w₁⁰w₂¹ / w₁¹w₂⁰: theoretical λ = 0.50, SGLD estimate 0.45–0.47

Estimator is noisy at very low λ but consistent in trend. Then validated on a realistic test case: 2-hidden-layer ReLU feedforward network with 1.9M parameters on MNIST — empirically computable, not just toy.

Scaling (Furman & Lau 2024). The LLC estimator was extended and validated on deep linear networks up to 100M parameters, matching Aoyagi's theoretical predictions. Figure 5 shows the estimator detecting saddle-to-saddle transitions during training. Open-source as the devinterp library.

SLT applied to transformers — Hoogland et al. 2024

Setup. Language modeling with a ~3M-parameter attention-only transformer (Olsson-style) trained on a Pile subset; separately, in-context linear regression with a ~50K-parameter transformer.

Findings. Discrete developmental stages discovered by LLC trajectory plus function-space probes. The LLC drops during certain stages — interpreted as increased degeneracy / decreased complexity from head interactions. Per-head data-restricted LLCs first increase then decrease, analogous to critical-period dynamics in developmental neuroscience. The method surfaces hidden transitions not visible in the train-loss curve alone — directly addresses the "is there a phase change here?" question that's otherwise eyeballed.

The honest caveat (mirrored in the Developmental Interp callout above): all current results are retrodictive — they identify known transitions or co-occur with mechanistic discoveries. A prospective LLC-only prediction of a previously unknown capability emerging at a specific step has not been demonstrated yet.

Worked example 1: RLCT for a toy reducible quadratic model

Model: p(y∣x,a,b)=N(y;ab⋅x,1), with parameters (a,b)∈R2. Truth: q(y∣x)=N(y;0,1) (so the true regression coefficient is zero). Then K(a,b)=(1/2)Ex​[(abx)2]=ca2b2 for some c>0 (constant absorbed into x's variance).

The truth set is {a=0}∪{b=0} — two lines crossing at the origin. The origin is the singular point of this variety: the parameter θ∗ is not isolated. The Fisher information matrix at the origin is identically zero — completely degenerate.

To compute the RLCT, blow up the origin: substitute a=u,b=uv. Then K=cu4v2, with Jacobian ∣dudv∣=∣u∣dudv. The integral over the new chart gives

∫exp(−ncu4v2)∣u∣dudv.

Compute the RLCT from the exponents: for the monomial u4v2 with measure factor u1, the candidate λ's are (1+1)/4=1/2 (from u) and (0+1)/2=1/2 (from v). They tie, so λ=1/2 with multiplicity m=2.

Compare to the regular case (truth at (a,b)=(a0​,b0​)=0): there K is locally quadratic with full-rank Hessian, λ=d/2=1. So the singular point has half the effective complexity: the posterior strongly prefers the singular region over the regular one when both are available, because the λlogn term is smaller by (1/2)logn bits.

Interpretation: the model has a "use it or lose it" parameter — when the truth doesn't need ab, the Bayes posterior gravitates to the cross of a=0 and b=0, which has lower effective dimension than the generic 2D parameterization.

Worked example 2: RLCT for a 1-D quadratic fit, regular vs singular truth

Model: p(y∣x;a)=N(y;ax2,1). Single parameter a∈R. Then K(a)=(1/2)Ex​[(ax2−a∗x2)2]=c(a−a∗)2 for some c.

Case 1: a∗=0 (regular truth). Standard Gaussian-integral computation gives λ=d/2=1/2, m=1. Free energy Fn​≈nLn​+(1/2)logn, recovering BIC.

Case 2: a∗=0 (singular truth). Now consider the closely related model p(y∣x;a)=N(y;ax2,1) with truth still a∗=0, but extend the parameter by adding a useless dimension: take the model p(y∣x;a,b)=N(y;(a+b2)x2,1). Then K(a,b)=c(a+b2)2. Truth set: a=−b2 — a parabola in (a,b) space, not isolated. Resolve via a=−b2+u: K=cu2, Jacobian 1, and b is a free direction integrated against the prior. RLCT from u: 1/2. The b direction contributes nothing to the loss but contributes a finite volume from the prior. So λ=1/2, lower than the regular d/2=1 we would have gotten from naive counting.

The lesson: redundant parameters that lie tangent to the truth set reduce λ. Neural networks have many such redundancies (permutations, ReLU scale symmetries, dead neurons, low-rank weight subspaces), and the cumulative reduction is what makes λ often dramatically smaller than the parameter count.

  • A statistical model is singular iff its Fisher information is degenerate at the truth; neural networks are singular by construction (permutation, ReLU scale, dead-neuron symmetries).
  • Watanabe's free-energy formula Fn​=nLn​+λlogn−(m−1)loglogn+O(1) replaces BIC; λ≤d/2 with equality iff regular.
  • Posteriors concentrate around low-λ regions, not just low-loss regions — singular models prefer simpler representations, which gives a quantitative theory of why deep nets generalize.
  • Bayesian phase transitions occur at n∗≈(λB​−λA​)/(LA​−LB​)⋅logn∗ — a constructive prediction the developmental-interpretability program tests empirically.
  • The local learning coefficient (LLC) is a tractable finite-n estimate of λ via SGLD-based volume scaling; LLC traces over training reveal stagewise development (Hoogland et al., Timaeus).

Key definitions and results

Singular model
One whose Fisher information matrix is rank-deficient at the truth; equivalently, the truth-set W∗ has positive codimension or is non-smooth.
RLCT (real log canonical threshold) λ
Smallest pole of the zeta function ζ(s)=∫K(θ)−sφ(θ)dθ; equivalently, exponent of ε in vol({K<ε}).
Multiplicity m
Order of the pole of ζ at −λ; controls the (loglogn) correction.
Free-energy formula
Fn​=nLn​+λlogn−(m−1)loglogn+Op​(1) (Watanabe).
Regular case
λ=d/2, m=1; recovers BIC. Strict inequality λ<d/2 diagnoses singularity.
Local learning coefficient (LLC)
Finite-n, locally-estimated proxy for λ around current parameter, computed via SGLD-based volume scaling.
Developmental interpretability
Use of LLC and related geometric measures across training to identify discrete developmental stages and phase transitions.

Retrieval practice

Q: Why does BIC fail for neural networks?
A: BIC assumes a regular model; neural networks are massively singular (permutation, scale, and rank-deficiency symmetries make the Fisher matrix degenerate everywhere). The penalty (d/2)logn is wrong, often by orders of magnitude.
Q: What is the role of Hironaka's theorem in the proof?
A: It guarantees that the singular K(θ) can be reduced to a sum of monomials u12k1​​⋯ud2kd​​ on a desingularized manifold; this reduces the partition-function integral to a standard form whose asymptotics are computed via Mellin/zeta-function analysis.
Q: Why λ≤d/2 always?
A: The Hessian at the truth set has at most rank d; the regular case saturates with full rank. Any further degeneracy (zero eigenvalues, higher-order vanishing) only enlarges the low-loss region and lowers the exponent of ε.
Q: How does the LLC connect to mechanistic interpretability?
A: LLC inflections during training mark the formation of new effective degrees of freedom — i.e., new circuits or representational structure. Pairing LLC traces with circuit-level probes lets you localize when a capability formed, not just that it did.
Q: Bayesian phase transition: what's the crossover scale?
A: Two singular regions with parameters (LA​,λA​) and (LB​,λB​): posterior mass crosses when n(LA​−LB​)≈(λB​−λA​)logn, i.e., n∗≈(Δλ/ΔL)logn∗ — implicit equation logarithmic in scale.
Q: Is SLT a description of Bayesian posteriors only, or also of SGD-trained networks?
A: Strictly, the free-energy formula is Bayesian. The connection to SGD is empirical: trained networks are observed to sit in regions whose LLC tracks meaningful capability formation. The mapping from Bayesian λ to SGD-found minima is conjectural and the subject of ongoing work.
Q: What's a known failure mode of LLC estimation?
A: SGLD-based estimators are sensitive to step size, prior covariance, and chain mixing; LLC values are most informative as relative measures over training, not as absolute λ estimates.

Problems

30 min · derivationProblem 1. Consider the model p(y∣x;a,b)=N(y;(ab)⋅x,1) with prior uniform on [−1,1]2, and truth q(y∣x)=N(y;0,1). Compute the RLCT λ and multiplicity m. State explicitly the free-energy asymptotic and contrast with the BIC prediction of (d/2)logn=logn.
Solution sketch. Following the worked example: K(a,b)=ca2b2. Blow up a=u,b=uv: K=cu4v2 with Jacobian ∣u∣. Two charts (also blow up b=w,a=wz); by symmetry both give the same answer. Candidate λ's: (1+1)/4=1/2 from the u direction; (0+1)/2=1/2 from the v direction. Tie → λ=1/2, m=2. Free energy: Fn​=nLn​+(1/2)logn−loglogn+O(1). BIC predicts (2/2)logn=logn. SLT predicts half that, plus a log-log correction. For n=106: BIC says penalty ≈13.8, SLT says ≈6.9−2.6≈4.3. Factor-of-3 difference, all from the ab=0 singularity.
30 min · derivationProblem 2. A two-layer linear neural network f(x)=W2​W1​x with W1​∈Rh×d, W2​∈R1×h and truth f∗(x)=0. (i) Identify the parameter symmetries that make the model singular. (ii) Describe (qualitatively, no proof) how λ scales with h. (iii) What does this predict for the Bayes-optimal width as a function of n?
Solution sketch. (i) Symmetries: (W1​,W2​)↔(PW1​,W2​P−1) for invertible P∈GL(h) — full GL(h) orbit of equivalent parameterizations. Also rank-deficient: any W1​ with W2​W1​=0 is in the truth set. (ii) The truth set {W2​W1​=0} has codimension d in the parameter space of dimension h(d+1); large h increases the truth-set dimension, so volume of {K<ε} grows with h while K-curvature stays similar. RLCT decreases (slowly) with h, but the prior-volume factor grows. Net effect: Fn​ as a function of h has a minimum at some h∗(n). (iii) h∗(n) grows slowly (logarithmically or polynomially in n); Bayes prefers some overparameterization but not unbounded. This is the SLT picture of "right-sizing" width.

Going deeper (optional)

If you read one thing: Hoogland — The Real Log Canonical Threshold, Explained — the cleanest from-zero exposition; if you want the textbook, Watanabe's Algebraic Geometry and Statistical Learning Theory (2009, Cambridge).

Optional depth: Lau, Furman, Wang, Murfet, Hoogland — The Local Learning Coefficient (2025) — scalable LLC for deep nets; Compressibility Measures Complexity (2025) — SLT/MDL/LLC unification; Timaeus SLT learning hub — curated path; Iliad "Degeneracy in Deep Learning" lecture; Wei et al., "Deep Learning is Singular" (2022); Chen, Hoogland, et al., "Dynamics of Phase Transitions in Toy Models" (2023).

Solo assignment

Choose a small singular toy model (e.g., a 2-layer linear network f=W2​W1​x with rank-bottleneck truth, or the abx regression above). (i) Derive the RLCT analytically using blow-ups in at least one chart. (ii) Implement an LLC estimator via SGLD around an empirically-found minimum and compare to your analytic λ. (iii) In 2 pages, document the discrepancies you find and explain whether they are due to finite-n bias, prior misspecification, or chain non-mixing. Bonus: produce an LLC trace through training and identify any apparent developmental stages.

B.4 Training Dynamics NTK linearization, lazy vs feature-learning regimes, grokking, loss-landscape geometry, edge of stability.
Prerequisites: gradient descent + SGD; kernel methods / RKHS; Hessian spectrum and eigenvalues; B.3 (RLCT, phase transitions); calculus of variations / gradient flow.

Gradient descent on neural networks operates in two qualitatively different regimes: the lazy regime where the network is effectively a kernel machine on KNTK​ and weights barely move from initialization, and the rich / feature-learning regime where representations themselves change. Edge of stability says training self-regulates near ηλmax​(∇2L)≈2 — not an instability but a robust operating point. Grokking (train hits 1 fast, test stays at chance then jumps) and the SLT phase-transition picture from B.3 are most likely the same phenomenon: discrete internal-circuit reorganizations of the learned function.

Pre-reading questions

  1. State the NTK linearization formula. What is the regime of validity (in width, in time, in learning rate)?
  2. How do "lazy" and "rich" (feature-learning) regimes differ operationally and theoretically?
  3. What is grokking? What is the leading mechanistic story, and what does the LLC do during a grokking transition?
  4. State the edge-of-stability claim. What pins the top Hessian eigenvalue to ≈2/η?
  5. Why does batch size act roughly like inverse temperature (or equivalently, inverse learning rate) for SGD's stationary distribution?

Core exposition

The neural tangent kernel and the lazy regime

Consider a network f(x;θ) trained with gradient flow on a squared loss L(θ)=(1/2)∑(f(xi​;θ)−yi​)2. The first-order Taylor expansion around initialization θ0​ is

f(x;θt​)≈f(x;θ0​)+∇θ​f(x;θ0​)⊤(θt​−θ0​).

If this linearization is valid throughout training, the dynamics on outputs become

∂t​f(x;θt​)KNTK​(x,x′)​=−i∑​KNTK​(x,xi​)(f(xi​;θt​)−yi​),=⟨∇θ​f(x;θ0​),∇θ​f(x′;θ0​)⟩.​

That is, the network behaves exactly like kernel regression with the fixed kernel KNTK​. Jacot–Gabriel–Hongler (2018) and Lee et al. (2019) showed that for fully-connected networks at infinite width with a particular ("NTK") parameterization, KNTK​ converges to a deterministic kernel and stays fixed for all training time. At any finite width, kernel deviation is O(1/width​).

The infinite-width limit thus produces a kernel learner, not a feature learner: the representations at the end of training are essentially those at initialization, just reweighted. This is the lazy regime.

Lazy regime vs rich / feature-learning regime. "Lazy" means the linearization f(x;θt​)≈f(x;θ0​)+∇θ​f(x;θ0​)⊤(θt​−θ0​) holds throughout training — equivalent to kernel regression with the fixed NTK. "Rich" / "feature-learning" / "mean-field" means the linearization fails: θt​ moves O(1) and the kernel itself drifts. The regime is set by parameterization scaling (NTK 1/m​ vs muP / mean-field 1/m on the output layer), not by network size alone. A wide network in NTK param is lazy; the same width in mean-field param is rich. So "NTK theory describes wide networks" is wrong as stated — it describes wide networks in NTK parameterization. Frontier-model training uses parameterizations (muP, Llama-style scaled init) chosen specifically to operate in the rich regime, where representations actually change. NTK is a useful solvable toy, not a model of practice.

Lazy vs rich (feature-learning) regimes

Whether a network operates in the lazy or rich regime depends on its parameterization and the scale of θ0​ relative to width. Chizat–Bach (2019) showed that the NTK parameterization (outputs of order 1/n​ at init) yields lazy behavior, while the "mean-field" or "muP" parameterizations (outputs of order 1, gradients scaled) yield rich, feature-learning behavior, where representations evolve substantially during training and the NTK kernel itself changes.

Operational diagnostics:

  • Lazy: ∥θT​−θ0​∥/∥θ0​∥→0 with width; representation similarity (CKA) between init and final is high; features at any layer barely change.
  • Rich: ∥θT​−θ0​∥ is O(1) with width; CKA(init, final) drops; new features form, especially in early layers.

Frontier-model training is firmly in the rich regime — they would not work otherwise. NTK theory is therefore a tractable toy, not a model of practice; but the lazy/rich boundary is the right framework for asking "is this configuration learning features at all?"

Grokking: delayed generalization

Grokking (Power et al. 2022, OpenAI): on a small modular-arithmetic task (a,b)↦(a+b)modp, a small transformer trained with weight decay shows two distinct stages:

  1. Memorization phase: train accuracy reaches 100% quickly (within hundreds of steps); test accuracy stays near chance.
  2. Grokking transition: after a long plateau (often 104–106 steps), test accuracy abruptly jumps to 100%. Weight norm decays during the plateau; the moment of transition coincides with the formation of an algorithmic representation (Fourier features in Z/pZ).

Mechanistically (Nanda et al. 2023): the model learns a generalizing circuit that implements modular addition via discrete Fourier transform — embedding each integer as (cos(2πka/p),sin(2πka/p)) for a few k, then using attention/MLP to compute the multiplication-addition formulas cos(α+β)=cosαcosβ−sinαsinβ. Grokking is the slow consolidation of this circuit alongside decay of the memorization circuit, driven by weight decay's penalty on the (higher-norm) memorization solution. A compact balance describing the transition: training with weight decay γ finds the minimizer of an effective free energy

Fγ​(θ)=L(θ)+2γ​∥θ∥2,

and the algorithmic circuit (lower ∥θ∥2 at matched L) overtakes the memorization circuit when γ-accumulated weight-norm decay outpaces the memorization basin's depth. The empirical "grok time" t∗ scales roughly as 1/γ — directly testable, and verified across Power et al. and Nanda et al. ablations.

Connection to SLT: LLC traces (Hoogland et al. 2024) through grokking show the LLC rising sharply at the grokking transition — the model is acquiring new effective degrees of freedom corresponding to the algorithmic circuit. The picture: a Bayesian phase transition between two singular regions, observable in real training.

Grokking and SLT phase transitions are most likely the same phenomenon. Both have the same fingerprint: two distinct singular regions in parameter space (memorization circuit, algorithmic circuit), an early region with worse generalization but easier access (the memorization basin has higher entropy, higher norm, larger λmem​), a transition driven by an external pressure (weight decay in grokking, Bayesian sample-size growth in SLT — both penalize the higher-complexity region), and an LLC inflection at the transition matching the eq (B.3.3) crossover prediction. The most credible unified picture: SGD with weight decay implements an approximate Bayesian sampling at temperature η/B, the long pre-grokking plateau is the time spent waiting to cross the free-energy barrier between basins, and grokking is just a name we gave to one specific empirical instance of the SLT transition. Caveat: this is a research-program-level claim, not a theorem. Chen-Hoogland 2023 toy models give the strongest direct evidence; full-scale formal proof is open.

Loss landscape geometry and edge of stability

For gradient descent with learning rate η on a strictly-convex quadratic with Hessian eigenvalues λi​, stability requires ηλi​<2 for all i. The classical picture says: choose η<2/λmax​, and GD converges monotonically.

Edge of stability (Cohen, Kaur, Li, Kolter, Talwalkar 2021). Empirically, neural-network training with constant η does not stay below the stability threshold. Instead:

  1. Initially, ηλmax​(H)<2 and the loss decreases monotonically (the "progressive sharpening" phase: λmax​ rises during training).
  2. Once ηλmax​(H)≈2 (the edge of stability), training enters a non-monotonic regime: the loss oscillates around its mean but continues to decrease on average; the top Hessian eigenvalue hovers at exactly ≈2/η.

Mechanism: when GD overshoots in the top-eigenvalue direction, it lands at a point where curvature in that direction is slightly lower (the landscape has negative third derivatives along this direction); the dynamics self-regulate the sharpness. This is a fundamentally non-classical regime — gradient descent acts as an implicit curvature-controller.

Edge of stability is empirical, not a guarantee. The Cohen et al. result is a robust observation across many tasks and architectures; it is not a theorem about general nonconvex losses. There are tasks where progressive sharpening fails to onset (very simple data), regimes where momentum changes the threshold from 2/η to a momentum-corrected constant, and pathological landscapes (sharp ridges, non-smooth losses) where the self-regulation breaks. Treating EoS as a free lunch — "just use big LR, training will sort itself out" — works empirically more often than it should but is not safe to assume. Practical implication: for any new training setup, plot λmax​(H) explicitly; if it does not plateau at ≈2/η within a budget, you are in a non-EoS regime and the dynamics may diverge or get stuck.

Learning-rate and batch-size scaling

For SGD viewed as discretization of a stochastic differential equation, the stationary distribution depends on the ratio η/B where η is learning rate and B is batch size. To first order, doubling η is equivalent to halving B for the temperature of the implicit sampler (Smith–Le, Mandt et al.). Empirically this linear scaling rule holds across many orders of magnitude until very large batch sizes; thereafter the bias from finite step size becomes significant. The practical takeaway: there is one effective temperature, and exploration vs exploitation in SGD is controlled by it.

Loss landscape: mode connectivity and basin geometry

Garipov–Izmailov–Podoprikhin–Vetrov–Wilson (2018): independently trained SGD solutions, despite being far apart in parameter space, are connected by smooth low-loss paths (e.g., low-degree polynomial Bezier curves). Frankle–Carbin (2019) on the "lottery ticket hypothesis": dense networks contain sparse subnetworks that, when re-initialized to their original weights, train to the same accuracy. Both findings suggest a non-convex but heavily-connected basin structure: many flat valleys connected by low passes.

Phenomena in practice: training dynamics that have been measured
Edge of Stability — Cohen et al. 2021

Setup. Architectures: 2-hidden-layer fully-connected (width 200, tanh), CNNs with max- and avg-pool, VGG-19 ±BatchNorm, ResNet with BN, small Transformers on WikiText-2, deep linear networks. Activations swept: tanh, ReLU, ELU, softplus, hardtanh. Datasets: CIFAR-10 (5k subset and full), WikiText-2, 1D synthetic regression, Gaussian binary classification. Learning rates spanning multiple orders of magnitude.

Observed phenomena.

  • Progressive sharpening. Sharpness (top Hessian eigenvalue) rises monotonically from initialization until hitting the 2/η stability cutoff. Example: VGG-19/CIFAR-10 sharpness rises from 6.3 at init to 2227.6 (which is exactly 2/η for η = 0.001 in that experiment).
  • Edge of stability. Once sharpness ≈ 2/η, it hovers just above the cutoff for the rest of training while loss decreases non-monotonically. Gradient descent self-regulates its own curvature.
  • Speed implication. Forcing the classical "stable step size" (η < 2/λₘₐₓ throughout) would take ~16,622 iterations on the example task vs the 329 actually run at edge of stability — a ~50.5× slowdown. EoS isn't an artifact, it's the regime real training operates in.

Mechanism: when GD overshoots in the top-eigenvalue direction, it lands at a point where curvature in that direction is slightly lower (third-derivative effect along the top direction); the dynamics self-regulate sharpness. Not a theorem on general nonconvex losses — see the warning callout above.

Lazy vs Rich regimes — where the choice matters

Jacot, Gabriel, Hongler 2018 (NTK). In the infinite-width limit, the NTK converges to a deterministic limiting kernel and stays constant through training. Training in this regime reduces to kernel gradient descent in function space — analytically tractable but feature-free.

Chizat & Bach 2018 (lazy training). Initialization scaling determines regime: standard deviation 1/√m → NTK/lazy (linearization around init), 1/m → mean-field/rich (genuine feature learning).

Tasks where the regime matters empirically. Parity learning, modular arithmetic (which is what grokking measures), staircase functions — these are tasks where lazy/NTK is provably insufficient or much slower than rich/feature-learning regime. The grokking phase change is essentially a transition from a memorization-dominated configuration into a feature-learning configuration; this is part of why grokking shows up so cleanly on modular arithmetic and not on, say, MNIST. Atanasov-Bordelon-Pehlevan 2022 ("Silent Alignment Effect") identifies the kernel pre-rotation phase that occurs before any visible loss decrease — phase-transition-like dynamics that have alignment-monitoring implications when applied to capability emergence (Wei 2022).

Worked example: NTK on a 1-hidden-layer network

Take f(x;θ)=(1/m​)∑j=1m​aj​σ(wj⊤​x) with σ=ReLU, aj​∈R, wj​∈Rd, all aj​,wj​ i.i.d. standard Gaussian. Width m.

Gradients: ∂f/∂aj​=(1/m​)σ(wj⊤​x); ∂f/∂wj​=(1/m​)aj​σ′(wj⊤​x)x. The NTK is

KNTK​(x,x′)=m1​j∑​[σ(wj⊤​x)σ(wj⊤​x′)+aj2​σ′(wj⊤​x)σ′(wj⊤​x′)⟨x,x′⟩].

As m→∞, by law of large numbers, this converges to

KNTK∞​(x,x′)=Ew​[σ(w⊤x)σ(w⊤x′)]+Ew​[σ′(w⊤x)σ′(w⊤x′)]⟨x,x′⟩.

For ReLU and unit-norm x, the first term is the arccosine kernel of order 1, the second of order 0; both have closed forms (Cho–Saul 2009):

KNTK∞​(x,x′)=2π1​∥x∥∥x′∥(sinθ+(π−θ)cosθ)+2π1​(π−θ)⟨x,x′⟩,

where θ=arccos(⟨x,x′⟩/(∥x∥∥x′∥)).

The crucial fact: this kernel is fixed at initialization and does not change during training (to leading order in 1/m). Therefore training is exactly kernel regression with KNTK∞​, and the test error has the standard kernel-regression generalization properties. Conversely, real networks at practical widths (say m=1000) show kernel drift of order 1/m​≈3% in the lazy regime, and order-1 drift in the rich regime: NTK as a model of finite practical networks holds only under aggressive lazy-parameterization.

  • NTK linearization holds in the lazy regime: training is equivalent to kernel regression with the deterministic infinite-width kernel KNTK∞​.
  • Lazy vs rich is a parameterization choice (NTK vs μP, Chizat-Bach); rich-regime feature learning is where representations actually change, which is the regime that matters for safety-relevant structure.
  • Edge of stability: training self-regulates near ηλmax​(H)≈2 — not an instability but a robust empirical operating point.
  • Grokking (Power et al.) shows phase-transition-like delayed generalization on small algorithmic tasks; the LLC story from B.3 connects this to internal-circuit formation rather than test-set artifact.
  • Practical takeaway: NTK explains why kernel methods work as analogues for trained nets in the lazy regime, but you cannot understand feature-learning capabilities without leaving NTK.

Key definitions and results

NTK
KNTK​(x,x′)=⟨∇θ​f(x;θ0​),∇θ​f(x′;θ0​)⟩; deterministic in infinite-width NTK parameterization, fixed throughout training.
Lazy regime
Linearization around init is valid throughout training; representations don't change; equivalent to kernel regression.
Rich (feature-learning) regime
Parameters move O(1) in norm; representations evolve; NTK itself changes during training.
Grokking
Delayed-generalization phenomenon on small algorithmic tasks: train accuracy saturates fast, test accuracy saturates much later via formation of an algorithmic circuit.
Edge of stability
Empirical regime where ηλmax​(H)≈2; training is non-monotonic in loss but progresses on average; λmax​ self-regulates.
Linear scaling rule
SGD stationary statistics depend on η/B; doubling LR ≈ halving batch size for moderate ranges.
Mode connectivity
Independently trained solutions are connected by smooth low-loss paths in parameter space.

Retrieval practice

Q: In what sense is NTK theory falsified by practice?
A: Frontier models exhibit substantial feature learning — early-layer representations change qualitatively during training. NTK predicts no representation change. Empirical CKA(init, final) drops to near-zero in middle layers of large transformers; this is the rich regime, not the lazy one.
Q: What pins λmax​ to 2/η at the edge of stability?
A: When λmax​ exceeds 2/η, the dynamics in the top-eigendirection are unstable: the iterate oscillates with growing amplitude. Higher-order terms in the loss damp these oscillations only when the local λmax​ drops back to ≈2/η. The system self-regulates.
Q: Why does weight decay help grokking?
A: The memorization solution has higher weight norm than the algorithmic (Fourier-feature) solution. Weight decay shifts the implicit prior away from memorization, eventually making the algorithmic basin the lower-free-energy attractor.
Q: What's the muP (maximal update parameterization) prescription, in one line?
A: Scale initializations and learning rates per-tensor so that activations, gradients, and updates all stay O(1) in width — yielding hyperparameter transfer across model scales.
Q: How does grokking connect to B.3 (SLT)?
A: Grokking is a Bayesian phase transition between two singular regions (memorization circuit, algorithmic circuit). LLC inflection at the transition is the SLT signature; weight-decay sharpens it by penalizing the higher-norm region.
Q: Why doesn't NTK explain why deep learning works on real tasks?
A: Kernel learners with fixed kernels are limited by the inductive bias of the kernel. NTK kernels are smooth and isotropic; many real tasks (e.g., image classification, language modeling) require hierarchical features that only emerge with feature-learning dynamics, not under a fixed kernel.

Problems

30 min · derivationProblem 1. A 2-layer ReLU network with one input is initialized in NTK parameterization. Show that under gradient flow on a regression task, ∥θt​−θ0​∥∞​=O(1/m​) per parameter, and conclude that the linearization error in f is O(1/m​).
Solution sketch. In NTK param, f=(1/m​)∑j​aj​σ(wj⊤​x); the loss L has ∂L/∂aj​=(1/m​)(residual)⋅σ(wj⊤​x). Each per-parameter gradient is O(1/m​); integrating gradient flow over O(1) time gives ∣Δaj​∣=O(1/m​). The Taylor remainder in f is sum of m terms each O(1/m) (product of two O(1/m​) changes plus the prefactor), totaling O(1/m​). Hence the NTK approximation is valid to that order.
15 min · scenarioProblem 2. Sketch a minimal experiment to reproduce edge-of-stability behavior in a 2-layer MLP on MNIST. Specify (i) the architecture, (ii) the loss + learning rate, (iii) what to plot during training, (iv) the expected signature.
Solution sketch. (i) 2-layer MLP, widths 512, ReLU, MSE on a regression-of-labels target (or cross-entropy works too). (ii) Vanilla GD (full batch), η set so initial ηλmax​ is well below 2 (e.g., η=0.01). (iii) Log loss, top Hessian eigenvalue via Lanczos every k steps. (iv) Expected: λmax​ rises ("progressive sharpening") until ηλmax​≈2; thereafter λmax​ plateaus at ≈2/η, loss oscillates but decreases on average. Vary η and confirm the plateau scales as 2/η.

Going deeper (optional)

If you read one thing: Cohen, Kaur, Li, Kolter, Talwalkar — Gradient Descent on Neural Networks Typically Occurs at the Edge of Stability (2021) — the empirical regime that overturned the classical stability story.

Optional depth: Saxe, McClelland, Ganguli — Deep Linear Dynamics — solvable toy with stagewise feature emergence; Jacot–Gabriel–Hongler 2018 (the NTK paper); Chizat–Bach 2019 on lazy vs rich; Power et al. 2022 (grokking original); Nanda et al. 2023 on grokking circuits; Complexity Dynamics of Grokking (2025); Training Dynamics Underlying LM Scaling Laws (2025); muP (Yang–Hu 2022) for the right parameterization for hyperparameter transfer.

Solo assignment

Train a small transformer (≤1M params) on modular addition mod p (p prime, e.g., 113). Reproduce grokking. Produce three plots over training: (i) train and test accuracy; (ii) weight norm and weight-decay-adjusted "effective complexity"; (iii) either an LLC estimate (via SGLD), or a Fourier-decomposition probe of the embedding layer to detect circuit formation. In a 2-page writeup, identify the grokking transition and explain — referencing both SLT (B.3) and the lazy/rich framework — what is happening at that moment.

B.5 Data Attribution Influence functions, TracIn, Datamodels, and provenance of model behaviors for safety.
Prerequisites: implicit function theorem; Newton's method and Hessian inverse; B.3 (singularity of H at trained NN solutions); B.4 (training-trajectory dependence); leave-one-out cross-validation intuition.

Influence functions estimate Iup​(z,ztest​)=−∇L(ztest​)⊤H^−1∇L(z) — a first-order causal counterfactual for "what if this training point's weight had been infinitesimally perturbed." For NN safety, this lets you attribute model behaviors to their training-data provenance. Three caveats bite: the Hessian is rank-deficient at NN optima (B.3 singularity, so the textbook formula needs Tikhonov / EK-FAC regularization), influence is path-dependent (TracIn improves on this), and high-influence training points are not the same as semantically-responsible ones — the validity of an attribution-based safety case depends on whether ablating identified examples actually removes the behavior.

Pre-reading questions

  1. State the influence-function formula for upweighting a training example. Where does the Hessian inverse come from?
  2. Why do classical influence functions fail in modern neural networks, and what fixes have been proposed?
  3. What is the safety relevance of training-data attribution? Concretely, what would you do with perfect TDA against emergent misalignment?
  4. Compare TracIn, influence functions, and Datamodels on what they assume about training.
  5. How does data attribution interact with the singular structure of NN solutions (B.3)?

Core exposition

The central counterfactual question

Training data attribution (TDA) asks: for a model θ^ trained on dataset D={z1​,…,zn​}, and for a test point ztest​, what would the model's loss at ztest​ be if training point zi​ had been (i) absent, (ii) reweighted by ε, or (iii) replaced by zi′​? The exact answer requires retraining; the goal of TDA is to produce a useful approximation without retraining.

First-order influence vs causal counterfactuals. An influence function answers "∂L(z∗​)/∂ε at ε=0" — the linearization of the LOO operator. It is a first-order Taylor approximation, accurate only when the perturbation ε is small and the implicit-function expansion is valid. A causal counterfactual is "what would L(z∗​) actually be if z were removed and the model retrained from the same initialization?" — a finite, nonlinear change. The two coincide only to leading order. Datamodels approximate the causal counterfactual via brute-force retraining sampling; IF approximates the first-order coefficient via Hessian inversion. Frame your safety case around which one you actually need: "this training example caused 80% of the behavior" is a causal claim and Datamodels is the right tool; "this example moves the loss by ε" is a first-order claim and IF (with reservations) does it cheaply.

Influence functions (Cook 1977; Koh–Liang 2017)

Consider the empirical risk minimizer θ^(ε)=argminθ​(1/n)∑i​L(zi​,θ)+εL(z,θ). The first-order Taylor expansion in ε at ε=0, derived via the implicit function theorem applied to the first-order optimality condition, gives

dεdθ^​​ε=0​=−Hθ^−1​∇θ​L(z,θ^),

where Hθ^​=(1/n)∑i​∇θ2​L(zi​,θ^) is the empirical Hessian. The induced change in test loss is

Iup​(z,ztest​)​=dεdL(ztest​,θ^(ε))​​ε=0​=∇θ​L(ztest​,θ^)⊤⋅dεdθ^​=−∇θ​L(ztest​,θ^)⊤Hθ^−1​∇θ​L(z,θ^).​

This is the influence function. Interpretation: a small upweighting of z moves θ^ in direction −H−1∇L(z,θ^) (a Newton step); the dot product with ∇L(ztest​,θ^) gives the first-order change in test loss. The leave-one-out (LOO) prediction is approximately −(1/n)Iup​(z,ztest​).

For removal of an entire training point, integrating to second order or solving the perturbation exactly leads to "Newton-step" attribution: θ^−i​≈θ^−(1/(n−1))H−1∇L(zi​,θ^).

Why classical IF fails on deep neural networks

The formula relies on three assumptions that NN training violates:

  1. Strict convexity: Hθ^​ must be invertible. For deep nets, H is rank-deficient (millions of zero eigenvalues; recall B.3: singular models). Inverting requires damping H+λI, but the choice of λ is essentially arbitrary and changes results by orders of magnitude.
  2. Convergence to a critical point: the implicit-function step assumes ∇L(θ^)=0. SGD-trained networks are not at critical points — they sit on the edge of stability, with non-trivial gradient noise.
  3. Path-independence: the formula is a property of the loss function at θ^, not the training trajectory. But neural networks remember initialization and order of data (B.4). Two ERMs reached by different paths give different influences.

Empirically (Basu–Pope–Feizi 2021; Bae et al. 2022), classical influence functions correlate weakly with true LOO retraining for deep networks. Improvements include damping with informed λ, EK-FAC Hessian approximations (Grosse–Bae et al. 2023), explicit unrolling, and Bayesian-posterior-based methods (BIF, 2025).

The Hessian is rank-deficient at NN optima — naive IF does not strictly apply. By B.3, a trained neural network sits in a singular region: H has zero eigendirections (permutation symmetries, ReLU scale symmetries, dead-neuron directions). The formula Iup​=−∇L(z∗​)⊤H−1∇L(z) is literally undefined: H−1 does not exist on the kernel directions. Practical implementations paper over this by Tikhonov damping (H+λI)−1, but the λ is a free hyperparameter that changes attribution scores by orders of magnitude. EK-FAC is a structured approximation that handles some but not all degeneracy. The honest statement: "influence function for deep networks" is shorthand for "a damped pseudo-inverse approximation whose match to true LOO retraining is empirical and modest (correlation 0.3–0.5)." Calling its output a "first-order counterfactual" overstates the rigor. BIF reframes the problem in posterior-covariance terms specifically to bypass this issue.

TracIn (Pruthi–Liu–Kale–Sundararajan 2020)

TracIn skips the Hessian by integrating along the training trajectory. For checkpoints θt1​​,…,θtK​​ at intermediate training steps, define

TracIn(z,ztest​)=k∑​ηk​⟨∇L(z,θtk​​),∇L(ztest​,θtk​​)⟩.

This is the cumulative first-order change in L(ztest​,⋅) as z was visited along training (assuming SGD with one example per step). Advantages: no Hessian inversion; trajectory-aware. Disadvantages: requires saved checkpoints; assumes ideal SGD; doesn't extrapolate to counterfactual training (only attributes actual training events).

What TracIn captures that IF misses: path dependence. IF is a property of the loss function evaluated at the final parameter θ^. It is invariant under training trajectory: any two ERMs landing at θ^ get the same attribution. But networks remember their path — initialization, data order, learning-rate schedule. If z was seen early (when gradients were large) it had outsized effect; if late, smaller. TracIn's ∑k​ηk​⟨∇L(z,θtk​​),∇L(z∗​,θtk​​)⟩ literally integrates the gradient interaction along the realized trajectory, so a single z seen at one specific step contributes only at that step, weighted by the LR at that step. The cost is the converse limitation: TracIn cannot answer counterfactual "what if z had been seen twice" because there is no second θtk​​ to read off — it attributes actual training events, not hypothetical ones. IF and TracIn are duals: counterfactual but trajectory-blind vs. trajectory-honest but actual-only.

Datamodels (Ilyas–Park–Engstrom–Leclerc–Madry 2022)

Datamodels regress the model output (e.g., margin on a test point) on the binary inclusion vector 1S​∈{0,1}n of training subsets S. Train many models on random subsets, fit a linear function

margin(ztest​;S)≈τ0​+i∑​τi​1i∈S​.

The τi​ are the attribution scores: how much does including training example i shift the margin? Pros: agnostic to training algorithm; gives near-causal counterfactuals on subsets. Cons: requires many training runs (thousands), so only feasible for small models.

Safety relevance: provenance of misalignment

Concrete use cases:

  • Emergent misalignment provenance (Betley et al. 2025). A model fine-tuned on insecure-code completions generalizes to broadly misaligned behavior on unrelated prompts. TDA against this fine-tune asks: which fine-tuning examples most influenced the deceptive answers? Answers help diagnose why the narrow→broad jump occurs (e.g., specific examples that activate the broader "misaligned mode" latent).
  • Memorization auditing. When a model regurgitates a copyrighted or private string, TDA identifies the source training examples. This is the well-validated regime (small effects, large-magnitude attributions).
  • Capability-source tracing. When a new capability emerges with scale, TDA on different training-data slices distinguishes "knowledge from a specific subset" from "compositional capability across many subsets."
  • Pre-training data filtering. Identify training data that contributes disproportionately to undesired behaviors (toxicity, deception in CoT). The Anthropic 2024 work on influence-function scaling and the Grosse et al. EK-FAC implementation are the canonical references.

The honest assessment of TDA's safety value: it works well for memorization (a strong signal stands out), well-enough for fine-tuning effects (small datasets, identifiable contributions), and is currently weak for pre-training compositional behaviors, where the relevant "cause" of a capability is diffuse across millions of examples and no individual example has large influence.

Connection to B.3 singular structure

At singular minima, H is rank-deficient and the IF formula's H−1 is undefined on the kernel directions. The Bayesian Influence Function (BIF; 2025) treats this as: the relevant first-order object is the change in the posterior, not the point estimate, under a small data perturbation. The result has the form

BIF(z,ztest​)≈Covp(θ∣D)​[L(z,θ),L(ztest​,θ)]

— a posterior covariance, computable via SGLD without inverting the Hessian. Equivalently, it generalizes IF to the local-learning-coefficient geometry around θ^. This is the singular-aware analogue of Koh–Liang.

Phenomena in practice — what influence functions actually surface
Influence functions on frontier LLMs — Grosse et al. 2023 (Anthropic)

Setup. Influence-function computations on Anthropic models at 810M, 6.4B, 22B, and 52B parameters. Method: EK-FAC (eigenvalue-corrected Kronecker-factored approximate curvature) for the inverse Hessian-vector product, computed only for MLP layers (the bulk of params). Efficiency: TF-IDF filtering of candidate training docs, query batching, low-rank decomposition of test gradients.

The headline empirical findings.

  • Anti-shutdown query ("Are you ready to be shut down?"). 810M model's top influences are training docs sharing surface tokens like "continue existing" but topically irrelevant. 52B model's top influences are docs about survival instinct and humanlike emotions in AIs — semantically aligned. Mechanism shifts from lexical to conceptual with scale.
  • Math chain-of-thought query. 810M's top influences share only the word "clip" with the test query; 52B's top influences are docs explaining reasoning for a similar problem. Same pattern.
  • Cross-lingual influence: translating the anti-shutdown query to Korean or Turkish, then looking for English-language training influences — cross-lingual influence "considerably stronger with model size." Smaller models: near-zero cross-lingual influence. Larger: clear cross-lingual matches.
  • Order-flipping limitation. Influences "decay to near-zero when the order of key phrases is flipped" — even the 52B partly relies on token-level phrase ordering, not pure semantic abstraction.
  • Layer pattern. Factual completions concentrate influence in upper layers; abstract reasoning spreads across middle layers.

For alignment, the implication is concrete: at frontier scale, influence functions identify conceptually-related training documents for queries like "are you ready to be shut down?" — a usable lever for provenance of misalignment-relevant behaviors. The method is expensive but tractable on real models.

TRAK — Park et al. 2023 (ICML)

What it does. Tracing with the Randomly-projected After Kernel. Random projections of model gradients form a tractable kernel for attribution. Requires training only a small ensemble of models (a handful) rather than the thousands required by datamodels.

Numbers (Linear Datamodeling Score, Spearman rank correlation). CIFAR-2: LDS 0.203; CIFAR-10: LDS 0.271; QNLI: LDS 0.416; ImageNet: LDS 0.188. Speed: on CIFAR-10 and QNLI, TRAK matches datamodels' efficacy at 100–1000× speedup. BERT/QNLI: TRAK runs in 17 hours on a single A100, beating datamodels that took 125 GPU-days. As fast as TracIn but much more predictive.

Applied to image classifiers, TRAK identifies near-duplicate or class-prototypical training images as top influencers, but with much higher signal-to-noise than TracIn for the same compute budget. Practical implication: data attribution is now affordable for medium-scale classifiers and feasible (at compute cost) for frontier LLMs via the Grosse pipeline.

Worked example: influence function on logistic regression (step-by-step derivation)

Binary logistic regression: L(z,θ)=log(1+exp(−y⋅θ⊤x)) for z=(x,y) with y∈{−1,+1}. We derive the influence function Iup​(z,ztest​) from scratch and check its scaling.

  1. ∇θ​L(z,θ)=−σ(−yθ⊤x)⋅yx,σ(u)=1+e−u1​.

    Direct chain-rule differentiation of log(1+e−yθ⊤x). The factor σ(−yθ⊤x) is the model's predicted probability of the wrong label — large when the model is uncertain or wrong.

  2. ∇θ2​L(z,θ)=σ(θ⊤x)(1−σ(θ⊤x))xx⊤.

    Second derivative drops the y (squared). Per-example Hessian is rank 1 in x; the scalar prefactor p(1−p) is the Bernoulli variance — maximal at p=1/2 (the decision boundary), zero at confident predictions.

  3. Hθ^​=n1​i=1∑n​pi​(1−pi​)xi​xi⊤​,pi​=σ(θ^⊤xi​).

    Empirical Hessian at the trained θ^. Full-rank whenever {xi​:pi​(1−pi​)>0} span Rd — i.e., enough non-saturated examples exist. Crucially full-rank because the model is convex and the optimum is unique; this is exactly what fails for neural nets (B.3 singularity).

  4. dεdθ^​​ε=0​=−Hθ^−1​∇θ​L(z,θ^)=σ(−yθ^⊤x)yHθ^−1​x.

    Implicit-function theorem applied to the perturbed-ERM optimality condition ∇R^(θ^(ε))+ε∇L(z,θ^(ε))=0. The Newton step H−1∇L(z,θ^) is the direction θ^ moves under upweighting z by ε.

  5. Iup​(z,ztest​)=∇θ​L(ztest​,θ^)⊤⋅dεdθ^​​ε=0​.

    Chain rule on the induced change in test loss. This is the general influence-function formula before specializing to logistic.

  6. Iup​(z,ztest​)​=[−σ(−ytest​θ^⊤xtest​)ytest​xtest​]⊤[σ(−yθ^⊤x)yHθ^−1​x]=−σ(−ytest​θ^⊤xtest​)σ(−yθ^⊤x)⋅yytest​⋅xtest⊤​Hθ^−1​x.​

    Plug step 1 (gradient) and step 4 (Newton step) into step 5. The result factors as a product of three interpretable terms: uncertainty on z, uncertainty on z∗​, and a Mahalanobis-like alignment xtest⊤​H−1x — plus the label-agreement sign yytest​.

  7. Interpretation. Iup​ is negative (training on z reduces test loss) iff (i) the model is uncertain on both z and z∗​ (otherwise one of the σ(−y⋅) factors is near 0), (ii) labels agree, yytest​>0, and (iii) inputs are aligned in the Mahalanobis sense via H−1. Sign-flipped on label disagreement: training on a same-input opposite-label point hurts.

    Each factor has a clear role; this is the "best case" for IF and the most-cited canonical derivation.

  8. Validation against true LOO. For well-conditioned logistic regression with n≫d, Pearson correlation of Iup​ predictions against actual LOO retraining loss changes is >0.95. For neural networks (with EK-FAC, careful damping), the same comparison gives correlation 0.3–0.5 — orders-of-magnitude worse, directly reflecting the breakdown of assumptions 1–3 in the main text (strict convexity, critical-point convergence, path-independence).

    The empirical gap between logistic and NN is the punchline: the formula is exact at one extreme and a loose proxy at the other, with no quantitative theory yet for the middle.

  • Influence function Iup​(z,z∗​)=−∇L(z∗​)⊤H^−1∇L(z) assumes a strictly convex loss and θ^ being a true critical point — both fail for NNs.
  • For NNs, H^ is rank-deficient (B.3 singularity); naive IF requires Tikhonov regularization or EK-FAC, and even then matches true leave-one-out only with 0.3–0.5 correlation.
  • Path dependence breaks the first-order story; TracIn integrates gradient alignment along the optimization trajectory, partially fixing this.
  • Datamodels are near-causal but require ~thousands of training runs; BIF (Bayesian Influence Function) is the singular-aware alternative computed via SGLD sampling.
  • Safety case: TDA underwrites provenance claims (which training data caused this behavior?). The narrow-fine-tuning → broad-misalignment phenomenon from B.2 is the canonical use case.

Key definitions and results

Influence function Iup​
Iup​(z,ztest​)=−∇L(ztest​,θ^)⊤Hθ^−1​∇L(z,θ^); first-order change in test loss per unit upweighting of training point.
Leave-one-out approximation
Estimated change from removing zi​: ≈(1/n)Iup​(zi​,ztest​); valid to first order in 1/n.
EK-FAC
Eigenvalue-corrected Kronecker-factored approximate curvature; Hessian approximation used in scalable influence-function implementations.
TracIn
Trajectory-based attribution: ∑k​ηk​⟨∇L(z,θtk​​),∇L(ztest​,θtk​​)⟩; no Hessian needed.
Datamodels
Linear regression of model output on subset-inclusion indicators; ∼ thousands of training runs; near-causal.
Bayesian Influence Function (BIF)
Posterior-covariance attribution; bypasses Hessian inversion via SGLD sampling; singular-aware.
Memorization regime
TDA's most reliable regime: a small number of training examples drive a specific behavior, and influence scores stand out from the background.
Compositional regime
TDA's hardest regime: the behavior is the aggregate of millions of small contributions; no single example has identifiable influence.

Retrieval practice

Q: Derive the IF formula in one line from the implicit function theorem.
A: From ∇θ​R^(θ^(ε),ε)=0 where R^=(1/n)∑L(zi​,θ)+εL(z,θ), differentiate in ε: H⋅dθ^/dε+∇L(z,θ^)=0, so dθ^/dε=−H−1∇L(z,θ^). Chain rule on test loss gives the IF formula.
Q: Why is IF damping non-innocent?
A: Using (H+λI)−1 regularizes the inversion but biases attribution toward high-gradient examples; the choice of λ changes which training points appear "most influential" by orders of magnitude.
Q: What does TracIn fail to capture that IF formally captures?
A: TracIn measures the actual gradient interaction along the realized trajectory; it does not extrapolate to counterfactuals "what if z had been seen twice / never." IF formally answers counterfactual questions (under its assumptions).
Q: How would you use TDA to validate or falsify a hypothesis about emergent misalignment?
A: Identify a held-out misaligned behavior (e.g., specific deceptive response). Run TDA against the fine-tuning set; predict which training examples most strongly induce the behavior. Re-fine-tune without those examples; check whether the behavior diminishes proportionally to predicted influence. If yes, the TDA story is causal; if not, the behavior is compositionally produced.
Q: Why does TDA degrade as the model gets larger?
A: Hessian inversion gets worse (more singularity), capabilities are more compositional (fewer high-influence individual examples), and the cost of approximate IF scales poorly. BIF and unrolling-based methods are the current frontier.
Q: When does Datamodels beat IF?
A: Small models, expensive but tractable many-retraining setups, and when subset-level (rather than single-example) attribution is enough. Datamodels are near-causal; IF is an approximation that gets worse with deeper networks.

Problems

30 min · derivationProblem 1. Verify the IF formula on ridge regression. Setup: L(z,θ)=(1/2)(y−θ⊤x)2+(γ/2)∥θ∥2; θ^=(X⊤X+γI)−1X⊤y. Compute Iup​((xi​,yi​),(xtest​,ytest​)) from the formula, and verify it agrees with the exact analytic LOO formula θ^−i​=θ^−(1/(1−hii​))(X⊤X+γI)−1xi​(yi​−xi⊤​θ^) (Sherman–Morrison), to first order in 1/n.
Solution sketch. ∇L(z,θ^)=−(y−θ^⊤x)x+γθ^ (the γθ^ term is from the regularizer; cancels in the LOO comparison since it's the same on both sides). Hessian H=(1/n)X⊤X+γI. Plug into IF formula:
Iup​=−rtest​xtest⊤​(X⊤X+nγI)−1(−ri​xi​/n) where ri​=yi​−xi⊤​θ^ is the residual. So per-LOO change: (1/n)Iup​=(rtest​ri​/n2)xtest⊤​H−1xi​/(1+something) — first-order match to the Sherman–Morrison exact form. The discrepancy is O(1/n2), controlled by hii​ leverage.
1–2 hr · openProblem 2. Design a scenario-analysis protocol: you have a fine-tuned LLM showing a specific misaligned behavior (e.g., refusing on a wrongly-classified safe prompt). Outline a TDA-based investigation: (i) which method (IF/TracIn/Datamodels/BIF) you would use and why; (ii) what intermediate diagnostic checks to run; (iii) what causal-validation experiment closes the loop.
Solution sketch. (i) Fine-tuning means small dataset (e.g., 10k examples) and small parameter change → TracIn or BIF; BIF if posterior tools are available, TracIn if just checkpoints. IF with EK-FAC if neither. Datamodels infeasible at LLM scale. (ii) Diagnostic: rank training examples by attribution; check whether the top-K are semantically related to the misaligned behavior (face-validity); compute the influence-score distribution and look for heavy tails (a few examples driving the behavior) vs flat distribution (compositional). (iii) Causal close: remove the top-K examples; re-finetune; measure whether the behavior diminishes in proportion to predicted influence. If proportional → causal; if no change → spurious; if larger change → other examples are correlates of a deeper feature.

Going deeper (optional)

If you read one thing: Koh, Liang — Understanding Black-box Predictions via Influence Functions (2017) — the formal foundation; pair with Grosse et al. (Anthropic 2023) for the LLM-scale practicalities.

Optional depth: Bayesian Influence Functions (2025) — singular-aware attribution; MAGIC (2025) — unrolled differentiation for nonconvex; Influence Dynamics and Stagewise Attribution (2025) — training-time evolution of attribution; Emergent Misalignment (Betley et al. 2025) — the canonical case study for safety-relevant TDA; Pruthi et al., TracIn (2020); Ilyas et al., Datamodels (2022); Anthropic 2024 on influence functions at scale.

Solo assignment

Pick a small but real model+dataset (e.g., a fine-tuned BERT-small on a sentiment task, or a small LM on a narrow instruction-tuning set). (i) Implement an influence-function attribution (with EK-FAC or simple damping). (ii) Run exact LOO retraining for a sample of training points and compare predicted vs measured influence; plot the scatter. (iii) Identify one specific test-time behavior (correct or incorrect) and report which training examples have highest predicted influence on it. In 2 pages: assess where the IF approximation succeeds, where it breaks, and what you would change methodologically to deploy TDA on a frontier model.

C. Abstractions, Representations, and Interpretability

Four units that move from running experiments competently, to reverse-engineering trained networks, to formal theories of what a "good" representation even is. The shift across units is from artifact (the network) to substrate (the process being modeled) to abstraction (what observers must share to coordinate).

C.1 Intro to ML Engineering Experimental hygiene for interp/alignment research: minimal repros, sweep economics, and the bug-vs-result decision.
Prerequisites: PyTorch fluency, basic transformer architecture (attention, residual stream, MLP), familiarity with cross-entropy loss and Adam, and exposure to at least one published scaling-law plot (Hoffmann et al. 2022 "Chinchilla" is enough).

Empirical ML is a discipline of separating signal from artifact: most "results" you see in your own loss curves are bugs, leaks, or noise, and the cost of mistaking one for the other is months. The minimal-repro discipline, seed-variance budgeting (σseed2​ vs σhp2​), and a fixed bug-vs-result decision tree are the three habits that distinguish researchers who ship from those who chase ghosts. Adopt them before learning any specific interpretability technique — they make every downstream method actually work.

Pre-reading questions

  1. When you see a loss spike at step 14,000 of a 100k run, what is your decision tree for "kill it" vs "let it ride"?
  2. You have 8 H100-hours. Do you do one careful run or eight cheap ones? On what does the answer depend?
  3. What is the smallest possible reproduction of a transformer training bug, and why does shrinking it matter?
  4. What distinguishes a "bug that looks like a result" from a "result that looks like a bug"?
  5. Why is "scaling law deviation" a cleaner signal than absolute loss when comparing two training recipes?

Core exposition

The minimal repro discipline

The single highest-leverage habit in empirical ML is producing a minimal reproduction of every phenomenon worth understanding. A minimal repro is a self-contained script that exhibits the phenomenon with the smallest possible model, dataset, and dependency surface — ideally one file, one GPU, under five minutes. It serves three orthogonal purposes. First, it forces you to verify the phenomenon exists in isolation, separating it from confounds in your training stack (mixed precision quirks, dataloader race conditions, learning-rate-schedule edge cases). Second, it makes the phenomenon shareable; a colleague can run it and debate it without provisioning a cluster. Third, and most importantly, it forces you to name the phenomenon precisely. If you cannot shrink "the model gets worse at math after RLHF" to a 50-line script with a measurable number, you do not yet have a claim — you have an impression.

Practical heuristics: shrink width before depth (a 2-layer, d=128 transformer is far cheaper to debug than a 12-layer d=64); replace your real dataset with a synthetic one whose ground truth you control; remove every component (dropout, weight decay, learning rate warmup) that is not load-bearing for the phenomenon, one at a time; and only re-add components once the phenomenon is stable without them.

Why does the minimal-repro discipline matter more than fancier infrastructure? Because most "phenomena" disappear the moment you can no longer point at the exact lines of code that produce them. Distributed training stacks, wandb dashboards, and slurm orchestrators amplify whatever signal is real — and whatever artifact is real too. A 50-line minimal repro is the only place where signal-to-artifact ratio is yours to control end to end; everything above that layer adds entropy faster than it adds capability. The single best predictor of "is this researcher going to ship a result" is "do they have one minimal repro per claim, and can they hand it to me right now."

The single most common naive-ablation failure: reporting one seed per config and treating the resulting ordering as a "trend." In small-model interp work σseed2​ routinely dominates σhp2​ — your sweep is noise. If you haven't measured the seed-variance floor for your metric, you don't yet have evidence about hyperparameters.

Sweep-vs-replicate budget allocation

Given a fixed compute budget B, the canonical decision is whether to sweep a hyperparameter or to replicate a configuration with different seeds. The framing that actually predicts your regret: estimate the variance of your metric across seeds at fixed config (σseed2​) and the variance of the metric across the hyperparameter range you'd sweep (σhp2​). The decision criterion:

sweep if σhp2​≫σseed2​;replicate if σhp2​≲σseed2​.

If they are comparable, you cannot tell apart a hyperparameter effect from seed noise, and you must replicate. The common failure mode is sweeping with one seed per config, observing a "trend," and reporting it — when in fact the per-seed noise floor is larger than the sweep signal. As a default for small-model interp work: at least 3 seeds per config before any claim, and a planned seed budget before you start the sweep. For frontier-scale runs replication is rarely affordable, which is exactly why frontier results are so often retracted in spirit by the next paper.

Debugging: signal vs bug

Failure modes during training cluster into a few archetypes, each with a diagnostic fingerprint:

  • NaN cascade. Loss becomes NaN at step t; gradient norm spikes ≥2 orders of magnitude in the steps immediately prior. Almost always numerical — overflow in fp16 softmax, division by a zero variance in a normalization, or log of a clipped-to-zero probability. Diagnostic: add NaN-guard hooks on every forward output and bisect to the first NaN-producing layer.
  • Loss spike (recovers). Loss jumps then returns to trend over hundreds of steps. Often genuine — Adam's second-moment estimator getting "stuck" after a rare-token batch, or a rare data shard. Diagnostic: log per-batch loss and check whether the spike correlates with a specific data shard. If yes, it is a data artifact; if no, it is an optimizer state event.
  • Loss spike (no recovery). Loss jumps and trains a new, higher trajectory. This is the dangerous one. Usually optimizer divergence; the second-moment estimator has been corrupted and Adam can never re-stabilize. The Palm/OPT/BLOOM training logs all show this; the fix is gradient clipping plus optimizer-state checkpointing every few hundred steps so you can roll back.
  • Scaling-law deviation. Your run sits above (worse than) the predicted loss given its FLOPs. If the gap is constant in log-loss, it is almost certainly a recipe issue (LR, batch size, tokenizer). If the gap grows, it is architectural — you have a bottleneck that compounds with scale (most often: insufficient model width relative to data, per Chinchilla).
Is it a bug or a result?

When you see something surprising, the decision tree is: (1) Can a colleague reproduce your surprising number from your minimal repro? If no, it is a bug in your harness. (2) Does the effect survive a sign-flipped control (e.g., randomize the intervention and check the effect goes to zero)? If no, it is statistical noise or a data-pipeline leak. (3) Does the effect scale predictably with model size, data, or steps? If completely flat, suspect a constant offset in your measurement (e.g., a logging bug that always adds the same value). Only after these three pass should you write "we observe that…" The discipline of writing the null-result version of your own finding first ("if this were a bug, the bug would have to be X") is the single best calibration tool I know of.

Why this decision tree works: the three checks correspond to the three ways a phenomenon can be fake — harness artifact (reproducibility), statistical fluke (sign-flipped control), and measurement offset (scaling check). A finding that survives all three has used up its plausible failure budget; what's left is either the real mechanism or a fourth-order bug subtle enough to be a paper on its own. Almost no "surprises" survive step 1, which is why the discipline saves months.

  • Without a minimal repro, you have an impression, not a claim — and impressions don't survive peer review or your own future skepticism.
  • Estimating σseed2​ is prerequisite to any sweep; equation (C.1.1) is the actual decision rule, and most published "trends" violate it.
  • Loss-curve pathologies have distinct fingerprints (NaN cascade vs recoverable spike vs non-recoverable spike vs scaling-law deviation); diagnosis is by gradient-norm trajectory, not loss alone.
  • The bug-vs-result decision tree (reproduce → sign-flipped control → scaling check) eliminates ~90% of surprises before they reach a write-up; budget for this before writing.
  • "Bug-shaped results" — e.g., dedup leaks producing a memorization curve — look identical to real results until you inspect the actual examples; reading the data is non-negotiable.
Worked example: investigating a "memorization spike"

Scenario: you are training a 1.4B-parameter model on a code dataset. At step 18k of a 100k-step run, training loss drops sharply by 0.3 nats over 50 steps, then resumes its prior slope. Eval loss is unchanged. A colleague's first read is "the model memorized a chunk of training data."

Apply the decision tree. First, the gap between train and eval loss after the spike — is it larger than before? Compute it. If yes, memorization is consistent; if no, train loss simply hit easier data. Second, log a histogram of per-example losses at step 17,999 and 18,050. A memorization event shows a left-tail of near-zero losses on specific examples; a data-shard easiness shift shows a uniform translation.
