Third, replay the dataloader at step 18k with a different seed for the shard-permutation: does the spike still occur at the same step (bug in step counter), at a different step (data effect), or not at all (optimizer state effect)?

Suppose the histogram shows ~200 examples with loss <0.01 and the rest unchanged. That's the memorization fingerprint. Now the real question: is this a bug? Look at those 200 examples. If they are exact duplicates of held-out evaluation prompts that slipped through deduplication, you have a serious leak — a bug-shaped result. If they are common library boilerplate (license headers, imports), it is a real but uninteresting memorization event. The same loss-curve picture, two completely different write-ups. Until you read the examples, you do not have a finding.

Key definitions and results

Minimal repro
The smallest self-contained script reproducing a phenomenon; if you cannot write one, you have an impression, not a claim.
Seed variance σseed2​
Spread of an outcome metric across random seeds at fixed config; the noise floor below which sweep results are uninterpretable.
Chinchilla scaling law
For dense LLMs, compute-optimal allocation has tokens ≈20× parameters; deviations predict whether you are data- or parameter-bottlenecked.
NaN cascade
Loss → NaN preceded by gradient-norm spike; almost always a numerical/precision issue, not a learning-dynamics one.
Optimizer-divergence spike
Non-recoverable loss spike caused by corrupted Adam second-moment estimates; mitigated by gradient clipping and frequent optimizer-state checkpointing.
Sign-flipped control
The same intervention applied with a randomized or inverted target; effect must vanish or invert, else the original effect is artifact.
Bug-shaped result
A finding whose mechanism is "your pipeline leaks information"; indistinguishable from a real result until you inspect the data.

Retrieval practice

Q: Why does loss spike then recover in some runs, but not in others?
A: Recovery requires the optimizer's second-moment estimator to remain uncorrupted. If gradient clipping is in place and the spike is data-driven (rare batch), Adam's running statistics absorb it. If clipping fails and the gradient updates push Adam's v to extreme values, future updates become near-zero in all directions and the model can't escape.
Q: Why is "sweep with one seed per config" almost always wrong?
A: You cannot distinguish hyperparameter effects from seed noise without a noise-floor estimate. If σseed2​≈σhp2​, your trend is statistically indistinguishable from random walks of three independent re-runs.
Q: What's the first thing to check when your run is above the Chinchilla curve?
A: Learning rate. Most runs that miss the law are simply under-LR'd at their batch size. The second thing is whether your token count is correct (cosmetic tokenizers can lie by 10-15%).
Q: When is a 2-layer d=128 model not a valid minimal repro?
A: When the phenomenon requires representational capacity it doesn't have — induction heads (need ≥2 layers, fine), grokking (small-model is canonical, fine), but multi-step reasoning chains or in-context learning of complex algorithms can disappear below a width threshold. The honest answer: replicate at scale once after you understand the small-model story.
Q: You see scaling-law deviation that grows with FLOPs. What's the leading hypothesis?
A: An architectural or recipe bottleneck whose impact compounds — typically insufficient width for the data regime, or a normalization choice that becomes unstable at scale (RMSNorm vs LayerNorm interactions with bf16 are a classic).
Q: Why is gradient-norm logging non-negotiable?
A: It is the leading indicator for nearly every training pathology. NaN cascades, optimizer divergence, and dead-neuron events all show in grad norm 10-100 steps before they show in loss.

Problems

15 min · scenario15 min · scenarioProblem 1. You're running ablations for a paper. You have 200 H100-hours total. The full ablation grid has 32 cells. Each full run costs 8 H100-hours; each minimal repro costs 0.5 hours. Design your protocol.
Solution sketch. Don't try to run the full grid (would cost 256 hours). Instead: phase 1, run minimal repros on all 32 cells (16 hours) to identify the ~6 cells with the strongest effect signal at small scale. Phase 2, run 3 seeds × 6 cells at full scale (144 hours) for the cells that survived phase 1. Phase 3, use the remaining ~40 hours for follow-up runs on whatever phase 2 surprised you with. Critical addition: in phase 1, also include 3 seeds × 1 cell at full scale (24 hours) before phase 2, to estimate σseed2​ and confirm the small-scale ordering predicts large-scale ordering. If small-scale doesn't predict large-scale, you don't have a minimal repro and you need to redesign.
30 min · derivation15 min · scenarioProblem 2. A teammate reports that adding a particular auxiliary loss improves downstream benchmark scores by 1.2 points. You suspect this is noise. What four things do you ask for before believing it?
Solution sketch. (1) Number of seeds and per-seed scores — 1.2 points is within typical benchmark seed variance for many LM benchmarks. (2) Sign-flipped control — does multiplying the aux loss coefficient by zero (or randomizing its targets) leave the improvement intact? If yes, the aux loss isn't doing the work; some other change is. (3) The full eval suite, not the one benchmark — if 9 of 10 benchmarks moved by ±0.5 and one moved by +1.2, this is consistent with noise, and the report is a multiple-comparisons artifact. (4) The diff. Auxiliary losses often co-introduce changes to LR schedule, gradient clipping, or batch composition that account for the effect. Demand the actual diff, not the verbal description.

Going deeper (optional)

If you read one thing: Iliad ML Engineering track — the Colabs walk through experiment workflow at the right granularity; do the attention notebook even if you've built transformers before.

Optional depth: Attention from scratch Colab — the residual-stream view used in C.2; Runpod + VS Code remote — the actual GPU workflow you'll use; Tinker docs — the 2025 post-training API for fine-tunes without managing infra.

Solo assignment

Pick a tiny phenomenon you find surprising (induction-head emergence step, grokking onset, loss-curve kink in your favorite open run). Write a one-file minimal repro under 100 lines. Then write a 500-word memo: (1) the phenomenon's operational definition (what number, measured how); (2) the three plausible bug-shaped explanations and how you ruled each out; (3) what an adversarial reviewer would attack and how you'd defend it. Submit the script and the memo together.

C.2 Mechanistic Interpretability Circuits, superposition, sparse autoencoders, and the open question of whether SAE features are real.
Prerequisites: C.1; transformer internals (Q/K/V, residual stream as additive); linear algebra (subspaces, projections, rank); basic L1/L2 regularization; familiarity with the linear representation hypothesis.

Real transformers don't have one feature per neuron — they pack n≫d features into a d-dimensional residual stream via superposition, exploiting sparsity to tolerate near-orthogonal interference. Sparse autoencoders attempt to recover the feature directions by solving LSAE​(x)=∥x−x^∥22​+λ∥f(x)∥1​; the IOI circuit in GPT-2 small is the canonical existence proof that algorithm-level reverse-engineering is possible. Whether SAE features are "real" ontological units of computation, or partial duals induced by the ℓ1​ prior, is the open question.

Pre-reading questions

  1. State the superposition hypothesis precisely. When (sparsity condition) does it predict features are not aligned with the neuron basis?
  2. Write the SAE loss. What does each hyperparameter control, and why does L1 induce sparsity rather than just shrinkage?
  3. Name the IOI circuit components and the role each plays in subject-deletion behavior.
  4. Why is "feature splitting" evidence against treating SAE features as ground-truth ontological units?
  5. What would a refutation of the "SAE features are real" claim look like, operationally?

Core exposition

From neurons to features: superposition

The naive read of a network — "each neuron computes one feature" — is wrong for nontrivial transformers, and Elhage et al. 2022 ("Toy Models of Superposition") explains why. Suppose a network with hidden dimension d wants to represent n features with sparsity s (fraction of inputs where each feature is active). The superposition sparsity condition states that packing n>d features is tolerable when

s≲d1​,E[⟨vi​,vj​⟩]=O(d​1​) for random unit vi​,vj​∈Rd.

Then the network can place n>d feature directions in the residual stream as a set of (approximately) linearly independent vectors, accepting controlled interference at the price of representational capacity. A sparse feature is recoverable as long as the simultaneously-active feature count is small relative to d​: with k features firing, the noise in any one coordinate's readout scales as k​/d​, giving a Johnson-Lindenstrauss-style packing bound n≲exp(cd) for any fixed interference budget.

The operational consequence: features in real transformers are directions in the residual stream, not neurons. Individual MLP neurons are polysemantic — they fire for unrelated concepts because each neuron sits in a basis that doesn't align with the feature basis. Reverse-engineering a network therefore requires finding the feature directions, not reading them off neurons.

Four distinct levels of abstraction get conflated in interp talks: neurons are the canonical-basis dimensions of an MLP layer (often polysemantic, almost never the right unit); SAE features are recovered directions in the residual stream (candidate "concepts," each ideally monosemantic); attention heads are computational units that read from and write to the residual stream via Q/K/V/O matrices (the unit of attention pattern analysis); circuits are subgraphs of heads + MLPs + features that jointly implement a specific algorithm (e.g., IOI, induction). A claim about features is not a claim about heads; a claim about heads is not a claim about circuits.

Sparse autoencoders (SAEs)

An SAE is a one-hidden-layer autoencoder trained on residual-stream activations x∈Rd, with hidden dimension m≫d (often m=8d to 64d), encoder f(x)=ReLU(We​x+be​), decoder x^=Wd​f(x)+bd​, and loss

LSAE​(x)=∥x−x^∥22​+λ∥f(x)∥1​.

The reconstruction term forces the dictionary Wd​ to span the activation manifold; the ℓ1​ penalty pushes f(x) toward sparse, ideally one-hot codes. The hope: each hidden unit fi​ corresponds to a single interpretable feature, and the columns of Wd​ are the recovered feature directions (monosemanticity). λ trades reconstruction error against sparsity; too low and you recover a dense PCA-like basis, too high and you collapse to a few features that miss most of the variance.

Two prominent variants. Top-k SAE (Makhzani & Frey; Gao et al. 2024 at OpenAI) replaces ℓ1​ with a hard top-k constraint, giving the objective

LTopK​(x)=∥x−Wd​TopKk​(We​x+be​)∥22​,TopKk​(z)i​=zi​⋅1[zi​∈top-k(z)].

This removes the shrinkage bias ℓ1​ imposes on active features (ℓ1​ penalizes their magnitudes too, biasing decoder columns to compensate by growing) and tends to give cleaner reconstruction-vs-sparsity Pareto fronts. Gated SAEs (Rajamanoharan et al. 2024) separate the "is this feature active" gate from the "how much" magnitude, recovering the ℓ1​-induced shrinkage at the cost of doubled parameters in the encoder.

Feature splitting plus dictionary non-identifiability is the most serious open problem for the "SAE features = ontology" claim. Train two SAEs on the same activations with different m or different λ and you recover different feature sets — not because one is wrong, but because the SAE objective has no unique minimizer in the over-complete regime: any rotation that preserves sparsity gives a valid alternative decomposition. Engels et al. 2024 and Bricken et al. 2024 demonstrate empirically that two SAEs trained on identical data with different seeds can disagree on ∼30% of features at m=16d. If the SAE basis is non-canonical, then "SAE feature X explains behavior Y" is a claim about your particular SAE run, not about the model. Calibrate ontological confidence accordingly.

At scale, Templeton et al. 2024 (Anthropic) trained SAEs on Claude 3 Sonnet's residual streams, recovering on the order of 107 features including identifiable concepts ("Golden Gate Bridge," "code with security vulnerabilities," "sycophantic praise") that could be steered to change model behavior. This is the strongest evidence yet that something feature-like lives in the activations.

Circuits: the IOI case

The IOI circuit (Wang, Variengien, Conmy, Shlegeris, Steinhardt 2022, "Interpretability in the Wild") was the first end-to-end reverse-engineering of a non-trivial algorithm in a real language model. It established the modern mech-interp playbook: (1) define a behavior precisely on a templated dataset, (2) localize via activation patching, (3) name the heads by function, (4) verify by ablation that the named circuit accounts for most of the behavior. Before Wang et al., interp had induction heads (Olsson et al. 2022) and toy models of superposition (Elhage et al. 2022) but no scaled-up algorithmic story. After IOI, dozens of follow-up circuit papers (greater-than detection, modular arithmetic, docstring completion) used the same methodology, and the field's burden of proof shifted from "show interesting heads" to "show a discoverable, intervention-validated algorithm." It also revealed the redundancy problem (Backup Name Movers) that made single-head ablations misleading and motivated path patching as the standard tool.

Beyond features, the goal is to identify circuits: subgraphs of the computation that implement a specific algorithm. The canonical worked case is Indirect Object Identification (IOI; Wang et al. 2022) in GPT-2 small. Task: given "When Mary and John went to the store, John gave a drink to ___", predict "Mary." Wang et al. identify, by causal intervention (activation patching), a circuit spanning ~26 attention heads organized into named functional groups:

  • Duplicate Token Heads (e.g., 0.1, 0.10, 3.0): in early layers, attend from the second occurrence of a name back to the first. These flag which tokens are repeated.
  • Previous Token Heads (e.g., 2.2, 4.11): attend to the token immediately preceding the current one; supply positional structure.
  • S-Inhibition Heads (e.g., 7.3, 7.9, 8.6, 8.10): write a signal into the residual stream at the END position that says "the subject token is duplicate; do not copy it." They suppress the wrong name.
  • Name Mover Heads (e.g., 9.9, 9.6, 10.0): in late layers, attend from the END position to the indirect object (Mary) and copy its embedding to the output via their OV circuits. These are the heads that actually write the answer.
  • Backup Name Movers (e.g., 9.0, 9.7, 10.10, 10.6, 11.2, 11.9): redundant copies that activate when primary name movers are ablated. Their existence means single-head ablations underestimate causal importance — a robustness property of the learned circuit, not the experimenter's tool.
  • Negative Name Movers (e.g., 10.7, 11.10): heads that decrease the logit of the correct name, partially canceling the name movers' output. They appear to implement a calibration mechanism.

The IOI circuit is the existence proof that mechanistic interpretation at the level of algorithm-implementing subgraphs is possible. It is also a sobering anchor: 26 heads for a 7-word sentence in a 12-layer model. Scaling this style of analysis to GPT-4 via case-by-case manual investigation is hopeless, which is why SAEs and automated attribution methods (Marks et al. 2024 sparse feature circuits; Anthropic's 2025 attribution graphs) are the active frontier.

Are SAE features real?

The most important open question in mech interp 2025. Three concerns:

(1) Feature splitting / non-identifiability. Train two SAEs on the same activations with different dictionary sizes or different ℓ1​ coefficients. You get different feature sets. A "Golden Gate Bridge" feature at m=8d splits into "Golden Gate at night," "Golden Gate photographed," "Golden Gate mentioned in poetry," etc. at m=64d. This is not pathology — finer dictionaries can resolve finer features — but it means there is no canonical SAE and "the feature" is not a well-defined object. Engels et al. and Bricken et al. have argued this undermines SAE-as-ontology claims.

(2) Reconstruction loss vs interpretability. Lower Lrecon​ at fixed sparsity does not entail more interpretable features. SAEs can find sparse codes that happen to be dense in semantic content (a single feature firing for many concepts that co-occur in training data) and pass quantitative monosemanticity checks while still being interpretively useless.

(3) The "dark matter." SAEs reconstruct ~80-90% of activation variance in practice. The missing 10-20% may contain the most important computation (rare features, dense computations not in superposition). Recent crosscoder / transcoder methods (Anthropic 2024-2025) aim to fix this by reconstructing function, not just activations: a transcoder is trained to predict an MLP's output from a sparse code of its input, capturing the input → output mapping the MLP implements rather than the activations it produces.

The honest summary: SAEs find something that often looks like features, and steering on these directions changes model behavior in semantically coherent ways. Whether this "something" is the ontology the network is "really" using, or an interpretable but partial dual basis induced by the loss, is unsettled.

You might wonder: are SAE features "real" in the same sense a head is real? Here's a sharpening. A head is a fixed projection WO​WV​ in the model's weights — there's no dispute about its existence; reverse-engineering its function is what's hard. An SAE feature is a column of Wd​ in an auxiliary model trained post hoc with an ℓ1​ prior; its existence is conditional on the SAE training run. Two operational tests for "real": (1) does the feature direction survive across seeds and dictionary sizes (most don't, fully); (2) does intervention on it predict downstream behavior in a way that other parameter-matched directions (random unit vectors, PCA components) do not (often yes — this is the strongest evidence). Anthropic's 2024-2025 work argues the second test is what matters; critics counter that the first is the prerequisite for ontology claims. Both can be right — features may be a useful operational handle without being the network's "native" representation.

Phenomena in practice: what mech-interp has actually found
The IOI circuit in GPT-2 small — Wang et al. 2022

Task and method. GPT-2 small (12 layers, 12 heads/layer, 117M params). Task: complete sentences like "When Mary and John went to the store, John gave a drink to ___" → "Mary". Method: path patching with corrupted ABC distribution, causal interventions, attention pattern analysis.

The 26-head circuit, by role. A specific named subgraph implements a 4-stage human-interpretable algorithm (detect duplicate name → compute its position → inhibit attention to that position → copy the remaining IO name to logits):

  • Duplicate Token Heads (2): 3.0, 0.1 — active at S2, attend to S1
  • Previous Token Heads (2): 4.11, 2.2
  • Induction Heads (2): 5.5, 6.9 — backup duplicate detection
  • S-Inhibition Heads (4): 7.3, 7.9, 8.6, 8.10
  • Name Mover Heads (3): 9.6, 9.9, 10.0 — copy IO name to logits
  • Negative Name Mover Heads (2): 10.7, 11.10 — write against the correct answer
  • Backup Name Movers (8): 9.0, 9.7, 10.1, 10.2, 10.6, 10.10, 11.2, 11.9 — activate when primary Name Movers are ablated (self-repair)
  • Fuzzy variants: Fuzzy Duplicate Token Head (0.10), Fuzzy Induction Heads (5.8, 5.9)

Faithfulness. The circuit recovers ~87% of the full-model logit difference (residual 13% of F(M) = 3.56 logit diff). The Name Mover OV-matrix is approximately a name-copying matrix when projected onto the relevant subspace — direct mechanistic interpretation. Backup heads activating on ablation is the most striking empirical detail: it shows neural circuits have redundancy, which complicates the "this single head implements function X" story.

ACDC — automated circuit discovery, Conmy et al. 2023

Method. Iterative edge-pruning over the model's computational graph. Each edge is ablated with a corrupted-distribution mean; edges with negligible KL impact are pruned. Tested on IOI, Greater-Than, Docstring, Induction, Gendered Pronoun, tracr-reverse, tracr-xproportion.

Findings. ACDC with zero ablations perfectly recovers tracr ground-truth circuits (AUC-ROC = 1.000 on tracr-xproportion and tracr-reverse). On IOI it rediscovers the Wang et al. heads. Faithfulness target on IOI is the Wang baseline (logit diff ≈ 3.55). ACDC compares favorably with Subnetwork Probing (SP) and Head Importance Score Pruning (HISP); ACDC operates on edges, the others originally on nodes. Paper itself acknowledges that matching the manual-Wang performance is empirically unstable — the methodology is real but not yet plug-and-play.

SAE features at scale — Bricken 2023 → Templeton 2024

Bricken et al. 2023 ("Towards Monosemanticity"). One-layer transformer, 512-dim MLP. Trained ReLU SAEs with L1 sparsity penalty on MLP activations across dictionary sizes 512, 1024, 2048, 4096, 8192, 16384, 32768, 131072. Found dramatically more monosemantic features than neurons. Interpretable features: Arabic-script feature, Hebrew-script feature, base64 feature, DNA-sequence feature, all-caps feature, etc. Feature splitting: as dictionary widens, a single base64 feature in the 512-dim SAE splits into ~3 sub-features in the 4096-dim SAE (different base64 sub-contexts) and further in 16k/32k. Released full visualizations for ~90 dictionaries; demonstrated "universality" — equivalent features appear in independently trained models.

Templeton et al. 2024 ("Scaling Monosemanticity"). SAEs on the middle-layer residual stream of Claude 3 Sonnet at three scales: ~1M, ~4M, ~34M features. Notable specific features:

  • 34M/31164353 = Golden Gate Bridge feature (multilingual + image-responsive)
  • 34M/9493533 = Brain Sciences / neuroscience
  • 1M/887839 = generic Tourist Attractions (Eiffel Tower, Pisa, Golden Gate, Sistine Chapel)

Feature steering — "Golden Gate Claude" demo. Clamping the Golden Gate feature to 10× max activation → model self-identifies as the bridge. Clamping Transit Infrastructure feature to 5× max → unprompted bridge mentions in responses. Safety-relevant feature categories explicitly catalogued: backdoors, slurs, lying/deception, power-seeking, sycophancy, bioweapons. Authors flag that even 34M features does not cover all knowledge in Sonnet — "dark matter" remains.

Top-k and Gated SAEs — scaling and shrinkage fixes

Top-k SAEs (Gao et al. 2024, OpenAI). Replace L1 penalty with hard sparsity via TopK activation (keep largest k pre-activations, zero rest). Trained on GPT-2 small and GPT-4-architecture models up to 16M latents on 40B tokens of GPT-4 activations. Clean power laws in MSE vs compute / dictionary size. Few dead latents even at largest scale. Headline result: 16M-latent SAE on GPT-4 reaches downstream LM-loss equivalent to 10% of GPT-4 pretraining compute when substituted in.

Gated SAEs (Rajamanoharan et al. 2024, GDM). Diagnoses a specific failure mode of L1 SAEs: shrinkage — L1 systematically underestimates feature magnitudes because the penalty applies to the magnitude itself. Gated SAEs separate two encoder roles: gating (which directions are active, L1 here only) and magnitude estimation (pure linear, no L1 bias). Tested on Pythia-2.8B and Gemma residual/MLP/attention sites. Pareto improvement over standard ReLU+L1 at every site tested. At maximal loss-recovered point, Gated SAE wins every comparison; about half the activated features needed for equal loss recovery vs L1 SAE.

Attribution Graphs — Lindsey et al. 2025 ("On the Biology of a Large Language Model")

Setup. Claude 3.5 Haiku. Cross-layer transcoder (CLT) replacing MLPs with ~30M interpretable features. Attribution graphs trace causal feature interactions; validated by intervention.

Specific circuits discovered.

  • Multi-step reasoning: "capital of state containing Dallas" → internal Dallas → Texas → Austin sequence. Inhibiting Texas features changes output (e.g., swapping in California → "Sacramento") — direct causal evidence the intermediate step is load-bearing.
  • Poetry rhyme planning: model activates candidate end-of-line rhyme features at the newline, before writing the line. Injecting alternative rhyme features causes line restructuring in ~70% of 25 test poems.
  • Multilingual circuits: ~30–40% feature intersection-over-union across English / French / Chinese for matched-meaning prompts in middle layers; shared antonym features across languages.
  • Addition: mix of low-precision "near X" features and memorized single-digit lookup tables. The model's verbal explanation contradicts its actual computational mechanism — no metacognition.
  • Refusals: "several dozen" refusal features identified across 200 harmful prompts; known-entity features suppress default refusal.
  • Jailbreaks (acronym "BOMB"): refusal circuit activates only after completing the first sentence (triggered by "new sentence" features); model stitches tokens without recognizing decoded meaning until forced to restart.
  • Hallucinations: driven by erroneous "known answer" feature activation for unknown entities — e.g., model confidently invents biographies for "Andrej Karpathy" prompts when the known-answer feature misfires.
Feature absorption — Chanin et al. 2024

Setup. First-letter classification task on token sets, k-sparse probing on SAE latents in Gemma-2-2B and Pythia.

Phenomenon. A general feature ("starts-with-L") is silently absorbed into specific token features ("lion"). The general feature then fails to fire in contexts where both should logically apply. Direct evidence: SAE latents identified as "first-letter" classifiers significantly underperform linear probes on precision/recall on the same task. This complicates the monosemanticity claim — a "lion" feature secretly carries hidden "L"-ness on top of its surface meaning.

Worked example: superposition condition and SAE recovery

Consider a toy: d=4, true features n=8, each feature independently active with probability p=0.1. Sparsity s=p=0.1; the condition s<1/d=0.25 holds, so superposition is feasible. Generate ground-truth feature directions v1​,…,v8​∈R4 as random unit vectors; generate activations x=∑i​ai​vi​ where ai​ is a half-normal with prob p, zero otherwise.

Train an SAE with hidden dim m=16 and λ chosen so average sparsity ≈0.1⋅8=0.8 features per example. After training, compute the maximum cosine similarity between each true vi​ and each SAE decoder column. You'll typically see 7 or 8 of the true features recovered with cos>0.9 and the remaining SAE columns being either redundant copies (two columns at cos>0.8 with the same vi​) or dead. This matches the empirical phenomenology at scale: dictionary slightly larger than feature count gives near-perfect recovery with some dead units.

Now repeat with p=0.5 (dense regime, condition violated). Reconstruction error stays low but no decoder column has cos>0.6 with any single vi​ — the SAE recovers a rotated mixture, not the original basis. This is the most mechanically clean version of the identifiability concern: when features aren't sparse in the data, the SAE basis is not the feature basis even if both exist.

  • Superposition: when feature sparsity s≲1/d, networks can represent more features than dimensions by placing them along nearly-orthogonal directions with controlled interference ⟨vi​,vj​⟩∼O(1/d​).
  • SAE objective LSAE​=∥x−x^∥2+λ∥f(x)∥1​ recovers features assuming sparsity; top-k and gated variants address dead features and L1-shrinkage bias.
  • IOI circuit (Wang et al. 2022): Duplicate/Previous Token heads → S-Inhibition heads → Name Movers (with Backup and Negative variants) — a discoverable, named algorithm in a 124M-param GPT-2.
  • SAE-as-ontology is contested: feature splitting (one "feature" at scale K becomes many at 8K), recon-vs-interp tradeoffs, and "dark matter" reconstruction error suggest SAE features are partly L1-shaped artifacts.
  • Practical bar: a circuit-level claim should reproduce on adversarial inputs, survive causal intervention (activation patching), and predict downstream behavior — not just look plausible on cherry-picked examples.

Key definitions and results

Linear representation hypothesis
High-level features are encoded as approximately linear directions in activation space; manipulation along these directions causally controls model behavior.
Superposition hypothesis
Networks represent more features than they have neurons by placing features along (nearly) linearly independent residual-stream directions; feasible when feature activations are sufficiently sparse (s≲1/d for the simplest analysis).
SAE
Sparse autoencoder with overcomplete dictionary minimizing ∥x−x^∥22​+λ∥f(x)∥1​; columns of the decoder are candidate feature directions.
Top-k SAE
Variant that hard-thresholds f(x) to its k largest entries; removes the ℓ1​ shrinkage bias on active features.
Feature splitting
The phenomenon that increasing SAE dictionary size resolves a single feature into multiple sub-features; implies no canonical "true" SAE.
Name Mover head
In the IOI circuit, a late-layer attention head that copies the indirect object's token embedding to the END position via its OV circuit.
S-Inhibition head
Mid-layer head that suppresses the subject (duplicate name) token at END, enabling Name Movers to attend to the correct (non-duplicate) name.
Activation patching
Causal-intervention method: replace a component's activation with one from a different prompt and measure downstream change; the gold standard for circuit-level causal claims.
Transcoder / crosscoder
SAE variants that reconstruct function (MLP output, or cross-layer activations) rather than raw activations, aiming to capture computation, not just representation.

Retrieval practice

Q: Why does ℓ1​ produce sparsity instead of just small weights?
A: The ℓ1​ ball is non-smooth at the axes; the optimum of a smooth loss + ℓ1​ penalty typically lies on a low-dimensional face of the ball, i.e., with some coordinates exactly zero. ℓ2​ has a smooth ball and pulls all coordinates uniformly toward zero without producing exact zeros.
Q: Why are Backup Name Movers a problem for naive ablation studies?
A: Ablating Name Movers individually shows small effect because Backup Name Movers compensate. You'd conclude the heads are unimportant. Path patching and joint ablations reveal the full circuit. The lesson: redundancy in learned circuits means causal-importance methods must be designed to handle distributed implementations.
Q: You train an SAE and 30% of features are "dead" (never fire). Is this a problem?
A: Mild. Dead features waste capacity but don't corrupt the live ones. Often fixed by resampling (Anthropic, Bricken et al. 2023) — periodically reinitializing dead features to high-loss examples. If >60% are dead, your λ is too high or your dictionary too large for the data.
Q: What does steering on an SAE feature direction actually do, mechanically?
A: You add αv (where v is the feature's decoder column) to the residual stream at some layer during a forward pass. Downstream computation reads this as an extra feature firing with magnitude α. If the feature is "real," output shifts coherently along its semantic; if it's a polysemantic artifact, the output drifts incoherently.
Q: Why doesn't reconstruction loss alone certify a "good" SAE?
A: Reconstruction is necessary but not sufficient. PCA achieves low reconstruction loss with dense, uninterpretable components. The interpretability bar is monosemanticity (features fire for a single concept) and causal effect (steering produces semantically coherent change), neither of which is directly optimized.
Q: State the superposition sparsity condition.
A: Heuristic version: features can be packed at density n>d when expected simultaneously-active feature count is small relative to d​; for independent features active with probability p, this gives the rough condition p⋅n≲d​, or per-feature sparsity s≲1/d for the simplest n≈d/s regime.

Problems

30 min · derivationProblem 1. Derive (informally) why ℓ1​ plus reconstruction induces an interference cost between any two feature directions vi​,vj​ whose inner product is non-zero. Why does this create the "Decoder Shrinkage" bias that Top-k SAEs are designed to fix?
Solution sketch. When features i and j co-occur, their contributions to x are ai​vi​+aj​vj​. The optimal sparse code fi​,fj​ is recovered via the encoder, but at decode x^=fi​Wd​[:,i]+fj​Wd​[:,j]. If ⟨vi​,vj​⟩=0, the ℓ1​ penalty makes it cheaper to under-activate fi​,fj​ and compensate by inflating ∥Wd​[:,i]∥ and ∥Wd​[:,j]∥ (no ℓ1​ on decoder). The activations are biased low; this is "shrinkage." Top-k removes the ℓ1​ on active features (only the gating is hard, not the magnitude), so active features can take their natural magnitudes and decoder columns don't need to compensate.
15 min · scenarioProblem 2. You apply activation patching on the IOI prompt: replace the activation of head 9.9 at the END token with the activation from a counterfactual prompt where Mary and John are swapped. Predict what happens to the output logit on "Mary" vs "John" and explain in terms of the circuit.
Solution sketch. Head 9.9 is a primary Name Mover. Its END-position output is approximately "(indirect object name embedding) via OV." Patching with the swapped counterfactual replaces that output with "John" embedding instead of "Mary." Predicted effect: logit on "John" rises substantially, logit on "Mary" falls. Magnitude is partial because (a) other Name Movers (9.6, 10.0) still write "Mary"; (b) Backup Name Movers may compensate. Wang et al. report ∼50% of the logit difference moves, consistent with one of several redundant heads being intercepted. If you also patch 9.6 and 10.0, the effect approaches full inversion.
15 min · scenarioProblem 3. Construct a minimal toy where SAE feature splitting is forced. Specify the data-generating process and the resulting SAEs at two dictionary sizes.
Solution sketch. Data: 2-feature hierarchical generative model in d=10. Feature A = "animal" (direction u, prob 0.3). When A active, conditionally fire one of two sub-features: A_dog (u+ε1​) or A_cat (u+ε2​), each with prob 0.5. Train SAE with m=4: it recovers u as a single feature (animal). Train SAE with m=8: it recovers u+ε1​ and u+ε2​ as two features (dog, cat). Both are valid sparse codes; neither is wrong. This is the feature-splitting phenomenon: the "right" dictionary depends on the resolution you want, and there is no objective stopping point.

Going deeper (optional)

If you read one thing: Anthropic's 2025 attribution graphs — best end-to-end view of the current scalable circuit story, with concrete case studies showing where SAE-based circuit tracing succeeds and where it doesn't.

Optional depth: Open Problems in Mech Interp (2025) — frontier critique, including the SAE-as-ontology debate; A Pragmatic Vision for Interpretability — why interp's value may be diagnostic rather than ontological; Neuronpedia — actually click around a real SAE; calibration for "what features look like"; SAE training Colab — train one yourself, with the dead-feature resampling trick.

Solo assignment

Pick one prompt-completion behavior in GPT-2 small or Pythia-160M (e.g., gender pronoun resolution, simple arithmetic, a specific induction pattern). (1) Form a hypothesis about which heads implement it. (2) Run activation patching to validate or falsify. (3) Pull SAE features (via Neuronpedia or a Gemma-2-2B SAE) at the relevant layer and identify the top 3 features that fire on your prompts. (4) Write a 2-page memo: what you found, what would falsify the hypothesis, and where you suspect the analysis breaks down. The deliverable is the memo, not the notebook.

C.3 Computational Mechanics ε-machines, causal states, statistical complexity, and why transformers learn belief-state geometry.
Prerequisites: C.2; HMMs (forward algorithm, belief updates); Shannon entropy; basic probability simplex geometry; linear algebra of stochastic matrices.

Crutchfield's causal states S(x≤t​)={x≤t′​:P(X>t​∣x≤t′​)=P(X>t​∣x≤t​)} are the unique minimal sufficient statistic for prediction. The associated ϵ-machine is the smallest unifilar HMM equivalent to the process; its entropy Cμ​=H[S] — the statistical complexity — measures pattern, with Cμ​≥hμ​ (entropy rate) and Cμ​=0 iff the process is IID. Shai et al. 2024 showed transformers trained on HMM emissions develop residual-stream geometry isomorphic to the mixed-state presentation, suggesting causal-state recovery is what next-token learners actually do.

Pre-reading questions

  1. State Crutchfield's definition of a causal state. Why is the equivalence relation on past sequences, not on hidden states?
  2. What is statistical complexity Cμ​, and how does it differ from entropy rate hμ​?
  3. For the even process, work out the causal states and compute Cμ​.
  4. What does it mean for a transformer to "learn belief state geometry," and what would falsify this claim?
  5. Why is the mixed state presentation (MSP) the relevant object, not the ε-machine itself?

Core exposition

Causal states and the ε-machine

Computational mechanics (Crutchfield & Young 1989; Shalizi & Crutchfield 2001) asks: given a stationary stochastic process producing observations x0​,x1​,… drawn from some distribution μ, what is the minimal predictive representation? Define an equivalence relation on past sequences:

x≤t​∼ϵ​x≤t′​⟺P(X>t​∣X≤t​=x≤t​)=P(X>t​∣X≤t​=x≤t′​).

ε-machines are not HMMs in the textbook sense, despite both being state automata with stochastic emissions. An HMM is a generative model: you posit a hidden Markov chain and emission distributions, then fit parameters to data. There can be many HMMs equivalent to the same process — the latent structure is a modeling assumption, identifiable only up to relabeling and (sometimes) not even that (Locatello-style non-identifiability). An ε-machine is a derived object: its states are defined directly by the equivalence relation in equation (C.3.2) on past observations of the process. It is unique up to relabeling, minimal, and unifilar (deterministic transitions given emission). Concretely: the same data-generating process can be represented by many HMMs but exactly one ε-machine, and the ε-machine is typically smaller than the HMMs you'd write down naively.

Two pasts are equivalent iff they induce the same distribution over futures. The equivalence classes are the causal states; the function mapping pasts to their class is ϵ; the resulting automaton (causal states + transition probabilities + emission probabilities) is the ε-machine. Crutchfield's theorem: the ε-machine is the unique minimal sufficient statistic for prediction; any predictor with fewer states either loses predictive accuracy or is non-deterministic in its state transitions.

The statistical complexity Cμ​=H[S] is the Shannon entropy of the stationary distribution over causal states. This is the irreducible memory cost of optimal prediction. Crucially, Cμ​≥hμ​ (entropy rate) with equality only for IID processes — for any process with structure, you must remember strictly more than you reveal per step.

Why Cμ​≥hμ​? The entropy rate hμ​=limn​H(Xn​∣X<n​) is the information revealed per emission once you have all past context. The statistical complexity Cμ​=H[S] is the information your predictor must store about the past to reach that rate. Storing strictly less than hμ​ per step is impossible because the predictor's uncertainty about Xn​ is itself hμ​ — so its representation must carry enough state to produce a distribution of that entropy. Storing exactly hμ​ corresponds to "I store everything I'm about to reveal," which means the process has no structure beyond what its single next emission carries — that's the IID case. Any process with memory forces strict inequality: prediction memory is bounded below by the entropy rate, but pattern adds on top (you remember which distribution to draw from, not just the distribution's entropy).

The mixed state presentation

The ε-machine is the generator's view: each causal state has a deterministic transition on each emission. But a predictor that observes the emissions doesn't know which causal state the generator is in; it maintains a belief distribution over states. As observations arrive, this belief is updated by Bayes' rule. The set of beliefs the predictor can hold (as a function of observed history) is the mixed state presentation (MSP) of the process. The MSP lives in the simplex over causal states and has its own geometry: vertices, edges, fractal structure for certain processes. The MSP is what an optimal predictor needs to track; the ε-machine is what the generator needs to run.

A key distinction: generation requires knowing the ε-machine state; prediction requires tracking the MSP belief. For some processes (the ε-machine state is observable from emissions) these coincide. For most non-trivial processes they don't, and the MSP can have many more "effective states" than the ε-machine — sometimes uncountably many, if the belief simplex is densely visited.

HMMs vs ε-machines vs GHMMs. An HMM is a generative posit (hidden Markov chain + emissions); many HMMs can represent the same process. An ε-machine is the unique minimal unifilar HMM derived from causal-state equivalence — the smallest sufficient statistic for prediction. A generalised hidden Markov model (GHMM) drops the HMM's classical-state assumption and lets the latent dynamics live on richer state spaces (e.g., quantum-like superposition or continuous belief manifolds). Some processes have no efficient classical-HMM representation but admit a compact GHMM realization — these are exactly the cases where ε-machines fail to capture process complexity and where GHMM-style belief geometry (fractal MSP, post-quantum representations; Piotrowski et al. 2025) becomes the right object. The motivation: if frontier LLMs are predicting processes whose data-generating "world" is non-classical at any level (causal-state set is infinite or has algebraic structure that classical HMMs miss), then the GHMM belief geometry is what optimal prediction would track.

Transformers and belief state geometry

Shai et al. 2024 ("Transformers Represent Belief State Geometry in their Residual Stream") trained small transformers on token sequences emitted by hand-designed HMMs (mess3 process, RRXOR process). After training, they applied linear probes from the residual stream to the ground-truth belief state at each token position. Result: for processes where the MSP forms a 2D or fractal geometry inside the belief simplex, the residual stream activations recover that geometry — projecting the activations to 2D via PCA produces a configuration visually isomorphic to the true MSP, including fractal substructure for the mess3 process.

This is a striking result for several reasons. First, the geometry is not on the generator's ε-machine (which is finite and discrete) but on the predictor's MSP (which can be fractal). The transformer is doing the harder thing — tracking the belief, not the state. Second, the linear-probe recovery means the geometry is encoded approximately linearly in residual-stream directions, supporting the linear representation hypothesis from C.2 with an unusually clean ground truth. Third, the geometry is recovered even on processes where the transformer's downstream loss is near-optimal but the MSP has many "states" that could in principle be merged for the same loss; the transformer doesn't merge them, suggesting it represents the belief rather than a sufficient statistic of the belief.

The follow-up work (Piotrowski et al. 2025 on constrained belief updates; the quantum/post-quantum GHMM extensions) develops mechanism — how attention plus MLPs implement the Bayesian update — and pushes to processes that exceed classical HMM capacity. The agenda is sometimes overstated: belief-state geometry is empirically present for toy HMMs trained from scratch, but extrapolating to "frontier LLMs represent the belief state of the data-generating process of the internet" is a long inference that the field has not validated.

How strong is the Shai et al. 2024 result, really? Three caveats to keep in mind. First, the HMMs are hand-designed (mess3, RRXOR, fanizza) with known MSP geometry — this is the cleanest possible ground truth, and the linear probe accuracy is the tightest possible signal. Second, the architecture is tiny (4-layer, d=64) and trained to near-optimal loss on a process whose entropy rate is known in closed form; the "transformer learns belief geometry" claim is for the post-convergence regime, not mid-training. Third, the MSP geometry is recovered via linear probe with full regression — the probe is a learned readout, so what's being claimed is "the MSP is linearly decodable from the residual stream," which is weaker than "the residual stream is the MSP." None of this undermines the result for what it claims, but the jump from "small transformers on toy HMMs encode the MSP" to "language models encode a Bayesian posterior over a data-generating process" is several inferential steps and shouldn't be confused with the empirical content.

Phenomena in practice: what comp-mech experiments showed
Shai et al. 2024 — Transformers Represent Belief State Geometry

Setup. Train small transformers from scratch on sequences emitted by stochastic processes with known ε-machines: mess3 (parameters x = 0.05, α = 0.85 — a 3-state HMM whose mixed-state presentation is provably a Sierpiński-like fractal on the 2-simplex over the 3 latent states) and RRXOR. Linear probe (PCA / linear projection) trained from residual-stream activations to the predicted belief-state coordinates.

Observed phenomena.

  • mess3: final-layer residual stream projected linearly reproduces the predicted Sierpiński fractal in detail. The transformer represents the belief simplex geometry, not just the next-token distribution.
  • RRXOR: belief-state information is distributed across layers. Single-layer probes don't recover the geometry; concatenating all layers' activations is required.
  • Next-token-prediction-only is insufficient. For RRXOR, correlation R² = 0.31 between belief-state geometry and next-token-distribution geometry — meaning the model is doing something more than predicting the immediate next-token distribution, it's actually tracking the latent belief.
  • For mess3, by contrast, next-token-distribution pairwise structure is preserved in the belief representation — the two are partly aligned but not equivalent.

The strong reading (sometimes overclaimed): "transformers represent the belief state of the data-generating process." The weak reading (defensible): "for hand-designed toy HMMs, small transformers trained from scratch develop residual-stream geometry isomorphic to the optimal-predictor MSP, recoverable by linear probe." Don't extrapolate from toy HMMs to frontier LLMs without separate evidence.

Piotrowski et al. 2025 — post-quantum representations

Setup. Train RNN, LSTM, and Transformer architectures on three sequence-generation processes whose minimal generators require, respectively, a classical HMM, a quantum operator, and a post-quantum (general nonstochastic) GHMM operator.

Findings. Networks linearly represent the minimal belief geometry of each process — including geometries that cannot be realized by any classical HMM. This extends the Shai result beyond classical processes: when the data-generating process requires a post-quantum minimal representation, networks find it. Universality observed across architectures and hyperparameters. Linear representation again — straight probe, not nonlinear decoding.

For the C.3 narrative this is the empirical anchor of the GHMM disambig callout: networks adopt the right object (post-quantum belief geometry) when that's what the process requires, not the wrong-but-tractable classical-HMM approximation.

Constrained belief updates — RRXOR geometry across layers

Paper. "Constrained belief updates explain geometric structures in transformer representations" (arXiv 2502.01954). Follows up on the RRXOR puzzle from Shai et al.

Phenomenon. Transformers implement constrained-Bayesian belief updates — a partial-inference variant shaped by architectural constraints like parallel per-layer updates rather than full sequential inference. This explains why RRXOR geometry distributes across layers (each layer can only do part of the Bayesian update). The takeaway for the broader research program: transformers don't perform unconstrained Bayes; they perform the closest-to-Bayes inference compatible with their forward-pass structure.

Worked example: the even process

Define the even process as a binary stochastic process: emit 1's and 0's such that between any two consecutive 0's, an even (positive) number of 1's appears. Equivalently: after a 0, you may emit 1's freely, but you cannot emit another 0 until you've emitted an even number of 1's since the last 0.

The causal states: knowing the past, what do you need to predict the future? Exactly one bit — whether the current count of 1's since the last 0 is even or odd. Call these states SE​ (even) and SO​ (odd).

  • From SE​: emit 0 with prob p, stay in SE​; emit 1 with prob 1−p, go to SO​.
  • From SO​: must emit 1 (prob 1), go to SE​.

For p=1/2, the stationary distribution and Cμ​ follow from the transition structure, computed step by step:

  1. P=(P(SE​→SE​)P(SO​→SE​)​P(SE​→SO​)P(SO​→SO​)​)=(p1​1−p0​).

    Write the row-stochastic transition matrix over causal states. SE​ self-loops on emission 0 (probability p) and goes to SO​ on emission 1 (probability 1−p); SO​ deterministically emits 1 and returns to SE​.

  2. πP=π,π=(πE​,πO​)⟹{πE​=pπE​+πO​πO​=(1−p)πE​​

    The stationary distribution π is the left eigenvector of P with eigenvalue 1. Writing out the two balance equations gives one relation πO​=(1−p)πE​; the other (after substitution) collapses to a tautology, which is expected for a 2-state chain.

  3. πE​(1+(1−p))=1⟹πE​=2−p1​,πO​=2−p1−p​.

    Normalize so πE​+πO​=1. The general form holds for any p∈(0,1); at p=1/2 this gives πE​=2/3, πO​=1/3.

  4. Cμ​=H[π]=−32​log2​32​−31​log2​31​≈0.667⋅0.585+0.333⋅1.585≈0.918 bits.

    Statistical complexity is the Shannon entropy of π. Compare to the entropy rate: hμ​=πE​⋅H(p)+πO​⋅0=(2/3)H(1/2)=2/3≈0.667 bits. So Cμ​≈0.918>hμ​≈0.667 — strict inequality, as expected for a non-IID process. Memory cost exceeds emission entropy by ∼0.25 bits per step.

Now the MSP. Watching emissions, the predictor's belief over {SE​,SO​} evolves: after observing a 0, the predictor knows the state is SE​ (probability 1) — because SO​ deterministically emits 1. After observing a 1 from a known SE​, the predictor knows the state is now SO​. Then after another 1, back to SE​. So once a 0 has been seen, the MSP belief is always a vertex of the simplex (degenerate). Before any 0, the belief sits at the stationary distribution. The even process is "synchronizable": the MSP has only three points — the stationary belief, δSE​​, and δSO​​. Contrast this with the mess3 process used by Shai et al., whose MSP is a Sierpiński-triangle-like fractal — the predictor never synchronizes and the belief geometry is genuinely high-dimensional. The even process is the simplest non-trivial sanity check; mess3 is where transformer geometry recovery becomes visually striking.

  • Causal states are the unique minimal sufficient statistic for prediction — the equivalence classes of pasts inducing the same future distribution.
  • Statistical complexity Cμ​=H[S] measures pattern (irreducible memory cost of optimal prediction); Cμ​≥hμ​, and Cμ​=0 iff the process is IID.
  • The ε-machine is the smallest unifilar HMM equivalent to the process; the mixed-state presentation tracks the observer's belief over causal states and can be fractal.
  • Shai et al. 2024 empirical: transformers trained on HMM emissions develop residual-stream geometry isomorphic to the MSP — causal-state recovery is what next-token learners do.
  • Comp mech is the right lens for "what should an optimal predictor compute?" — and gives a target for what trained models should learn if they're at the prediction-optimal frontier.

Key definitions and results

Causal state
Equivalence class of past observation sequences inducing the same conditional distribution over futures; the unique minimal sufficient statistic for prediction.
ε-machine
Automaton whose states are causal states and whose transitions are labeled by emission probabilities; unique minimal predictively sufficient representation of a stationary process.
Statistical complexity Cμ​
Shannon entropy of the stationary distribution over causal states; irreducible memory cost of optimal prediction. Cμ​≥hμ​ (entropy rate).
Mixed state presentation (MSP)
The set of belief distributions over causal states an observer can hold given observed history; lives in the simplex over causal states; can be fractal.
Synchronization
Property that after some finite observation, the predictor's belief collapses to a single causal state (a simplex vertex); not all processes synchronize.
Crutchfield minimality
Any predictor with fewer than |causal states| states is either lossy in prediction or non-deterministic in transitions; the ε-machine is unique up to relabeling.
Belief-state recovery claim
Empirical finding (Shai et al. 2024) that small transformers trained on HMM emissions encode the MSP geometry approximately linearly in their residual stream.

Retrieval practice

Q: Why must Cμ​≥hμ​?
A: hμ​ is the rate at which the process reveals new information per emission; Cμ​ is the rate at which a predictor must store information about the past. If Cμ​<hμ​, the predictor would be revealing more entropy than it stores about the past, which is impossible for a process with structure (only IID processes saturate the bound with Cμ​=hμ​=0 for a degenerate generator, or in general Cμ​=0 only for IID).
Q: Why is the MSP, not the ε-machine, what the transformer represents?
A: The transformer is a predictor, not a generator. It only sees emissions; it doesn't know which causal state generated each. Its job is to maintain a distribution over causal states given history — exactly the MSP. The ε-machine state is the latent the predictor is trying to infer.
Q: What would refute the "transformers learn belief state geometry" claim?
A: A trained transformer that achieves near-optimal next-token loss on an HMM but whose residual stream does not linearly encode the MSP. Specifically: train on a process whose MSP has a known fractal structure; if PCA of residual streams shows a structurally different geometry (e.g., a smooth manifold instead of fractal, or a lower-dimensional collapse), the claim fails. Shai et al. showed this doesn't happen for their test processes, but it's an empirical claim and could fail for other architectures or processes.
Q: For the even process, what is the MSP at "no observations yet"?
A: The stationary distribution: P(SE​)=2/3, P(SO​)=1/3 (for p=1/2). After any 0 is observed, the belief snaps to δSE​​; after a 1 from a known SE​, to δSO​​; and so on. So the MSP has three points only.
Q: What is the relationship between Cμ​ and the optimal predictor's representation dimension?
A: Cμ​ is an information-theoretic lower bound (in bits) on what must be stored; the predictor's representation must have at least Cμ​ bits of information about the past. For processes with finite causal states, dim≥⌈log2​∣causal states∣⌉ is a coarser bound. For processes with infinite/fractal MSPs, no finite-dimensional representation is exactly optimal.
Q: Why is "generation easier than prediction" possible?
A: To generate, you only need to track which causal state you're in (the ε-machine state) — that's it. To predict, you need to track the belief over states given emissions, which can have a much larger or even fractal state space (the MSP). The asymmetry arises whenever emissions don't uniquely identify the state.

Problems

30 min · derivationProblem 1. Consider the "Golden Mean" process: binary, where the constraint is no two consecutive 0's. Construct the ε-machine, find the stationary distribution (parametrize by the probability of emitting 1 from the "free" state), and compute Cμ​.
Solution sketch. Two states. SA​ = "just emitted 1 (or initial)": may emit 0 with prob q → SB​, or 1 with prob 1−q → SA​. SB​ = "just emitted 0": must emit 1 (prob 1) → SA​. Stationary: πB​=qπA​, πA​+πB​=1, so πA​=1/(1+q), πB​=q/(1+q). Cμ​=H[πA​,πB​]. At q=1/2: πA​=2/3, πB​=1/3, Cμ​≈0.918 bits. Notice this is isomorphic to the even process's stationary distribution at p=1/2 — both are the same 2-state Markov chain with different emission labels.
30 min · derivationProblem 2. Show that for an IID process, Cμ​=0. Conversely, give an example of a process with Cμ​>0 but hμ​=0.
Solution sketch. IID: all pasts induce the same future distribution (the marginal), so there's exactly one causal state; H[S]=0. Converse: a deterministic periodic sequence, e.g., 01010101... — hμ​=0 (no entropy in emissions given enough history) but the predictor needs to know whether the next emission is 0 or 1, i.e., two causal states with equal probability: Cμ​=1 bit. This is the cleanest example of why prediction memory and entropy rate are different things.
1–2 hr · openProblem 3. Design a small experiment to test whether a 2-layer, d=64 transformer trained on the even process represents the MSP linearly in its residual stream. Specify the training setup, the probe, and the falsification criterion.
Solution sketch. Setup: generate ∼106 tokens from the even process with p=0.5; train a 2-layer transformer with cross-entropy on next-token prediction until eval loss matches the entropy rate (=hμ​ for this process, computable in closed form). Probe: at each token position, extract residual stream after layer 1 and layer 2; fit a linear probe (least squares) from the 64-d activation to the 2-d ground-truth belief vector (P(SE​),P(SO​)) at that position, computed via Bayes from the observed emission sequence. Metric: MSE of probe predictions. Falsification: if MSE is not significantly lower than the MSE of probing on a randomly-initialized transformer (i.e., a chance baseline), the linear-encoding claim fails for this architecture/process. Pass: MSE ≪ chance baseline and visualization shows the predicted belief points clustering at the three MSP points.

Going deeper (optional)

If you read one thing: Shai et al. 2024, "Transformers Represent Belief State Geometry" — the empirical bridge from comp mech to interpretability; figures alone justify the read.

Optional depth: Constrained belief updates (2025) — mechanism story for how attention implements Bayesian belief updating; Post-quantum representations (2025) — GHMM extension, when classical HMM capacity is insufficient; HMM/GHMM exercise sheet — work through the process-design tutorial before any modeling.

Solo assignment

Implement a 2-state HMM (e.g., the even process), generate sequences, compute belief-state updates from observations in closed form. Train a tiny transformer (2 layers, d=64) on the sequences. Fit a linear probe from each layer's residual stream to the ground-truth belief vector; report R2 per layer. Then run the same probe on a randomly-initialized transformer as a baseline. Write a 1-page memo: how cleanly does the geometry emerge, at which layer, and what would make you doubt the result (e.g., probe overfitting, baseline contamination)?

C.4 Abstractions and Latents Natural abstractions, good regulators, information bottlenecks, and why different minds might converge on the same concepts.
Prerequisites: Information theory (entropy, mutual information, KL); Bayesian networks; basic measure theory helpful; C.3 (causal states); familiarity with the alignment "pointers problem."

The natural abstractions hypothesis (Wentworth) claims high-dim systems admit low-dim summaries Λ with mediation I(Xi​;Xj​∣Λ)≈0 and observer-uniqueness H(Λ∣Xi​)≈0 — a constructive read of the Conant–Ashby good-regulator theorem. Tishby's information bottleneck and the representation-convergence empirics (Kornblith CKA, Huh Platonic-representation 2024) are consistent with this, but Locatello et al.'s impossibility theorem says disentanglement without inductive bias is impossible — so any "natural" abstraction is natural relative to a prior, not absolute. The point for alignment: identifying the right abstraction is what lets you point at "human values" precisely enough to optimize toward them.

Pre-reading questions

  1. State the natural abstraction hypothesis formally. What "low-dimensional summary" and what "distance" is it about?
  2. What does the Conant-Ashby good regulator theorem actually say, and what does it not say?
  3. How does Tishby's information bottleneck principle relate to abstraction selection?
  4. Under what conditions can a latent variable be identified up to symmetry from data, and when is identifiability hopeless?
  5. Why would two architectures trained on the same data converge on similar abstractions, if they do?

Core exposition

The natural abstraction hypothesis

Wentworth's natural abstraction hypothesis (NAH; 2020-2024 series) is the conjecture that natural systems support a small set of low-dimensional summaries that are sufficient for prediction-at-a-distance, and that different observers will converge on these summaries despite using different sensors or representations. The formal version (Wentworth, "Natural Latents"):

(i) Mediation:(ii) Redundancy:​I(Xi​;Xj​∣Λ)≈0for distant i,jH(Λ∣Xi​)≈0for each i​

Mediation says Λ screens off distant variables: once you know Λ, the variables become conditionally independent. Redundancy says Λ is determinable from any single Xi​ — the information is spread redundantly through the system. Wentworth's main result: if a latent satisfies (i) and (ii) for a distribution, it is essentially unique up to isomorphism. Any two observers who find any natural latent satisfying these conditions are recovering the same object (modulo encoding).

"Natural latent" is not the same kind of object as "latent variable" in latent-variable models (LVMs). In an LVM (factor analysis, VAE, HMM), the latent is a modeling assumption: you posit it exists, then fit. Different LVMs can fit the same data with different latents — the latent is your construction. A natural latent is instead a property of the distribution itself: it exists iff conditions (i)–(ii) of equation (C.4.1) are satisfied by some random variable Λ on the joint distribution, and when it exists it's unique up to isomorphism. There may be no natural latent for a given distribution (most distributions); when there is, it's the same object every observer recovers. The shift is "latent variable as your model" → "natural latent as a structural fact about the world the model is trying to capture."

The intuition: temperature is natural for a gas. Mediation: given the temperature, molecules in distant subregions become conditionally independent — the macrostate screens off the micro-correlations. Redundancy: any sufficiently large subregion of the gas tells you the temperature. The same applies to many physical and structural aggregates: pressure, chemical concentration, the orientation of a rigid object. Concepts in human language often correspond to such aggregates — which is the alignment-relevant claim: if AI systems learn the natural latents of the world, they may learn the same concepts humans do, making value transfer tractable.

Good regulator and information bottleneck

The good regulator theorem (Conant & Ashby 1970, "Every Good Regulator of a System Must Be a Model of That System") emerged from second-order cybernetics — Ashby's tradition of treating biological and artificial systems as homeostatic regulators against environmental perturbation. The original proof is short and information-theoretic: given a system S, a regulator R, and disturbances D with R choosing actions to minimize entropy of outcomes Z, if R is optimal then there exists a mapping h:R→S such that R and S become isomorphic. The theorem was philosophically influential for decades (cited heavily in cognitive science and predictive processing) but only recently re-entered AI safety discourse as a foundation for Wentworth's NAH and the "AI systems will necessarily develop world models" argument in agent foundations. The modern reading drops the strict Ashby assumptions (perfect regulation, no internal noise) and uses the structural intuition: control requires modeling. The strict theorem only proves it in a narrow setting; the alignment-relevant reading is the looser philosophical generalization.

Conant & Ashby's good regulator theorem (1970) states: "every good regulator of a system must be a model of that system." The precise version: an optimal controller R that minimizes the entropy of a system S's outputs (given disturbances D) must implement a function isomorphic to S's dynamics. The theorem is often invoked loosely to mean "intelligence requires modeling"; the technical content is narrower — it concerns optimal regulators of stochastic systems, requires specific information-flow assumptions, and depends on how "optimality" is defined. Still, the structural conclusion holds: any system that competently controls outcomes in a world will carry an internal representation isomorphic to the world's relevant structure. For alignment, this argues that capable AI systems will necessarily develop world models; the question is whether those world models share abstractions with humans.

Tishby's information bottleneck (Tishby, Pereira & Bialek 1999) is the complementary view from learning. Given input X and target Y, find a representation T that minimizes

LIB​(T)=I(X;T)−βI(T;Y).

The first term penalizes how much information T retains about X (compress); the second rewards relevance to Y (predict). The IB principle predicts that optimal representations lie on a Pareto front between compression and prediction; as β varies, you trace out a hierarchy of abstractions. Tishby's later "deep learning IB" interpretation — that SGD passes through a "compression phase" — has been contested (Saxe et al. 2018 argue the compression phase is an artifact of activation function choice). But the principle itself is robust: any sufficient statistic for Y that is minimal lives on the IB curve.

Connection to NAH: a natural latent satisfying mediation is essentially a sufficient statistic for prediction-at-a-distance. Adding redundancy is the constraint that the representation be recoverable from any local view, not just from a central oracle — which is what makes it shared across observers with different sensors.

Identifiability of latent variables

When can data uniquely determine a latent? The general answer is: rarely. For standard latent-variable models (Gaussian mixtures, factor analysis, plain VAEs), latents are identifiable only up to rotations, scalings, or label permutations. Locatello et al. 2019 showed that for fully unsupervised disentanglement, no algorithm can succeed without inductive bias — the problem is provably underdetermined. Identifiability requires additional structure: auxiliary labels (Khemakhem et al. 2020, "Identifiable VAEs"), known symmetries, or assumptions on the latent's dynamics. Natural latents bypass classical identifiability by trading uniqueness-of-the-best-latent for uniqueness-up-to-isomorphism-among-natural-latents: if a latent satisfies mediation and redundancy, it is unique within that class. Whether any natural latent exists for a given distribution is an empirical question per distribution.

The Locatello et al. 2019 impossibility theorem is sharper than "disentanglement is hard" — it states that for any generative model class large enough to contain arbitrary rotations of the prior, there exist infinitely many models that fit any finite dataset equally well but disagree on the disentangled factorization. No algorithm operating only on i.i.d. samples can distinguish them. The mistake to avoid: assuming a method "found" the right latents because reconstruction is good and the features look interpretable. Both are consistent with a rotated decomposition where you got lucky on the labels. The theorem doesn't say identifiability is impossible when you add inductive bias (auxiliary labels, time structure, intervention, mediation + redundancy à la NAH) — it says you must add it, and you must make the inductive bias explicit. Any claim of recovered latents that omits the inductive bias is unprovable as stated.

Condensation: discrete modular structure from knowledge organization

Natural latents ask "what low-dimensional summaries are preserved across observers?" Condensation (Demski 2022) asks a complementary question: "how should a world model be organized into discrete, interpretable modules?" The distinction is subtle but load-bearing — natural latents give minimal sufficient statistics; condensation factors a world model into causally separable conceptual chunks that remain stable under intervention. Where natural-latent theorems give pointwise agreement on summary variables, condensation predicts agreement on the boundaries between concepts — which features belong together as one "object" or "law" versus which belong in separate modules.

The condensation agreement result (informal statement): if two agents organize their world models via condensation — decomposing beliefs into causally independent modules — they converge on approximately the same partition, even without direct communication. This is the discrete-structure complement to the natural-latent uniqueness theorem; both predict observer-independent structure but on different objects (continuous summaries vs. discrete partitions). For alignment this matters because human values are typically expressed as relations between discrete concepts ("don't deceive a person about a promise"), so transferring values requires shared module boundaries, not just shared summary statistics.

Condensation vs compression: a maximally compressed code throws away redundancy; condensation preserves redundancy between modules while removing it within. Compression is information-theoretic; condensation is causally-grounded — it cares about which features stay together under intervention, not just which ones co-vary.

Why convergence might happen — and what could prevent it

The case for cross-architectural convergence of abstractions: (1) natural latents are distribution-determined, so any sufficiently capable predictor of the same data should recover them; (2) information bottleneck pressure (compute, parameter count) pushes representations toward minimal sufficient statistics; (3) shared inductive biases in deep networks (locality, smoothness, low-rank) further constrain the accessible representations. Empirical evidence: Kornblith et al. 2019 ("Similarity of Neural Network Representations") show CKA similarities between trained networks of different architectures can be very high; Huh et al. 2024 ("The Platonic Representation Hypothesis") find vision and language models develop increasingly similar internal representations as they scale.

Against: networks of similar capacity can implement provably different algorithms for the same task (Zhong et al. 2024 on modular arithmetic — multiple distinct algorithms found in different seeds); SAE features split with dictionary size (C.2); and many cognitive concepts (legal categories, social roles) plausibly don't satisfy natural-latent conditions. The honest reading: convergence is empirically present for some abstractions (physical/perceptual) and contested or absent for others (cultural/abstract). The NAH is a research program, not a settled fact.

Phenomena in practice: convergence measurements
Platonic Representation Hypothesis — Huh et al. 2024 (ICML oral)

Setup. Cross-modal alignment measured between vision models (DINOv2, CLIP variants, supervised ImageNet models — 78 vision models total) and language models (LLaMA family etc.) on paired image-caption datasets, primarily Wikipedia Image-Text (WIT). Metrics: mutual k-nearest-neighbor alignment + CKA.

Findings.

  • As both vision and language models scale, their kernel structures on paired data become more similar — linear relationship between LM capability and vision-LM alignment.
  • Headline number: max mutual-NN alignment ≈ 0.16 (out of theoretical max 1.0). Authors flag this themselves as modest — open question whether the gap is noise or structural divergence. Don't overstate.
  • CLIP models show notably higher alignment than DINO-trained vision models — explicit language supervision matters.
  • Alignment to DINOv2 correlates with downstream LM benchmarks: linear with Hellaswag, emergence-like trend with GSM8K.
  • Vision models with strong VTAB transfer cluster tightly in representation space; weak models scatter.
  • Alignment to DINOv2 decreases after fine-tuning on ImageNet classification — task specialization distorts the "Platonic" alignment.

The right read: real but modest evidence for convergence at scale, not a settled theorem. Cite with the 0.16 number to keep the claim honest.

CKA across networks — Kornblith et al. 2019

Setup. Comparison of CCA, linear regression, and a proposed similarity index equivalent to Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) across layers of CIFAR-trained networks (ResNets up to ResNet-62, wide variants, Plain-nets).

Findings.

  • Theoretical: CCA and any statistic invariant to invertible linear transformations cannot meaningfully compare representations whose dimensionality exceeds the number of datapoints. CKA is only invariant to orthogonal transforms + isotropic scaling, which is the right invariance for neural-network representation comparison.
  • Sanity check: CKA reliably matches corresponding layers across different random seeds of the same architecture. CCA fails this baseline — direct evidence CCA is the wrong tool.
  • Layers become more similar to each other and to wide networks as width increases; similarity of early layers saturates first.
  • Grid-pattern CKA heatmap for ResNet-62 reveals post-residual vs. block-interior structure — visualization of the architecture's compute organization that's not obvious from forward-pass diagrams alone.

CKA is now the standard "are these two networks computing similar things at corresponding depths" tool. The Platonic Hypothesis evidence rests on CKA + mutual-NN metrics.

Natural latents — Wentworth's worked examples

Framework recap. A natural latent satisfies mediation (parts of the system are independent given the latent) AND redundancy (the latent is fully recoverable from each part alone). When both hold, the latent is uniquely determined up to information equivalence (Wentworth's uniqueness theorem).

The temperature-of-gas example. Take two spatially separated mesoscopic chunks of an equilibrium ideal gas. The pair (pressure, temperature) mediates between their microstates — intensive quantities determine the joint distribution — AND is redundantly estimable from either chunk alone (each chunk's local statistics are sufficient for the intensive variables). Therefore (P, T) is the natural latent. Microstate is not (mediates but isn't redundant); local kinetic energy is partial but not quite redundant. This is the cleanest physics example because the thermodynamic limit makes both conditions hold sharply.

Coin-flip example. Many flips of a single biased coin: "bias of the coin" mediates between flips (conditionally independent given bias) AND is redundantly estimable (sufficient statistics across many flips). The individual outcomes are not natural latents — not redundant. Distinguishes "bottom-up latents" (individual variables) from "top-down latents" (shared parameters).

Where it fails. When mediation holds but redundancy fails: e.g., the full microstate of a gas mediates trivially but cannot be redundantly recovered from any single chunk. Wentworth's pointers problem: even when a natural latent exists in the world, an agent's internal pointer to it may not be uniquely determined — multiple representations of the same external latent can be equally consistent with the agent's data.

Worked example: temperature as a natural latent

Model a gas as N=104 molecules with velocities v1​,…,vN​∈R3, drawn i.i.d. from a Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution parameterized by temperature T. The latent Λ=T.

Mediation: I(vi​;vj​∣T)=0 exactly — the velocities are independent given T by construction. Distant molecules are conditionally independent given temperature.

Redundancy: H(T∣vi​) is small for any single vi​? Strictly, no — a single molecule's velocity is a weak estimator of T. But for any subregion of size k≫1 molecules, H(T∣vi1​​,…,vik​​)→0 as k→∞. The redundancy condition holds in the "any sufficiently large local view" sense, which is how Wentworth defines it for continuous systems.

Now consider two observers: observer A measures position-binned average kinetic energy in 100 spatial cells; observer B measures velocity-distribution moments (mean, variance) across the whole system. Both estimators converge to functions of T (kinetic energy =(3/2)kB​T; variance =kB​T/m). Despite measuring different things, both observers recover the same latent T up to deterministic transformation. The NAH prediction holds: the natural latent is unique, and two observers with different sensors converge on it.

Contrast: try to apply the same analysis to "social class" in a population of humans. Mediation fails — knowing class doesn't make distant individuals independent (they share other latents: economic conditions, geography). Redundancy fails — a single individual's observable features only weakly determine class. The concept doesn't satisfy NAH and isn't expected to converge across observers. This is consistent with the empirical observation that human cultures disagree about social-class taxonomies in a way they don't disagree about temperature.

  • Natural abstractions hypothesis: high-dim systems admit low-dim summaries Λ with mediation I(Xi​;Xj​∣Λ)≈0 and observer-uniqueness H(Λ∣Xi​)≈0.
  • Conant-Ashby good-regulator: every good regulator of a system must contain a model of that system — "controlling well requires modeling."
  • Tishby information bottleneck minI(X;T)−βI(T;Y) gives a constructive recipe for sufficient-and-minimal representations.
  • Locatello et al.'s impossibility theorem: disentanglement without inductive bias is fundamentally non-identifiable — so "natural" abstractions are natural relative to a prior, not absolute.
  • Convergence empirics (CKA, mutual-NN decoding, Huh Platonic Representation 2024) are consistent with natural abstractions, but also with multiple alternative causal stories; this is not yet pinned down.
  • For alignment: identifying the right abstraction is precisely the "pointers problem" — what lets you point at "human values" precisely enough to optimize toward.

Key definitions and results

Natural abstraction hypothesis
