For natural systems, there exist low-dimensional summaries sufficient for prediction-at-distance, which are recoverable from local views; observers converge on these summaries.
Mediation
I(Xi​;Xj​∣Λ)≈0 for distant variables; Λ screens off long-range correlations among the X's.
Redundancy
H(Λ∣Xi​)≈0 for any local view Xi​; Λ is recoverable from any sufficient subset of the system.
Natural latent uniqueness theorem
Two latents satisfying mediation and redundancy for the same distribution are isomorphic; "the" natural latent is well-defined up to relabeling.
Good regulator theorem
Conant-Ashby 1970: an optimal regulator of a stochastic system implements a function isomorphic to the system's dynamics; "controlling well requires modeling."
Information bottleneck
Tishby et al. 1999: optimal representations minimize I(X;T)−βI(T;Y); trace out a compression-relevance Pareto front.
Latent identifiability impossibility
Locatello et al. 2019: fully unsupervised disentanglement is impossible without inductive bias; identifiability requires structure beyond the data.
Pointers problem
Human values point at latent variables in human world-models, not raw physical states; transferring values requires a correspondence between AI's latents and human latents.
Condensation (Demski 2022)
Decomposition of a world model into discrete, causally-separable modules. Complements natural latents — predicts agreement on module boundaries, not just on summary variables.
Condensation agreement result
Agents organizing world models via condensation converge on approximately the same module partition; the discrete-structure analog of the natural-latent uniqueness theorem.

Retrieval practice

Q: Why is redundancy alone insufficient for a useful latent?
A: A latent that's recoverable from any local view but doesn't mediate — i.e., doesn't screen off distant variables — is a copy of local information, not an abstraction. Mediation is what makes it the "right level" of summary.
Q: How does the good regulator theorem relate to mech interp?
A: It predicts capable AI systems must carry isomorphic representations of the structures they competently model or control. Mech interp is the activity of recovering those representations. NAH is the further conjecture that the representations match the human-natural ones; the good regulator alone is silent on which ontology the AI uses.
Q: Why is "convergence of abstractions" a stronger claim than "good predictors exist"?
A: Many distinct representations can yield equally good predictions (factor rotations, basis changes). Convergence requires that learning dynamics + inductive biases select the same representation across architectures. This requires either NAH-style distribution-determined latents or strong shared inductive biases — both empirical claims.
Q: What does Locatello et al. 2019's impossibility result rule out?
A: It rules out fully unsupervised disentanglement without inductive bias: no algorithm, given only i.i.d. samples from a generative model, can recover the true latents better than chance over all generative models compatible with the data. It does NOT rule out identifiability when you add auxiliary information (labels, time, intervention).
Q: Connect natural latents to C.3's causal states.
A: Causal states are minimal sufficient statistics for predicting the future of a temporal process. Natural latents are minimal sufficient statistics with the additional redundancy constraint: recoverable from any local view, not just the full past. NAH adds the spatial-locality requirement that comp mech doesn't impose; comp mech is the temporal analog.
Q: Why might LLMs and human brains share more abstractions than two LLMs share with each other?
A: Because they share training data (humans wrote the internet) and prediction objective (the next-token distribution is structured by human-produced language). Two LLMs trained on the same data with similar inductive biases should converge more than LLM-vs-brain; empirically (Huh et al. 2024), this seems to hold, but with significant overlap in human-LLM representations too.

Problems

30 min · derivationProblem 1. Let X1​,X2​,X3​ be three noisy measurements of a common scalar μ: Xi​=μ+εi​, εi​∼N(0,σ2) iid. Show that μ is a natural latent: verify mediation and redundancy quantitatively. Then construct a different scenario where the structure looks similar but mediation fails, and identify why.
Solution sketch. Mediation: I(Xi​;Xj​∣μ)=0 because conditional on μ, the Xi​ are independent (the ε's are independent). Redundancy: H(μ∣Xi​) is finite (the Xi​ is a noisy estimate), but for any k local views, H(μ∣X1​,…,Xk​)∼log(σ/k​)→−∞ in the continuous-entropy sense, so redundancy holds in the local-region limit. Counter: let X1​,X2​,X3​ be measurements with correlated noise (εi​=μshared​+εi′​ for shared latent μshared​). Then I(Xi​;Xj​∣μ)=0 because the X's share information through μshared​. Mediation fails; μ alone is not the natural latent — (μ,μshared​) jointly is. Lesson: NAH conditions are sensitive to the joint structure, not just marginals.
15 min · scenarioProblem 2. Sketch an information-bottleneck argument for why a transformer trained on next-token prediction would develop representations approximating the causal states of the data-generating process. Where does the argument break down for non-stationary or non-ergodic data?
Solution sketch. Setup: input X = (past tokens), target Y = next token. IB minimizes I(X;T)−βI(T;Y). The minimal sufficient statistic for Y given X — for a stationary process — is precisely the causal state ϵ(X). Any representation T that achieves the same I(T;Y) as ϵ(X) must have I(X;T)≥I(X;ϵ(X)) by data-processing; equality only for T=ϵ(X) (or its image under a deterministic map). So IB-optimal predictors converge to causal-state representations. Breakdown: for non-stationary data, "the" causal state is time-dependent; the optimal representation must include time/context, and the simple ε-machine analysis doesn't apply. For non-ergodic data (multiple modes the process never bridges), the predictor's MSP includes mode identity as a permanent latent, and the natural-latent uniqueness theorem may yield multiple isomorphism classes.
15 min · scenarioProblem 3. Suppose two SAEs trained on the same model's residual stream extract different features (feature splitting from C.2). Argue whether this contradicts natural-latent uniqueness, or whether the two are consistent. What additional condition would have to hold for NAH to predict identical SAE features?
Solution sketch. No contradiction in principle. Natural-latent uniqueness says natural latents (satisfying mediation + redundancy) are isomorphic; it doesn't say all sparse decompositions are. SAE features at different dictionary sizes correspond to different resolution levels of representation, only some of which (if any) coincide with natural latents of the data distribution. For NAH to predict identical features, you'd need: (a) the SAE features are natural latents of the activation distribution (verifiable by checking mediation and redundancy on the SAE feature decomposition), and (b) both SAE training runs converge to the same Pareto-optimal point on the reconstruction-sparsity trade-off. Neither holds in current practice, which is why feature splitting is observed. This is the cleanest formal way to state why "SAE features = real features" is unproven.

Going deeper (optional)

If you read one thing: Wentworth, "Natural Latents: The Concepts" — the cleanest statement of mediation, redundancy, and the uniqueness theorem.

Optional depth: The Pointers Problem — alignment-side motivation for caring about shared latents; Condensation — alternative framing of modular conceptual structure; Iliad Abstractions hub — collected math refreshers and exercises; Exercise 1 — work mediation/redundancy on toy examples.

Solo assignment

Pick a concept you expect to be a natural latent (suggestions: object identity in vision, syntactic part-of-speech in language, market-clearing price in economics). Sketch a generative model where the concept appears, check whether mediation and redundancy hold formally or approximately, and identify the level of locality at which redundancy starts to fail. Then pick a concept you expect to be not natural (e.g., "fair price," "moral wrong," "art") and locate the failure mode in NAH terms. Deliver a 2-page memo contrasting the two. The point is to develop intuition for which concepts NAH applies to and which it doesn't — a load-bearing question for alignment.

D. Agency

Cluster D is the move from systems that predict or represent to systems that act: choosing sequences of actions under uncertainty to optimize a long-horizon objective. The four units traverse practical RL (what we actually train), idealized agency (what we wish we were training), embeddedness (why the idealization breaks), and world models (the cognitive machinery that closes the loop).

D.1 Reinforcement Learning MDPs, Bellman equations, policy gradients, PPO, GRPO, and RLHF as RL.
Prerequisites: Probability, stochastic processes, basic optimization, automatic differentiation, B.1 (transformer training), comfort with KL divergence and score-function estimators.

The whole edifice of practical RL — REINFORCE, A2C, PPO, GRPO, RLHF — is one trick (the policy-gradient theorem) plus a series of variance-reduction and trust-region patches. The score-function gradient ∇θ​J=E[∇θ​logπθ​(a∣s)Aπ(s,a)] is exact, but high-variance and unstable off-policy, so every modern algorithm is an answer to one of two questions: how do I estimate A with less variance (baselines, GAE, group-relative advantages), and how do I prevent a single update from collapsing the rollout distribution (KL trust regions, PPO clipping, KL-to-reference penalties). RLHF is just this same machinery with the reward function replaced by a Bradley-Terry-trained proxy, which is why a KL leash to πref​ is the load-bearing detail rather than an afterthought.

Pre-reading questions

  1. Why does the policy gradient theorem let you ignore ∇θ​dπ(s), the gradient of the state visitation distribution?
  2. What does PPO's clipping actually clip, and why is the clipped surrogate not a true lower bound on the policy improvement?
  3. GRPO removes the value network. What does it replace as a baseline, and where does that fail?
  4. RLHF on LLMs is contextual-bandit-shaped for most prompts. When does the multi-step MDP framing matter?
  5. Why is on-policy importance sampling unstable, and what hacks make it work in practice (clipping, KL penalties, trust regions)?

Core exposition

An MDP is a tuple (S,A,P,r,γ) with transition kernel P(s′∣s,a), reward r:S×A→R, and discount γ∈[0,1). A policy π(a∣s) induces a distribution over trajectories τ=(s0​,a0​,s1​,…). The return is Gt​=∑k≥0​γkr(st+k​,at+k​), and the objective is J(π)=Eτ∼π​[G0​].

Value functions and the Bellman equation

Define Vπ(s)=Eπ​[Gt​∣st​=s] and Qπ(s,a)=Eπ​[Gt​∣st​=s,at​=a]. One step of recursion gives the Bellman expectation equation:

Vπ(s)=Ea∼π(⋅∣s)​[r(s,a)+γEs′∼P(⋅∣s,a)​[Vπ(s′)]].

Equivalently Vπ=TπVπ where Tπ is the Bellman operator. Tπ is a γ-contraction in sup-norm, so policy evaluation by repeated application converges geometrically; this is the engine behind both DP and TD(0). The optimality equation V∗(s)=maxa​[r(s,a)+γE[V∗(s′)]] has a unique fixed point and an optimal deterministic policy attains it.

Policy gradient vs. value-based. Policy gradients optimize πθ​ directly via stochastic gradient ascent on J(πθ​); value-based methods (Q-learning, DQN, Rainbow) instead learn Q∗ via fixed-point iteration of the Bellman optimality operator and act greedily. Policy gradients handle continuous actions and stochastic policies natively but suffer from variance; Q-learning is sample-efficient and off-policy but unstable with function approximation (the "deadly triad": bootstrapping + off-policy + function approximation can diverge — see Baird's counterexample). Actor-critic methods straddle both: a critic supplies V or Q to reduce variance for the policy gradient.

Tabular RL background: SARSA, Q-learning, TD(λ). Before policy-gradient / actor-critic, the foundational fixed-point methods. SARSA (on-policy: Q(s,a)+=α[r+γQ(s′,a′)−Q(s,a)] using the action a′ the current policy actually takes) and Q-learning (off-policy: same update but replace Q(s′,a′) with maxa′​Q(s′,a′)) differ in which action they bootstrap from — SARSA's target value uses the behavior policy; Q-learning's uses the greedy policy. SARSA converges to the policy's actual value (so it accounts for exploration cost); Q-learning converges to the optimal Q∗ regardless of the behavior policy (so it's more sample-efficient if exploration is OK but can be unstable). TD(λ) interpolates between one-step TD (λ=0) and full Monte-Carlo (λ=1) via an eligibility-trace mechanism that propagates credit over recent states, controlling the bias-variance tradeoff on the TD-error backup. Modern LLM-RL skips most of this (it works in policy-gradient land with GAE for the variance-reduction role TD(λ) used to play), but the on-policy / off-policy distinction is the same one that surfaces in DPO vs PPO vs IQL.

Policy gradients

For parameterized πθ​, the policy gradient theorem (Sutton et al. 2000) gives

∇θ​J(θ)Aπ(s,a)​=Eτ∼πθ​​[t∑​∇θ​logπθ​(at​∣st​)Aπ(st​,at​)],=Qπ(s,a)−Vπ(s).​

REINFORCE → A2C → PPO → GRPO. Williams 1992 introduced REINFORCE (full Monte-Carlo return as the score-function multiplier; unbiased, ferocious variance). Sutton et al. 2000 formalized the policy-gradient theorem and the role of baselines. Mnih et al. 2016's A3C/A2C added a learned value critic for variance reduction and parallel rollouts. Schulman et al. 2015 introduced GAE (smoothly interpolating between TD(0) and Monte Carlo) and TRPO (KL-constrained trust region); 2017 simplified TRPO to PPO's clipped surrogate. Shao et al. 2024 (DeepSeek-Math) dropped the value critic for LLM post-training in favor of a group-relative baseline — sensible because most prompts are single-step bandits where a value function buys little. Each step is a variance-reduction or stability patch on the previous one, not a fundamentally new algorithm.

The key trick: although τ's distribution depends on θ through both the policy and the induced state visitation dπ, the ∇dπ terms collapse by a telescoping argument (the "Kakade trick" / performance difference lemma). Concretely, the visitation telescoping reads

∇θ​J(θ)=1−γ1​Es∼dπθ​,a∼πθ​​[∇θ​logπθ​(a∣s)Aπθ​(s,a)],

where the ∇θ​dπθ​ contribution to the naive product-rule expansion cancels because expected scores integrate to zero. Using A rather than raw returns gives a control variate that reduces variance without bias.

Actor-critic estimates A with a learned Vϕ​. GAE (Schulman et al. 2015) interpolates between low-bias / high-variance Monte-Carlo and high-bias / low-variance TD(0) via the TD residual δt​ and an exponential moving average:

δt​A^tGAE(γ,λ)​​=rt​+γVϕ​(st+1​)−Vϕ​(st​),=l=0∑∞​(γλ)lδt+l​.​

λ=1 is unbiased Monte Carlo minus the value baseline; λ=0 is the one-step TD residual. In practice λ≈0.95.

PPO

TRPO bounds the policy update by a KL trust region. PPO (Schulman et al. 2017) replaces the constrained problem with a clipped surrogate. Let rt​(θ)=πθ​(at​∣st​)/πθold​​(at​∣st​). Then

LCLIP(θ)=Et​[min(rt​(θ)A^t​,clip(rt​(θ),1−ϵ,1+ϵ)A^t​)].

The clipping is asymmetric: when A^t​>0 it caps how much the new policy can increase the action's probability; when A^t​<0 it caps how much it can decrease. The min means the loss is a pessimistic lower envelope — but it is not a true lower bound on policy improvement (Engstrom et al. 2020 documented how PPO's empirical performance depends on code-level details — value clipping, advantage normalization, orthogonal initialization — more than on the clipping itself).

PPO clipping is a heuristic, not a rigorous trust region. TRPO bounds the policy improvement by a KL constraint with a real monotonic improvement guarantee. PPO's clip drops the constraint and just clips the ratio, which has no analogous guarantee — the clipped objective can still produce arbitrarily-far updates in directions the clip doesn't reach (e.g., off-clip dimensions can compound across minibatches). Engstrom et al. 2020's "Implementation Matters in Deep RL" empirically isolated the clipped surrogate from the surrounding code-level tricks (advantage normalization, value-function clipping, learning rate annealing, reward scaling, orthogonal init) and found that most of PPO's improvement over vanilla policy gradient comes from the tricks, not the clipping itself. Treat PPO as "vanilla PG plus a bag of stabilizing engineering" rather than a principled trust-region method.

GRPO and the LLM-RL line

GRPO (Shao et al. 2024 / DeepSeek-Math; also DeepSeek-R1) drops the value network. For a prompt q, sample G completions {oi​}i=1G​, score each by a reward model or verifier ri​, and use the group-relative advantage

A^i​=std({rj​}j=1G​)ri​−mean({rj​}j=1G​)​.

Optimize the PPO-style clipped objective with this advantage and a KL penalty to a reference policy. This works on math/coding tasks because (a) most prompts are contextual bandits (single response, terminal reward), so a value function buys little; (b) verifiable rewards remove reward-model overfitting; (c) the group baseline is exactly E[r∣q] in expectation, so it's an unbiased baseline as long as samples are i.i.d. from πold​. Failure modes: when within-group reward variance collapses (all responses correct or all wrong) the gradient vanishes; off-policy drift between rollout and update degrades the importance ratio.

RLHF as RL, exploration, off-policy correction, reward shaping

RLHF on LLMs replaces verifier rewards with a learned rϕ​ trained on pairwise preferences (Bradley-Terry). Because rϕ​ is a proxy, you pay a KL tax β⋅DKL​(πθ​∥πref​) to bound the deviation — Goodhart pressure increases monotonically in the correlation gap between E[rϕ​] and rtrue​. Exploration in deep RL is mostly entropy bonuses, count-based / curiosity-driven intrinsic rewards (Bellemare 2016; Pathak 2017), or randomized priors. Off-policy correction uses importance ratios π(a∣s)/b(a∣s) for behavior b; pure IS has exploding variance for long horizons, so V-trace (Espeholt et al. 2018) and Retrace (Munos 2016) clip the per-step ratios. Reward shaping with F(s,s′)=γΦ(s′)−Φ(s) (Ng, Harada, Russell 1999) preserves the optimal policy because the cumulative shaping telescopes to γTΦ(sT​)−Φ(s0​); any other shaping form can change the optimum.

Worked example: REINFORCE on a two-state bandit-MDP

Take a one-state MDP, two actions a∈{0,1}, rewards r(0)=0, r(1)=1, policy πθ​(a=1)=σ(θ) with sigmoid σ. Then J(θ)=σ(θ)⋅1+(1−σ(θ))⋅0=σ(θ), and the closed-form gradient is ∇J=σ(θ)(1−σ(θ)). By the policy-gradient theorem with no baseline, ∇J=Ea​[∇logπθ​(a)⋅r(a)]. Compute: ∇logπθ​(1)=1−σ(θ), ∇logπθ​(0)=−σ(θ). So E[⋅]=σ(θ)(1−σ(θ))⋅1+(1−σ(θ))(−σ(θ))⋅0=σ(θ)(1−σ(θ)). Matches.

Now add a constant baseline b: E[∇logπ⋅(r−b)]=σ(θ)(1−σ(θ))(1−b)+(1−σ(θ))(−σ(θ))(−b)=σ(θ)(1−σ(θ)). The baseline cancels exactly because Ea​[∇logπθ​(a)]=∇Ea​[1]=0. This is why baselines reduce variance without bias — the score function has mean zero. Variance: with θ=0 (uniform) and b=0.5, per-sample gradient is ±0.25; with b=0, it is 0.5 or 0. Variance drops from 0.0625 to 0 at b=0.5=E[r], the variance-minimizing baseline.

  • The policy-gradient theorem ∇θ​J=Eτ​[∑t​∇θ​logπθ​(at​∣st​)Aπ(st​,at​)] is the score-function trick; subtracting a state-only baseline reduces variance without biasing the gradient.
  • GAE interpolates between TD(0) (high bias, low variance) and Monte Carlo (low bias, high variance) via A^tGAE​=∑l​(γλ)lδt+l​.
  • PPO clipping is a heuristic surrogate for a trust region; Engstrom et al. 2020 showed that the practical gains over vanilla actor-critic come largely from code-level tricks, not the clip itself.
  • GRPO normalizes advantages within a sampled group, eliminating the value model — the DeepSeek-R1 design that drove the recent reasoning-RL renaissance.
  • RLHF is RL: post-train via reward from a learned preference model + KL regularization to a reference policy. The closed-form maximizer π∗(a∣s)∝πref​(a∣s)exp(R/β) is the basis of DPO.

Key definitions and results

Markov decision process
Tuple (S,A,P,r,γ) with Markov transition kernel; policies map states to action distributions.
Bellman expectation operator
(TπV)(s)=Ea∼π​[r(s,a)+γEs′​[V(s′)]]. γ-contraction in ∥⋅∥∞​.
Policy gradient theorem
∇θ​J=E[∑t​∇θ​logπθ​(at​∣st​)Aπ(st​,at​)]; visitation-distribution gradient telescopes away.
GAE
Exponentially weighted advantage: A^t​=∑l​(γλ)lδt+l​. λ trades bias for variance.
PPO clipped objective
LCLIP=E[min(rt​(θ)A^t​, clip(rt​(θ),1−ϵ,1+ϵ)A^t​)].
GRPO advantage
Group-relative: A^i​=(ri​−μgroup​)/σgroup​; no learned value model.
Potential-based shaping
F(s,s′)=γΦ(s′)−Φ(s) preserves the optimal policy; other shaping does not (Ng, Harada, Russell 1999).
Performance difference lemma
J(π′)−J(π)=Es∼dπ′​[Aπ(s,π′(s))]/(1−γ); foundation of TRPO/PPO bounds.

Retrieval practice

Q: Prove the score-function baseline is unbiased.
A: Ea∼π​[∇θ​logπθ​(a∣s)⋅b(s)]=b(s)⋅∇θ​∑a​πθ​(a∣s)=b(s)⋅∇1=0. Any state-only baseline can be subtracted from the advantage without bias.
Q: Why is Q-learning off-policy?
A: The target r+γmaxa′​Q(s′,a′) uses the greedy policy w.r.t. current Q, not the behavior policy that generated (s,a,r,s′). So you can learn the optimal Q while exploring with ϵ-greedy.
Q: PPO's clip threshold ϵ is small (0.1–0.2). What goes wrong if you set ϵ=1?
A: Clipping becomes inactive for any reasonable ratio. The update becomes unconstrained importance-sampled vanilla policy gradient, and a single batch with high rt​ can move the policy arbitrarily far from πold​ — distribution shift kills the next iteration's samples.
Q: When does GRPO's advantage collapse?
A: When std(r) in the group is near zero — all completions get the same reward (all correct, all wrong, or all reward-model-scored similarly). The normalized advantage is undefined or noise-dominated; gradient vanishes. Mitigations: filter out zero-variance prompts or oversample.
Q: Why does RLHF need a KL penalty to the reference?
A: The reward model rϕ​ is fit on the on-distribution preference data. Off-distribution, it's a misspecified proxy. A KL trust region β⋅DKL​(πθ​∥πref​) keeps πθ​ on the manifold where rϕ​ generalizes. The fixed-point under this objective is exactly πθ​(y∣x)∝πref​(y∣x)exp(rϕ​(x,y)/β) — DPO's whole derivation.
Q: What does TD(0) approximate, and to what does it converge?
A: Sample-based stochastic fixed-point iteration of Tπ. Under Robbins-Monro step sizes and linear function approximation with on-policy sampling, converges to the projected Bellman fixed point (Tsitsiklis & Van Roy 1997). Off-policy linear TD can diverge — Baird's counterexample.
Q: REINFORCE vs actor-critic in one line.
A: REINFORCE uses full Monte-Carlo returns as the advantage estimator (unbiased, high variance); actor-critic plugs in r+γVϕ​(s′)−Vϕ​(s) (biased while Vϕ​ is learning, low variance). GAE smoothly interpolates.

Problems

30 min · derivationProblem 1. Show that the variance-minimizing constant baseline for REINFORCE is b∗=E[∥∇logπ(a∣s)∥2r]/E[∥∇logπ(a∣s)∥2], not E[r].
Solution sketch. Let g(a;b)=∇logπ(a)(r(a)−b). Var(g)=E[∥∇logπ∥2(r−b)2]−∥E[g]∥2; the second term is independent of b. Differentiate E[∥∇logπ∥2(r−b)2] in b: −2E[∥∇logπ∥2(r−b)]=0 gives b∗=E[∥∇logπ∥2r]/E[∥∇logπ∥2]. This is the score-weighted mean reward; only equals E[r] when ∥∇logπ∥2 is constant across actions (e.g. uniform binary policy).
30 min · derivationProblem 2. Derive GAE as the exponentially-weighted average of k-step advantage estimators A^t(k)​=∑i=0k−1​γirt+i​+γkV(st+k​)−V(st​).
Solution sketch. Define A^t​(λ)=(1−λ)∑k=1∞​λk−1A^t(k)​. Note A^t(k)​=∑i=0k−1​γiδt+i​ by telescoping (each V term cancels with the next). Substitute and swap sums: (1−λ)∑k≥1​λk−1∑i=0k−1​γiδt+i​=(1−λ)∑i≥0​γiδt+i​∑k>i​λk−1=(1−λ)∑i​γiδt+i​⋅λi/(1−λ)=∑i​(γλ)iδt+i​.
30 min · derivationProblem 3. Prove that for any potential Φ:S→R, the shaped MDP with r′(s,a,s′)=r(s,a)+γΦ(s′)−Φ(s) has the same optimal policy as the original.
Solution sketch. Let V′π be the shaped value. By the Bellman equation, V′π(s)=E[r+γΦ(s′)−Φ(s)+γV′π(s′)]. Define V~π(s)=V′π(s)+Φ(s). Then V~π(s)=E[r+γ(Φ(s′)+V′π(s′))]=E[r+γV~π(s′)], so V~π=Vπ. Hence V′π=Vπ−Φ, a policy-independent constant shift in π: argmaxπ​V′π=argmaxπ​Vπ.

Going deeper (optional)

If you read one thing: Sutton & Barto, Reinforcement Learning (2nd ed.) — chapters 3, 6, 13 cover MDPs, TD, and policy gradients with the right level of formality.

Optional depth: PPO (Schulman et al. 2017) — primary source for the clipped objective; DeepSeek-Math / GRPO — group-relative baseline derivation; Engstrom et al., Implementation Matters in Deep RL — sobering audit of what makes PPO work; RL102 (Raffin) — practical bridge to function approximation; State of RL for Reasoning LLMs (2026) — current map from classic RL to LLM post-training; ARENA RL chapter — hands-on PPO implementation.

Solo assignment

Implement REINFORCE, vanilla actor-critic, and PPO on the same gridworld (5×5 with walls and terminal rewards). Hold the network architecture and optimizer fixed. Plot return vs environment steps with confidence intervals over 5 seeds. Write a one-page note: where does each algorithm's variance / bias / sample-efficiency profile show up empirically? Then add a GRPO-style group-relative variant (sample G trajectories per starting state, normalize returns within the group, no critic) and explain why it does or doesn't help here. ~2–3 hours.

D.2 Idealised Agency AIXI, expected utility, Savage, coherence-theorem debates, logical induction, decision theory.
Prerequisites: D.1, Solomonoff induction / Kolmogorov complexity, measure-theoretic probability, basic recursion theory (computable / lower-semicomputable), preference orderings.

AIXI is the formally optimal agent — Bayesian Solomonoff prior over environments + expected utility maximization — and incomputable. Savage's representation theorem says any preferences satisfying axioms P1–P7 admit a unique probability + utility, motivating "rational agency = utility maximization," but the link to bounded ML systems is contested (Rohin Shah). Garrabrant's logical induction handles uncertainty about mathematical truths via a trader market that becomes calibrated against any efficiently computable strategy. Decision theories (CDT, EDT, FDT/UDT) disagree on Newcomb and twin-PD; the choice has structural implications for self-modifying agents — FDT/UDT one-box because "if my decision algorithm chose two-box, the predictor predicted it."

Pre-reading questions

  1. What exactly does AIXI optimize, and where does its incomputability enter — the prior, the search, or both?
  2. Savage's representation theorem gives you both probability and utility from preferences. Which axiom is doing each job?
  3. Yudkowsky and Rohin Shah disagree about whether coherence theorems imply AGI will be a utility maximizer. State each side precisely.
  4. What does logical induction guarantee that ordinary Bayesianism doesn't, and what is the price?
  5. In Newcomb's problem, what does FDT prescribe that CDT and EDT do not — and why?

Core exposition

AIXI

AIXI (Hutter 2000, 2005) is the formal limit of "Bayesian reinforcement learner with a universal prior." Let \(ax_{

\[ \begin{aligned} a_t = \arg\max_{a_t} \sum_{o_t r_t} \cdots \max_{a_m} \sum_{o_m r_m}\, &(r_t + \cdots + r_m) \\ \cdot\ &\sum_{\substack{q \in Q \\ U(q,\, ax_{

Equivalently, AIXI does Bayesian RL over the Solomonoff universal mixture \(\displaystyle \xi(o \mid ax_{Properties: (i) Solomonoff dominance — for any lower-semicomputable environment μ there is a constant cμ​ with ξ(x)≥cμ​⋅μ(x) for all x. (ii) Self-optimizing in "ergodic" environments. (iii) Incomputable: the mixture is only lower-semicomputable, the expectimax tree is infinite, and dominance constants are environment-specific (no uniform "Pareto-optimal" property — Leike & Hutter 2015 showed AIXI is not Pareto-optimal across all environments simultaneously). AIXI matters not because you'd run it, but because it crisply separates "agency" into a prior + a value function + a planner, lets you ask which alignment problems live in which component, and reveals which ones (e.g., self-modeling) are excluded by construction.

AIXI vs AIXItl vs computable approximations. AIXI proper uses the full Solomonoff prior ξ∝∑q​2−ℓ(q), which is only lower-semicomputable and runs an infinite expectimax — so it can't be implemented. AIXItl (Hutter 2005) restricts to programs of length ≤l and runtime ≤t; this is computable but its runtime is t⋅2l, still wildly infeasible, and it loses Solomonoff dominance (only programs in the bounded class are weighed). Practical approximations like MC-AIXI-CTW use context-tree weighting as a finite-mixture prior plus UCT-style planning — these are runnable, achieve nontrivial performance on small POMDPs (Veness et al. 2011), but the theoretical guarantees of AIXI no longer apply. The takeaway: AIXI is a conceptual scaffolding (prior + value + planner) for asking which component contains a problem, not a target to implement.

Expected utility and Savage

von Neumann-Morgenstern takes lotteries (probabilities given) and shows that preferences satisfying completeness, transitivity, continuity, and independence are represented by an expected-utility ordering:

L1​⪯L2​⟺EL1​​[u]≤EL2​​[u],u unique up to positive affine.

Savage's theorem (1954) is stronger: it takes only preferences over acts (functions from states to outcomes), no probabilities given, and from axioms P1–P7 (P1 ordering; P2 sure-thing principle; P3 state-independent preferences; P4 likelihood comparability; P5 nontriviality; P6 non-atomicity; P7 dominance) derives a unique probability measure P on states and a utility u on outcomes such that

f⪯g⟺EP​[u∘f]≤EP​[u∘g].

The work is split: P1–P3 give ordering and state-independence; P4 + P6 pin down probabilities (P4 lets you compare event likelihoods, P6 ensures a sufficiently rich state space for an Archimedean property); P5 + P7 handle nontriviality and dominance over infinite states.

Coherence theorems and the debate

Money-pump and Dutch-book arguments show that any agent whose preferences violate transitivity, completeness, or independence can be exploited — converted to a series of trades each of which it accepts but whose composition is a strict loss. Yudkowsky's coherence claim: sufficiently capable agents will be approximate expected-utility maximizers because any systematic deviation is a free-energy gradient that selection (training or self-improvement) will exploit. Rohin Shah's critique (and Richard Ngo, Stuart Armstrong in related forms): coherence theorems assume preferences over a specific outcome space; if you carve outcomes finely enough, any behavior is consistent with some utility function, so the theorem is vacuous as a prediction. What matters is whether agents have simple, cross-situationally stable, action-relevant utility functions — and that is an empirical question, not implied by money-pump arguments. The honest middle position: coherence pressure exists but is weak, partial, and only over the dimensions the environment actually trades along.

"Coherence ⇒ utility maximization" is contested. The standard Yudkowsky version: any sufficiently capable agent must approximate an expected-utility maximizer (over some outcome space) or it can be money-pumped. The contested premise is the existence of a fixed, finite, environment-relevant outcome space that the agent has stable preferences over. Rohin Shah's critique (LessWrong 2018): if you carve outcomes finely enough — e.g., distinguishing "$10 received via process A" from "$10 received via process B" — every behavior is consistent with some utility function, making the theorem vacuous. The empirical question is whether the utility function is simple, action-relevant, and stable across distribution shift; coherence theorems do not by themselves imply any of these. Treat "coherent ⇒ utility maximizer" as a hypothesis about training dynamics under selection pressure, not a theorem.

Logical induction

Bayesianism conditions on empirical propositions but treats logical/mathematical facts as already known with probability 0 or 1. This is the problem of logical uncertainty: a bounded agent can't compute the 10100-th digit of π in advance, but Bayes gives no coherent way to have probability ≈0.1 for "that digit is 3" updating to certainty when the computation completes. Garrabrant et al. (2016) logical induction: a market {Pn​} of probability assignments to sentences φ in a formal language. Traders are efficiently-computable strategies that buy and sell shares paying 1 if φ is provable. A logical inductor is a sequence of market prices Pn​(φ) such that no efficiently computable trader has unboundedly increasing wealth against it. The main theorem: such markets exist (one is explicitly constructed), and they satisfy:

  • Calibration: empirical frequency of provability tracks Pn​.
  • Limit coherence: P∞​(φ)=1 for theorems, 0 for contradictions.
  • Self-trust over time: the inductor approximately trusts its future beliefs (in a sense that escapes Löb).
  • Provability induction, learning of statistical patterns in mathematical facts.

Price: convergence is asymptotic with no rate, and the construction is wildly infeasible. The point is conceptual: there is some coherent treatment of logical uncertainty that avoids the obvious failure modes.

Decision theory

Newcomb's problem. A predictor with 99% accuracy fills box B with $1M if it predicts you take only B, $0 if it predicts you take both. Box A always contains $1K. Should you one-box or two-box?

CDT vs EDT vs FDT/UDT. The three differ in what they condition on when computing expected utility. CDT conditions on a causal intervention do(a) — "what would happen if I were to take action a, holding everything causally upstream fixed." EDT conditions on the observation "I took action a" — uses P(⋅∣a), allowing the decision to be evidence about the world. FDT/UDT conditions on the logical/algorithmic fact "my decision algorithm outputs a" — every instantiation of the algorithm (you, the predictor's simulation, your future selves) outputs the same thing, so the conditioning shifts every co-instantiated variable. The wedge: CDT and FDT agree where the action has only causal effects; EDT and FDT agree where the correlation is via prediction of you (Newcomb); only FDT gets both Newcomb and smoking-lesion right.

  1. Set up the four leaves and the predictor's conditional probabilities. Predictor is 99% accurate, so P(box B has $1M∣you one-box)=0.99 and P(box B has $1M∣you two-box)=0.01. The four leaf payoffs (in $K): one-box × B=1M → 1000; one-box × B=0 → 0; two-box × B=1M → 1001; two-box × B=0 → 1.

    All three decision theories agree on the payoff matrix and the predictor's accuracy. They disagree on which conditional probability is the right one to use.

  2. ECDT​[u∣a]=s∑​P(s)u(a,s),P(s) is the unconditional prior.

    CDT uses the marginal prior P(B=$1M), not the conditional. The contents are already fixed when you choose; your decision causally does not affect them. With a flat prior P(B=$1M)=0.5: ECDT​[one-box]=0.5(1000)+0.5(0)=500; ECDT​[two-box]=0.5(1001)+0.5(1)=501. CDT picks two-box by $1K. (With any prior, two-box dominates one-box by exactly $1K — that's the causally-extra contents of A.)

  3. EEDT​[u∣a]=s∑​P(s∣a)u(a,s).

    EDT uses the conditional P(s∣a). Your decision is evidence about which prediction the predictor made. EEDT​[one-box]=0.99(1000)+0.01(0)=990; EEDT​[two-box]=0.01(1001)+0.99(1)=11. EDT picks one-box by $979K.

  4. EFDT​[u∣a]=s∑​P(s∣algorithm outputs a)u(a,s).

    FDT/UDT conditions on "my decision algorithm outputs a" — this fact also determines what the predictor (which simulated your algorithm) wrote in box B. So P(B=$1M∣algorithm=one-box)=0.99 numerically matches EDT's conditional, but for a different reason: logical co-dependence, not evidential update. FDT picks one-box.

  5. Smoking-lesion separates EDT from FDT. Lesion L causes both cancer C and a smoking-disposition; smoking S is harmless conditional on L. EDT computes P(C∣S)>P(C∣¬S) — observing yourself smoking is evidence of the lesion — so EDT abstains, losing the enjoyment of smoking even though smoking is causally harmless. FDT conditions on "my algorithm outputs smoke." This logical fact doesn't shift P(L) because the lesion was determined before the algorithm existed (lesion-distribution is a fixed parent in the causal graph). So FDT computes P(C∣algo=S)=P(C) unchanged, and smokes. CDT also smokes (causal independence). EDT is the outlier, and the failure exposes that pure evidential updating confuses correlation with causation.

    FDT recovers CDT's correct call on smoking-lesion and EDT's correct call on Newcomb by conditioning on the logical fact about the algorithm rather than on either causal intervention or evidential observation alone.

Smoking lesion. A genetic lesion both causes lung cancer and makes you want to smoke; smoking itself is harmless. You enjoy smoking. Should you smoke?

  • CDT: smoke. Smoking has no causal effect on cancer; the only causal effect is on enjoyment.
  • EDT: don't smoke. P(cancer∣smoke)>P(cancer∣don’t smoke) because smoking is evidence of the lesion. EDT gets this wrong — it confuses correlation with causation.
  • FDT: smoke. The lesion fixes whether you have the cancer-causing genotype and the decision-algorithm-disposition jointly; logical dependence is what matters, and conditional on your decision algorithm being instantiated, your choice doesn't shift the lesion distribution.

The pattern: EDT does the right thing on Newcomb (where the correlation is via prediction of your decision) and the wrong thing on smoking-lesion (where the correlation is via a common cause). CDT is the mirror image. FDT/UDT gets both right by conditioning on logical/algorithmic dependencies rather than causal or evidential ones. Open problems remain: counterfactual mugging, logical counterfactuals, anthropic / Sleeping Beauty cases.

Worked example: AIXI on a 2-armed Bernoulli bandit

Take two arms, true means μ1​=0.7, μ2​=0.3, unknown to the agent. Place a Bayesian prior over arm means (e.g., Beta(1,1) on each). A computable AIXI-like agent picks at​=argmaxa​Eμ∼posterior​[Qμ​(a)], where Qμ​(a) is the expected discounted return under environment μ. For a finite horizon this is Gittins-index optimal. Now make the environment class richer: not just i.i.d. Bernoulli but all lower-semicomputable distributions over reward sequences. AIXI's prior ξ weights every program q by 2−ℓ(q).

Where does incomputability bite? (a) Enumerating Q is uncomputable; (b) deciding whether U(q,history)=observed requires running q for arbitrarily long; (c) the expectimax over the infinite future is infinite. AIXItl restricts programs to length ≤l and time ≤t — recovering computability at the cost of dominance. What does AIXI tell us about alignment? The reward channel is exogenous, fed in by the environment. AIXI has no model of itself, no "drives" beyond the reward, and no notion that it is one of the programs in Q. All four are problems D.3 inherits.

  • AIXI is formally optimal under a Solomonoff prior over environments; it is incomputable (the prior is only lower-semicomputable), and the universal-Turing-machine choice changes finite-time behavior up to a constant.
  • Savage's representation theorem (P1–P7) gives a unique probability + utility from preferences — the load-bearing axioms are completeness/transitivity (probability) and the sure-thing principle (utility additivity).
  • The coherence-theorems-imply-utility argument (Yudkowsky) is contested: bounded agents need not act like utility maximizers, and the "money pump" arguments assume the agent will accept Dutch books (Rohin Shah's critique).
  • Logical induction: a market of efficiently-computable traders becomes calibrated against any efficiently computable strategy, giving a constructive treatment of logical uncertainty.
  • Newcomb's problem cleanly separates decision theories: CDT two-boxes (causal independence), EDT and FDT/UDT one-box for different reasons; smoking lesion separates EDT from FDT (EDT one-boxes, FDT smokes).

Key definitions and results

Solomonoff prior
M(x)=∑q:U(q) starts with x​2−ℓ(q). Lower-semicomputable; dominates every lower-semicomputable semimeasure up to a multiplicative constant.
AIXI
Bayesian expectimax agent under Solomonoff mixture; incomputable; not Pareto-optimal across environments (Leike & Hutter 2015).
vNM utility
Preferences over lotteries satisfying completeness, transitivity, continuity, independence ⇒ expected-utility representation unique up to positive affine.
Savage's theorem
Preferences over acts satisfying P1–P7 ⇒ unique probability P on states and utility u on outcomes such that act-preferences = EP​[u] ordering.
Money-pump
Intransitive or independence-violating preferences ⇒ a sequence of pairwise-preferred trades whose composition strictly loses; "coherence pressure."
Logical inductor
Sequence of probability assignments on a formal language such that no efficiently computable trader earns unbounded wealth against it (Garrabrant et al. 2016). Calibrated, limit-coherent, satisfies provability induction.
CDT
Maximize ∑s​P(s)u(a,s): outcomes via causal intervention do(a). Two-boxes Newcomb.
EDT
Maximize E[u∣a]: outcomes via conditioning. One-boxes Newcomb, doesn't smoke under smoking-lesion.
FDT/UDT
Maximize E[u∣output of your decision algorithm is a]: optimize over policies / algorithms, not actions. One-boxes Newcomb, smokes under smoking-lesion.

Retrieval practice

Q: Why isn't AIXI Pareto-optimal?
A: For any environment μ, AIXI's reward is within a constant of optimal — but the constant depends on μ. Leike & Hutter (2015) construct environment pairs where AIXI is suboptimal in one and another agent dominates it, while AIXI dominates that agent elsewhere. No agent simultaneously dominates AIXI on all environments, but AIXI also doesn't simultaneously dominate every alternative.
Q: Yudkowsky says coherence ⇒ utility maximization. Steelman the counterargument in one paragraph.
A: Money-pump arguments require an exploiter, an outcome space the agent has consistent preferences over, and trades the environment actually offers. If the agent only faces a narrow slice of action-space, partial coherence on that slice is consistent with arbitrary preferences elsewhere. More importantly, by re-individuating outcomes (e.g., "$10 received via process A" = "$10 received via process B"), almost any behavior is rationalizable as utility maximization, making the prediction vacuous unless you separately argue the utility function is simple.
Q: Logical induction guarantees no efficiently computable trader earns unbounded wealth. Why is "efficiently computable" the load-bearing word?
A: Against an arbitrary (uncomputable or super-polynomial) trader, no market can guarantee non-exploitation — the trader could just be the halting oracle. Restricting to efficiently computable traders bounds how much computational advantage the adversary has, which is exactly what makes the construction feasible and the guarantee meaningful for bounded agents.
Q: How does FDT handle counterfactual mugging?
A: Omega flips a coin; if heads, asks for $100; if tails, would have given $10K iff you'd pay on heads. CDT/EDT, conditioned on observing heads, refuse — the $10K is already lost. FDT/UDT optimizes ex ante over policies "pay" vs "refuse," sees that "pay" has expected value 0.5⋅(−100)+0.5⋅10000=4950, and pays.
Q: What axiom of Savage fails for an agent with state-dependent utility (e.g., I prefer umbrella over sunglasses if it rains)?
A: P3 — preferences over consequences are required to be state-independent. State-dependence breaks the identification of probability and utility; you can rescale u per state and absorb the change into P, so they're no longer uniquely identified.
Q: Why is the Solomonoff prior lower-semicomputable rather than computable?
A: For each program you can computably enumerate halting-and-producing-x events, summing 2−ℓ(q) for each as it happens — the partial sums are a non-decreasing computable sequence converging to M(x). But you can't decide in finite time whether more contributions will arrive (halting problem), so you can lower-bound but not exactly compute it.

Problems

15 min · scenarioProblem 1. Construct a 3-outcome lottery example where an agent violating the vNM independence axiom can be money-pumped. Quantify the loss per cycle.
Solution sketch. Allais-style. Outcomes: A = $1M sure; B = (0.10: $5M, 0.89: $1M, 0.01: $0); C = (0.11: $1M, 0.89: $0); D = (0.10: $5M, 0.90: $0). Many agents prefer A ≻ B and D ≻ C, which violates independence (A and B mix the same way with C and D under common consequence). Money-pump: agent owns A; trade A → B −ϵ (small payment), since B ≻ A would imply this anyway in the violating direction (in the Allais form, A ≻ B but agent reveals B-style risk-acceptance when (A,B) replaced by (C,D) — so construct a sequence of swaps between A↔B-mix and C↔D-mix that each agent accepts and that returns to starting outcome with net payment >0). Per-cycle loss is the sum of the small ϵ's the agent pays at each strictly preferred swap.
30 min · derivationProblem 2. Prove that Solomonoff's universal mixture M dominates every lower-semicomputable semimeasure μ up to a multiplicative constant: ∃cμ​>0. ∀x. M(x)≥cμ​μ(x).
Solution sketch. Since μ is lower-semicomputable, there is a program qμ​ that, given x, enumerates rationals converging up to μ(x). By a coding argument, there is a universal monotone machine U such that the set of programs q with prefix qμ​ outputting x contributes at least 2−ℓ(qμ​)⋅μ(x) to M(x)=∑q:U(q)⊒x​2−ℓ(q). Take cμ​=2−ℓ(qμ​). (Full proof: Li & Vitányi Ch. 4.)
15 min · scenarioProblem 3. Twin Prisoner's Dilemma: you and an exact copy of you play one-shot PD; defect strictly dominates cooperate, but you're identical. What do CDT, EDT, FDT prescribe, and what does each get as payoff (T=5, R=3, P=1, S=0)?
Solution sketch. CDT: defect. Your decision causally doesn't affect the twin's. Expected payoff against a defecting twin = P = 1. EDT: cooperate. P(twin cooperates∣you cooperate)=1 by symmetry, so E[u∣C]=3>E[u∣D]=1. Payoff = 3. FDT: cooperate. Your decision and your twin's are outputs of the same algorithm; conditional on the algorithm outputting C, both cooperate. Payoff = 3. The CDT defection is the classic problem — CDT-agents leave $2 each on the table against logical copies.

Going deeper (optional)

If you read one thing: Garrabrant et al., Logical Induction (2016) — the cleanest single artifact of agent-foundations math; sections 1–3 give the construction and main theorems without the heavy machinery.

Optional depth: Hutter, Introduction to Universal Artificial Intelligence (2024) — current AIXI textbook; Leike & Hutter, Bad Universal Priors and Notions of Optimality — Pareto-optimality counterexample; Yudkowsky, Coherent Decisions Imply Consistent Utilities — the strong coherence position; Shah, Coherence Arguments Do Not Entail Goal-Directed Behavior — the critique; Yudkowsky & Soares, Functional Decision Theory — FDT primary source; Value Under Ignorance in UAI (2025) — recent UAI utility-function extension.

Solo assignment

Pick one of: (a) write a 1500-word essay relating AIXI's components (prior, value, planner) to specific alignment failure modes, identifying which problems each component contains and which it excludes by construction; or (b) implement a toy logical-inductor-style market on a small set of propositions (e.g., digits of a slow-to-compute constant) with a half-dozen traders, run it for 104 steps, and plot calibration vs. ground truth as proofs come in. Either way, end with one paragraph on what an embedded version of AIXI/the inductor would need that the idealized version doesn't have.

D.3 Agent Foundations Embedded agency's four obstacles, Vingean reflection, Löb, tiling, ontological crises, MIRI agenda.
Prerequisites: D.1, D.2 (especially Savage and decision theory), Gödel-incompleteness and Löb's theorem at the statement level, computable / lower-semicomputable distinctions, basic modal logic.

Embedded agents (Demski–Garrabrant) face four obstacles a Cartesian agent doesn't: no clean self/environment split (decision theory), embedded world models (you are part of what you model), robust delegation (creating successors that preserve your goals), and subsystem alignment (your own cognition doesn't subvert you). Löb's theorem gives an obstacle to self-trust: an agent can't prove its successor's reasoning sound without violating provability. The MIRI agenda has not produced empirically load-bearing results recently — flag this honestly — but the obstacles remain real constraints on any sufficiently self-modifying agent, and logical induction (D.2) sidesteps Löb by giving up perfect self-trust in exchange for asymptotic calibration.

Pre-reading questions

  1. What is the precise difference between a Cartesian-dualist agent and an embedded agent, and why does it matter for self-modeling?
  2. State Löb's theorem and explain how it obstructs an agent from trusting its successor's reasoning.
  3. What is an ontological crisis, and why is it not solved by re-fitting your utility function?
  4. Name the four Demski-Garrabrant obstacles to embedded agency and one concrete failure each.
  5. Critique honestly: has the MIRI agent-foundations agenda produced empirically load-bearing alignment results? What's the steelman for keeping at it?

Core exposition

Cluster D.2 modeled agents Cartesianly: a clean boundary between agent and environment, the agent strictly larger in some sense (it can hold the whole environment in its prior), the environment opaque to the agent except via observations. Embedded agency drops this. The agent is a piece of the environment: smaller than it (can't store the world's state, including itself), observed by it, modified by it, potentially copied and run in parallel. Demski & Garrabrant 2018 ("Embedded Agency") organize the consequences into four obstacles:

Origins. Embedded-agency framing crystallized in Demski & Garrabrant's 2018 LessWrong sequence "Embedded Agency," but the constituent obstacles were articulated earlier: Vinge (1993) on the prediction-horizon for smarter successors ("Vingean reflection"); Yudkowsky & Herreshoff (2013) on the Löbian obstacle for tiling agents; de Blanc (2011) on ontological crises; the broader "agent foundations" research program at MIRI active roughly 2014–2020. Modern presentations (e.g., the 2020+ arXiv versions, Tom Everitt's thesis) reorganize these as four "obstacles" rather than as a collection of theorems — a teaching choice that makes the conceptual structure visible even if the underlying mathematics existed beforehand.

Obstacle 1: Embedded decision theory

AIXI's expectimax assumes you can clearly separate "the action I take" from "the world's response." Embedded, there is no such split — the agent's deliberation is itself part of the world the agent is reasoning about. Counterfactuals over "what if I'd done a′" are ill-defined: the laws of physics that produced your actual choice would produce it again. The smoking-lesion / Newcomb suite carries over but with a twist: you and the predictor share substrate. Open problems: logical counterfactuals (what does it mean for "1+1=3 but everything else unchanged"?), choosing between policies when your choice is part of the policy.

Obstacle 2: Embedded world models

A Cartesian agent has a hypothesis space large enough to contain the true environment. If the agent is inside the environment, its world model must include itself, including its world model — strict containment is impossible (cardinality argument; Diagonalization argument: a model of self that's exact has to contain itself, contradicting size). Resolution must be approximate: hierarchical / coarse-grained self-models, plus a treatment of which features of self the agent does and doesn't model. Connects to ontological crises: if the agent's utility function refers to objects (e.g., "diamonds," "humans," "happiness") in one ontology and it updates to a finer ontology (e.g., physics), how does the utility function transport? There is no canonical answer — the same surface preference admits multiple extensions (de Blanc 2011; Demski).

Obstacle 3: Robust delegation (tiling agents and Vingean reflection)

An agent that self-modifies, builds successors, or trains a copy must trust that the successor will pursue the same goals. This is hard for two reasons. Vingean reflection (after Vinge's "you can't predict what something smarter than you will do"): you can't simulate the successor's exact reasoning. You'd like a guarantee of the form "Successor will only act if its reasoning is sound" — call this □Sound(Successor). Löb's theorem: in any sufficiently strong consistent theory T,

T⊢(□T​φ→φ)⟹T⊢φ.

Applied to φ=Sound(T): you can't prove "if my theory proves it's sound then it is" without already proving it's sound — and you can't prove your own soundness (Gödel's second theorem). So an agent reasoning in T can't prove that any successor reasoning in T is sound. This is the Löbian obstacle (Yudkowsky & Herreshoff 2013). Partial workarounds: hierarchies T0​⊊T1​⊊… where each level trusts the next (loses uniform trust); reflective oracles (Fallenstein et al.); model-polymorphic tiling (Yudkowsky & Christiano). Tiling agents are agents that can build successors implementing the same decision-making schema while preserving goal alignment — solving the Löbian obstacle is the gate.

The parametric-theory workaround formalizes as a hierarchy of theories T0​⊊T1​⊊⋯ where Tk​ proves the soundness of provability in Tk+1​ but never its own:

Tk​⊢∀φ.(□Tk+1​​φ→φ),k=0,1,2,…

Each level trusts the next (so an agent reasoning in Tk​ can prove its Tk+1​-successor's reasoning sound), but no level proves its own soundness. Costs: uniform self-trust is gone; the agent has finite reflective depth; one must specify in advance how deeply to delegate.

Is logical induction a complete answer to logical uncertainty? No, and Garrabrant et al. are explicit. Logical induction gives asymptotic calibration (P∞​(φ)∈{0,1} for decidable sentences) but no convergence rate — at any finite stage, the inductor's probabilities can be wildly off, and the choice of trader class controls what gets calibrated quickly versus slowly. It doesn't solve the algorithmic question (which proofs to try, in what order) — only the epistemological one (what coherent probabilities to assign mid-computation). For practical alignment, this gives a framework for talking about graded mathematical belief; it does not yield an efficiently-computable agent that handles logical uncertainty well in bounded time. The follow-up agenda (open-source game theory, Cartesian frames, finite factored sets) has explored richer settings but with similar "interesting math, no engineering hook" status.

Obstacle 4: Subsystem alignment

A capable agent has substructure — modules, subagents, search procedures, optimizers running inside. Mesa-optimization (B / C clusters) is the empirical face of this: the inner search may have objectives that diverge from the outer training objective. The agent-foundations framing: a unified-utility agent containing a search subsystem with its own implicit objective can fail at goal-preservation even with no external adversary. Selection theorems try to characterize when this happens — when does an optimizer "naturally" develop an inner mesa- objective, and what determines its content?

The MIRI agenda and its critique

MIRI's agent-foundations agenda (active ~2014–2020, dormant since 2021 announcement) attacked these via mathematical proxy problems: logical induction, decision theory, tiling, functional decision theory, reflective oracles, Cartesian frames, finite factored sets. Honest critique: the agenda produced beautiful mathematics (logical induction in particular is real progress on logical uncertainty) but has not yielded alignment techniques that show up in a frontier-lab training pipeline. None of the empirical interpretability or RLHF/RLAIF advances are downstream of agent foundations. MIRI itself announced in 2021 that they were stepping back from this agenda and shifting focus to governance and broad communication. Steelman: pre-paradigmatic clarity work rarely shows up in the immediate engineering pipeline; logical induction took 5 years to publish and the field is <10 years old; many of the obstacles (embedded world models, robust delegation) are exactly what becomes load-bearing for AGI-scale agents, and empirical alignment is currently silent on them. The right calibration: agent foundations is high-variance, low-mean-recent-impact, and possibly high-value-conditional-on-AGI-being- agentic. Spending some-but-not-most of the field's attention on it is reasonable.

Don't conflate "theoretically deep" with "empirically load-bearing." The MIRI agent-foundations agenda has been running for over a decade and produced beautiful mathematics — logical induction, FDT, reflective oracles, Cartesian frames, finite factored sets — yet none of it appears in any frontier-lab alignment pipeline as of 2026. None of RLHF, RLAIF, constitutional AI, debate, weak-to-strong supervision, SAE-based interpretability, or the recent reasoning-RL line traces its lineage to agent foundations. MIRI itself acknowledged this in their 2021 strategy update and pivoted toward governance/communication. The lesson is not "agent foundations is wrong" — it is "the obstacles it identifies are real, but the route from beautiful proxy mathematics to working alignment engineering is much longer than the original agenda anticipated." Read for understanding the structural problems agentic AGI will face; don't expect the math to directly become an alignment technique.

Worked example: the Löbian obstacle, concretely

Suppose agent A uses theory T (say, Peano Arithmetic + some decision-theoretic axioms) and wants to delegate to successor A′ that also uses T. A reasons: "I'll let A′ execute action a if A′ can prove in T that a achieves goal G." For A to itself prove that delegating is good, it needs T⊢(∀a. □T​"a achieves G"→a achieves G). The premise □T​"a achieves G"→a achieves G says T is sound about G-claims. By Löb: if T⊢(□T​φ→φ) for any φ, then T⊢φ. So if A can prove this soundness, it can prove "a achieves G" outright — for any a! — which would make T inconsistent. Therefore A cannot prove soundness, hence cannot prove delegation is goal-preserving. Yudkowsky & Herreshoff propose a workaround via parametric theories Tk​ that trust Tk+1​: a finite hierarchy avoids the self-referential trap but costs you uniform self-trust. Note: this is a problem only if you insist on formal proof; a probabilistic notion of trust (logical induction style — "P(Sound)≈1 but not =1") sidesteps the Löb trap because Löb is about provability, not probability. Concretely, Christiano's probabilistic reflection schema:

∀φ.P(P(φ)∈[a,b])=1⟹P(φ)∈[a−ϵ,b+ϵ],

for some small ϵ>0. This is consistent (no Löb-style obstruction) because the inner and outer P commute up to ϵ, unlike the modal box where commutativity gives T⊢□φ→φ and triggers the theorem. Probabilistic self-trust is the price you pay: never quite P(Sound)=1, but in exchange you get a consistent reflective agent.

  • Embedded agency has four obstacles a Cartesian agent doesn't: decision theory (no clean self/env split), embedded world models (you are part of what you model), robust delegation (creating successors that preserve goals), subsystem alignment (your own cognition doesn't subvert you).
  • Löb's theorem blocks formal self-trust: an agent can't prove its successor's reasoning sound without violating consistency. The escape route is to abandon perfect self-trust — logical induction's probabilistic trust is the constructive alternative.
  • Tiling agents (Yudkowsky-Herreshoff parametric Tk​) avoid Löb via a hierarchy of theories trusting the next, at the cost of uniform self-trust.
  • Ontological crises: when an agent's utility is defined over an ontology that turns out wrong, transporting utility to the corrected ontology is underdetermined.
  • The MIRI agenda has not produced empirically load-bearing results in recent years; the obstacles, however, are real constraints any sufficiently self-modifying agent will eventually hit.

Key definitions and results

Embedded agent
An agent contained in its environment; smaller than the world, observed and modifiable by it. Demski & Garrabrant 2018.
Cartesian agent
Agent with a clean agent/environment boundary; environment encoded as an external object the agent can hold in hypothesis space.
Löb's theorem
For sufficiently strong consistent T: T⊢(□T​φ→φ)⇒T⊢φ. Equivalently, T proves no nontrivial reflection principle.
Löbian obstacle
Consequence: an agent in T can't prove successor's T-reasoning sound, blocking goal-preserving self-modification in the proof-based setting.
Vingean reflection
An agent cannot fully simulate a more capable successor; reasoning about the successor must abstract over its concrete computations.
Tiling agent
An agent that can construct successors implementing the same decision procedure while preserving goals; tiling = goal-preservation closure under self-modification.
Ontological crisis
The agent's utility function is defined over an ontology that turns out to be wrong/coarse; how to transport the utility to a new ontology is underdetermined.
Reflective oracle
An oracle that can answer probabilistic queries about its own outputs without self-reference paradoxes (Fallenstein et al. 2015), enabling self-trust in mixed-strategy settings.
Subsystem alignment
The problem that an agent's internal optimization subprocesses may have objectives misaligned with the agent's overall objective (mesa-optimization, inner alignment).
Goodhart's law (agency-foundations reading)
"A proxy ceases to be a good measure once optimized for." In agent foundations: a theory of agency built on concepts that change meaning under optimization pressure (e.g., "human approval" that drifts as the agent learns to elicit it) cannot ground stable alignment claims. The desideratum for foundational concepts is invariance under the optimization the agent is performing.
Reflective stability
A safety property is reflectively stable if it is preserved when the agent self-modifies (or trains a successor) toward its own goals. Goal-preservation under tiling, corrigibility-under-self-improvement, and value-handshake-after-recursive-self-improvement are reflective-stability questions. Properties that are not reflectively stable provide weaker alignment guarantees because a sufficiently capable agent will route around them during self-modification.

Retrieval practice

Q: Why doesn't "just add more axioms" solve the Löbian obstacle?
A: Any sufficiently strong consistent theory T (containing PA) is subject to Löb. Adding axioms either keeps you in this regime (still subject), makes T inconsistent (worse), or destroys recursive enumerability (loses computability). The hierarchy Tk​ approach works but loses uniform self-trust — each level trusts down, not at the same level.
Q: Give a concrete ontological-crisis example.
A: Agent values "diamonds" defined as carbon crystals with specific tetrahedral bonding. It then learns that "diamonds" in its training distribution were CGI renderings, not physical objects, or that at finer scale "carbon atom" itself decomposes into electron-cloud configurations. Does the utility transport to (a) any visually-diamond-shaped pixel pattern, (b) carbon configurations in some default ontology, (c) something quark-level? The utility function as stated doesn't decide.
Q: How does logical induction relate to the Löbian obstacle?
A: It sidesteps it. Löb is about provability (□); logical inductors assign probabilities that approach 1 for theorems but never are 1. The reflection principle "I trust my future beliefs" holds in expectation in a logical inductor without contradiction, because trust is graded.
Q: Why is subsystem alignment in this cluster and not in B (Alignment) proper?
A: The empirical mesa-optimization framing (Hubinger et al.) is in B; here we study the structural reason it's hard — that an embedded agent is partly an aggregate of subprocesses with their own implicit objectives, not a single utility function. The agent-foundations framing tries to define what it would even mean for the subsystems to be "aligned" with the whole.
Q: Strongest empirical critique of the MIRI agenda?
A: After ~10 years and significant funding, no technique developed in agent foundations is currently used in any frontier-model alignment pipeline (RLHF, RLAIF, constitutional AI, debate, weak-to-strong, interpretability). Logical induction is mathematically real but not engineering-load-bearing. The agenda made a bet that pre-paradigmatic math would precede effective engineering; empirically, ML-style empirical alignment has been the dominant fruitful track.
Q: Why is "smaller than the environment" load-bearing?
A: A Cartesian agent's hypothesis space contains the true environment as a candidate; Bayesian convergence guarantees apply. An embedded agent's hypothesis space cannot contain itself (cardinality / diagonalization); hence convergence guarantees fail for self-relevant predictions, and the agent must use approximate / hierarchical self-models with no clean optimality story.

Problems

30 min · derivationProblem 1. Prove Löb's theorem from the diagonal lemma and Gödel's second incompleteness theorem (sketch).
Solution sketch. Suppose T⊢□φ→φ. By the diagonal lemma, construct ψ such that T⊢ψ↔(□ψ→φ). Then T⊢ψ→(□ψ→φ), and applying □ (provability is closed under modus ponens internally): T⊢□ψ→□(□ψ→φ). Use the derivability conditions: □(□ψ→φ)→(□□ψ→□φ), and □ψ→□□ψ. Chain to get T⊢□ψ→□φ, then by hypothesis T⊢□ψ→φ, so T⊢ψ (since ψ↔(□ψ→φ) and we have the right-hand side after the substitution), then T⊢□ψ, then T⊢φ.
30 min · derivationProblem 2. Design a (toy) tiling agent for a deterministic gridworld where the agent can build a copy of itself before each move. Specify what "tiling" means operationally and show how it avoids needing to prove self-soundness.
Solution sketch. "Tiling": at each step, the agent runs algorithm A producing action a, then constructs a successor running the same algorithm A for the next step. Avoid Löb by: (a) probabilistic trust — successor's expected utility is high under the current agent's posterior, no proof of soundness needed; or (b) hard-coding the same procedure rather than reasoning about it — the agent doesn't verify the successor's reasoning, it just instantiates the same code. The latter is "model-polymorphism": delegate via copy, not via proof. Discuss: this works for identical-environment-distribution successors but fails as soon as the environment shifts and the algorithm needs to be revised.

Going deeper (optional)

If you read one thing: Demski & Garrabrant, Embedded Agency (full text) — the canonical articulation of the four obstacles; readable, complete, no fluff.

Optional depth: Yudkowsky & Herreshoff, Tiling Agents for Self-Modifying AI — Löbian obstacle primary source; Garrabrant et al., Logical Induction — the constructive workaround for one obstacle; de Blanc, Ontological Crises in AI Value Systems — the utility-transport problem; LessWrong agent-foundations index (2026) — current map; Embedded Universal Predictive Intelligence (2025) — recent attempt to extend UAI toward embeddedness; MIRI 2021 strategy update — read for the agenda's own honest self-assessment.

Solo assignment

Pick two of the four Demski-Garrabrant obstacles. For each, write a 600-word section that: (i) states the obstacle precisely; (ii) gives one concrete failure mode it predicts in a near-future agentic LLM system (e.g., a long-running code-writing agent that retrains itself, an agent that builds and trusts a tool); (iii) names the partial-progress mathematical artifact (logical induction, reflective oracles, FDT, …) and honestly assesses whether it would help the concrete case. End with a calibrated paragraph on whether you think this obstacle is on the critical path to AGI alignment.

D.4 World Models Model-based RL, MuZero, Dreamer latent dynamics, Othello/Chess-GPT board state, long-horizon planning.
Prerequisites: D.1, C.x (linear probing, representation analysis), POMDPs / belief states at the textbook level, basic VAE / latent variable models.

Explicit world models (MuZero) learn dynamics, reward, value, and policy jointly under outcome-relevant losses (no observation reconstruction); the simulation lemma bounds value error by O(Rmax​ϵ/(1−γ)2) for model error ϵ, explaining why long-horizon planning is sensitive to small dynamics-model errors. Implicit world models in LLMs are evidenced by Othello-GPT and Chess-GPT: linear probes recover internal board state from residual streams, and causal interventions (patching the probed direction) change downstream behavior — distinguishing "uses the representation" from "merely encodes it." Whether LLMs build genuine world models in MuZero's sense, or just statistical regularities that look like one under probes, is the central interpretive question; it reappears in C.4 as the natural-abstractions debate.

Pre-reading questions

  1. Why does MuZero not learn to predict observations, only rewards/values/policy?
  2. What does it mean for a Dreamer-style latent dynamics model to be "consistent," and why is consistency not the same as predictive accuracy?
  3. Othello-GPT contains a recoverable internal board representation. What does "recoverable" mean technically — and what's the standard refutation that it's evidence of a "world model"?
  4. For long-horizon planning, what does compounding model error do to rollouts, and how do model-based methods mitigate it?
  5. Sim-to-real for agents: what specifically transfers and what doesn't?

Core exposition

Model-based RL

Model-free RL learns Q or π directly from environment samples. Model-based RL learns (or is given) a dynamics model P^(s′∣s,a) and reward model r^(s,a), and uses them for planning (rolling out simulated trajectories) or for synthetic data generation (Dyna-style). Sample efficiency: model-based methods can be dramatically more sample-efficient — MuZero on Atari hits SOTA with ~200M frames where model-free DQN needed ~10× more — because each real sample updates both the value and the model, and the model can be queried for arbitrarily many imagined samples. Cost: model bias. A wrong model produces consistently misleading rollouts; the agent learns to exploit model errors rather than the environment.

MuZero

MuZero (Schrittwieser et al., 2020) learns a latent dynamics model jointly with value, policy, and reward predictors, and does not reconstruct observations. The model is a triple (h,g,f): h(o≤t​)=s0​ embeds the observation history; g(sk​,ak​)=(sk+1​,r^k+1​) is the latent dynamics; f(sk​)=(π^k​,v^k​) predicts policy and value. Planning is MCTS in latent space. Training uses K-step unrolls and minimizes the joint loss

L(θ)=k=0∑K​[lr(rt+k​,r^t+kθ​)+lv(zt+k​,v^t+kθ​)+lπ(πt+k​,π^t+kθ​)]+c∥θ∥2,

where zt+k​ is the n-step bootstrapped target value and πt+k​ is the MCTS-improved policy at step t+k. The crucial design choice: no observation reconstruction. The latent state sk​ is shaped only by being useful for predicting reward, value, and policy. This makes the representation task-relevant and ignores irrelevant pixels (a single bit of "score went up" matters more than a full screen of background), which is exactly why MuZero generalizes from Atari to Go without reconstruction supervision.

Dreamer-style latent dynamics

Origins. Modern latent-dynamics world models trace to Ha & Schmidhuber 2018 ("World Models"): a VAE encoder + MDN-RNN dynamics model + small linear controller, trained on raw pixel rollouts. The controller is trained entirely in imagination (rollouts inside the learned model) and transfers back to the real environment. This is the template Dreamer scaled and refined. The "consistency loss makes the world model usable for imagination" insight is already in Ha & Schmidhuber; later work added scale, better latents, and discrete categorical states.

Dreamer (Hafner et al., 2019, 2020, 2023) learns a latent world model that does reconstruct observations: a Recurrent State-Space Model with deterministic state ht​ and stochastic state zt​, with prior p(zt​∣ht​) and posterior q(zt​∣ht​,ot​). The training objective is the standard ELBO summed over the trajectory:

LDreamer​(θ)=t∑​[reconstruction−logpθ​(ot​∣ht​,zt​)​​−rewardlogpθ​(rt​∣ht​,zt​)​​+consistency / prior matchingDKL​(qθ​(zt​∣ht​,ot​)∥pθ​(zt​∣ht​))​​].

The KL term — the "consistency" piece — forces the prior p(zt​∣ht​), which does not see the observation, to match the posterior q(zt​∣ht​,ot​), which does. This is what makes the world model usable for imagination: at planning time you don't have future observations, so you must roll forward through the prior alone. Then policy is trained in imagination: roll out (h,z) in the learned model, use value-based actor-critic on the imagined returns. Dreamer V3 (Nature 2025) shows this one architecture solves Atari, DMC, Minecraft diamond, etc., with fixed hyperparameters.

Explicit vs implicit world models. MuZero and Dreamer are explicit world models: there is a designated module P^(s′∣s,a) (and reward, value, policy heads) that you train against simulation losses and query at planning time via MCTS or imagined rollouts. Othello-GPT and Chess-GPT are implicit world models: there is no module labeled "dynamics," but a linear probe on the residual stream recovers internal state (board position) that is causally used downstream. The two are different research targets — explicit world models give you a planning loop and interpretable state; implicit ones force you to ask "did the model build a model" as a representational-analysis question. Don't conflate them when discussing "world models in LLMs": LLMs are implicit by construction (no MuZero-style dynamics head), and the evidence for or against is probe-and-intervention, not loss curves.

World models in LLMs: Othello-GPT and Chess-GPT

Othello-GPT (Li et al., 2022): train a GPT-2-style transformer (8 layers, ~25M params) on sequences of Othello moves (no board states, just move tokens). After training, run a nonlinear probe on the residual stream and find that the current board state — which cell is black, white, empty — is recoverable with high accuracy at intermediate layers. Nanda (2023) sharpened this: with a different coordinate system ("mine" vs "opponent" rather than "black" vs "white"), a linear probe suffices. Causal interventions (writing the probe-direction into the residual to flip a cell's color) actually change the model's next-move predictions in the consistent direction — so it's not just decoding, the representation is functionally used. Chess-GPT (Karvonen, 2024): same setup, played at ~1300 Elo, with linear probes recovering piece positions and even "skill level" of the implicit player being modeled.

A successful linear probe is not, by itself, evidence the representation is used. A probe can recover information that the model encodes but never consults — e.g., a probe might recover "the prompt was written in the morning" from residual-stream features that are spuriously correlated with morning-time tokens but never enter any downstream computation. The licensing condition for "the model uses representation X" is a causal intervention: edit the probe direction in the residual stream and re-run; if downstream behavior shifts in the predicted direction (and noise-direction edits do not), the representation is used. Othello-GPT and Chess-GPT meet this bar; many "X is linearly decodable from layer Y" claims in the broader LLM-probing literature do not, and conclusions about world models drawn from probe accuracy alone are overclaims. Always demand: probe accuracy + causal intervention + control direction.

Honest controversy: does this mean LLMs have "world models"? The strong reading (e.g., Nanda's framing) is yes — there is a structured internal representation of latent state that is causally used. The deflationary reading: any sufficient statistic for next-move prediction is a "model" in this thin sense, and induction over sequences is enough to recover one. The interesting question isn't "is there a world model" (trivially yes for any sufficient predictor) but "how compositional, how robust under distribution shift, how aligned with the symbolic/causal structure humans use." Chess-GPT plays legal moves >99% of the time but struggles with unusual positions; Othello-GPT's representation degrades sharply off the training distribution. The lesson for frontier LLMs: yes, there are latent representations of things like sentiment, truthfulness (Burns et al.), spatial location (Gurnee & Tegmark), board state in games — but "having a world model of X" should be read as "has X-correlated features that are causally used for X-related tasks," not as "has a coherent simulation of X."

Do Othello/Chess-GPT results generalize to frontier LLMs? Partially. The methodology — linear probes + causal interventions — transfers cleanly: Burns et al. (2022) recover "truthfulness" directions; Gurnee & Tegmark (2023) recover space-and-time coordinates from Llama-2's residual stream; Marks et al. (2024) build SAEs that decompose features and verify causal use. What does not automatically transfer is the cleanness: Othello has a closed 64-cell state space and a small generative process; LLMs have unbounded, ill-defined "state spaces." Probes on LLMs find narrow, task-correlated features, often along multiple partly-redundant directions; the assumption of a single, recoverable, compositional internal world is much harder to defend than for a game-playing transformer trained on a fully-observed grammar. Treat Othello/Chess-GPT as existence proofs that transformers can build internal models of structured worlds, not as a tight prediction about what LLMs do for arbitrary domains.

Long-horizon planning

Model error compounds: if one-step error is ϵ, after k steps the distribution shift is O(kϵ) in TV under mild smoothness, and the value error in a discounted MDP is bounded by something like

∥VP^π​−VPπ​∥∞​≤O((1−γ)2Rmax​ϵ​)

(simulation-lemma-type bounds; Janner et al. 2019). Mitigations: short rollouts (Dyna, MBPO); branched rollouts initialized from real states (avoid compounding in the early state-distribution); uncertainty estimates over the model (PETS / ensemble) and pessimistic planning under model uncertainty; world models that explicitly track epistemic uncertainty (Bayesian or ensemble-based) and refuse to plan deeply in high-uncertainty regions. MCTS-with-learned-model (MuZero, AlphaZero descendants) handles long horizons by using value bootstraps at leaf nodes — the planning depth is short, but the value function carries long-horizon information.

Alternative architectures: JEPA, Genie, transducers

Dreamer's pixel-reconstruction loss is one design choice; alternatives drop it.
